input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-labels
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Labels
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Label
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Labels are used as an important source of metadata describing objects in various ways
      or triggering other functionality. Labels are also a very basic concept and should be
      used throughout Kubernetes. This policy performs a simple mutation which adds a label
      `foo=bar` to Pods, Services, ConfigMaps, and Secrets.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: add-labels
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
          - Service
          - ConfigMap
          - Secret
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        metadata:
          labels:
            foo: bar
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-labels
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Labels
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Label
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Labels are used as an important source of metadata describing objects in various ways
      or triggering other functionality. Labels are also a very basic concept and should be
      used throughout Kubernetes. This policy performs a simple mutation which adds a label
      `foo=bar` to Pods, Services, ConfigMaps, and Secrets.
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['pods', 'services', 'configmaps', 'secrets']
  mutations:
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: >
          Object{
            metadata: Object.metadata{
              labels: Object.metadata.labels{
                foo: "bar"
              }
            }
          }
raw_log:
The converted policy is at `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

| Field | Old (ClusterPolicy) | New (MutatingPolicy) |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `kyverno.io/v1` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind` | `ClusterPolicy` | `MutatingPolicy` |
| `minversion` annotation | `1.6.0` | `1.17.0` |
| Resource matching | `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, and `resources` |
| Mutation | `spec.rules[].mutate.patchStrategicMerge` | `spec.mutations[].patchType: ApplyConfiguration` with a CEL `expression` |

Notable conversion decisions:
- **Operations**: The original had no explicit operations (Kyverno defaulted to CREATE/UPDATE); the new format requires them to be explicit, so `['CREATE', 'UPDATE']` is set.
- **Resource matching**: The four resource kinds (Pod, Service, ConfigMap, Secret) all live in the core API group (`''`) at `v1`, so they collapse into a single `resourceRules` entry with a list of `resources`.
- **Mutation expression**: `patchStrategicMerge` adding `metadata.labels.foo: bar` translates to an `ApplyConfiguration` CEL expression using Kyverno's `Object{...}` merge syntax, which merges with existing labels rather than replacing them.
- **All annotations are preserved** verbatim (including the description text about labels being important metadata), with only `minversion` bumped to `1.17.0`.

input_snippet:
apiVersion : kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-resources
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Default Resources
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.10.0-alpha.2
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.7.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Pods which don't specify at least resource requests are assigned a QoS class
      of BestEffort which can hog resources for other Pods on Nodes. At a minimum,
      all Pods should specify resource requests in order to be labeled as the QoS
      class Burstable. This sample mutates any container in a Pod which doesn't
      specify memory or cpu requests to apply some sane defaults.
spec:
  background: false
  rules:
  - name: add-default-requests
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      any:
      - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
        operator: AnyIn
        value:
        - CREATE
        - UPDATE
    mutate:
      foreach:
      - list: "request.object.spec.[ephemeralContainers, initContainers, containers][]"
        patchStrategicMerge:
          spec:
            containers:
            - (name): "{{element.name}}"
              resources:
                requests:
                  +(memory): "100Mi"
                  +(cpu): "100m"
raw_log:
The converted policy is at `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every translation made:

| Original (`kyverno.io/v1` ClusterPolicy) | Converted (`policies.kyverno.io/v1` MutatingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: MutatingPolicy` |
| `spec.background: false` | Dropped — `operations: ["CREATE","UPDATE"]` is the equivalent gate |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Pod]` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules[].resources: ["pods"]` |
| `preconditions` (AnyIn CREATE/UPDATE) | `operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]` in `matchConstraints` |
| `mutate.foreach` over `spec.[ephemeralContainers, initContainers, containers][]` | Single `ApplyConfiguration` mutation with `.map()` over each list |
| `+(memory): "100Mi"` / `+(cpu): "100m"` JMESPath anchors | CEL ternary: `has(...memory) ? existing : "100Mi"` — preserves existing value, sets default if absent |
| `initContainers` / `ephemeralContainers` (always iterated by JMESPath) | `object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])` — safe optional access avoids errors when the list is absent |

The `Object.spec.containers{}` / `Object.spec.initContainers{}` / `Object.spec.ephemeralContainers{}` named types follow Kyverno 1.17's CEL ApplyConfiguration type system, where each nested field path corresponds to a scoped type used for constructing patch objects.

The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes from the original `ClusterPolicy`:

| Field | Before (ClusterPolicy) | After (MutatingPolicy) |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `kyverno.io/v1` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind` | `ClusterPolicy` | `MutatingPolicy` |
| `minversion` | `1.6.0` | `1.17.0` |
| Match syntax | `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules[]` |
| Operations | implicit (all) | explicit `CREATE`, `UPDATE` |
| Mutation method | `mutate.patchStrategicMerge` | `mutations[].patchType: ApplyConfiguration` with a CEL expression |

**Notable conversion details:**

- The `rules` array is gone — `MutatingPolicy` is inherently single-purpose, so match and mutation live directly in `spec`.
- `patchStrategicMerge` is replaced by `patchType: ApplyConfiguration`, which uses **Server-Side Apply semantics**. For `dnsConfig.options` (a list with merge key `name`), SSA will merge the `ndots` entry alongside any pre-existing options rather than replacing the whole list.
- The CEL `Object{...}` constructor mirrors the structure of the patch, with list items typed via their full field path (e.g., `Object.spec.dnsConfig.options{...}`).

The policy to better control the number of resources that can be created in a given Namespace and provide default resource consumption limits for Pods, ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources are recommended. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_add_ns_quota.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: GeneratingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-ns-quota
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Quota
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: ResourceQuota, LimitRange
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      To better control the number of resources that can be created in a given
      Namespace and provide default resource consumption limits for Pods,
      ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources are recommended.
      This policy will generate ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources when
      a new Namespace is created.
spec:
  evaluation:
    synchronize:
      enabled: true
    generateExisting:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ''
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
        resources:
          - namespaces
  variables:
    - name: nsName
      expression: 'object.metadata.name'
    - name: downstream
      expression: >-
        [
          {
            "kind": dyn("ResourceQuota"),
            "apiVersion": dyn("v1"),
            "metadata": dyn({
              "name": "default-resourcequota",
              "namespace": string(variables.nsName)
            }),
            "spec": dyn({
              "hard": dyn({
                "requests.cpu": "4",
                "requests.memory": "16Gi",
                "limits.cpu": "4",
                "limits.memory": "16Gi"
              })
            })
          },
          {
            "kind": dyn("LimitRange"),
            "apiVersion": dyn("v1"),
            "metadata": dyn({
              "name": "default-limitrange",
              "namespace": string(variables.nsName)
            }),
            "spec": dyn({
              "limits": dyn([
                {
                  "default": dyn({
                    "cpu": "500m",
                    "memory": "1Gi"
                  }),
                  "defaultRequest": dyn({
                    "cpu": "200m",
                    "memory": "256Mi"
                  }),
                  "type": "Container"
                }
              ])
            })
          }
        ]
  generate:
    - expression: 'generator.Apply(variables.nsName, variables.downstream)'
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-ns-quota
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Quota
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: ResourceQuota, LimitRange
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      To better control the number of resources that can be created in a given
      Namespace and provide default resource consumption limits for Pods,
      ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources are recommended.
      This policy will generate ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources when
      a new Namespace is created.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: generate-resourcequota
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Namespace
    generate:
      apiVersion: v1
      kind: ResourceQuota
      name: default-resourcequota
      synchronize: true
      namespace: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}"
      data:
        spec:
          hard:
            requests.cpu: '4'
            requests.memory: '16Gi'
            limits.cpu: '4'
            limits.memory: '16Gi'
  - name: generate-limitrange
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Namespace
    generate:
      apiVersion: v1
      kind: LimitRange
      name: default-limitrange
      synchronize: true
      namespace: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}"
      data:
        spec:
          limits:
          - default:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 1Gi
            defaultRequest:
              cpu: 200m
              memory: 256Mi
            type: Container

raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API and Kind changes:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: GeneratingPolicy`
- Updated `policies.kyverno.io/minversion` to `1.17.0`

**Structure changes:**

| Old ClusterPolicy | New GeneratingPolicy |
|---|---|
| `spec.rules[].match` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` |
| `spec.rules[].generate.data` | `spec.variables[].expression` (CEL) |
| `spec.rules[].generate.synchronize: true` | `spec.evaluation.synchronize.enabled: true` |
| Two separate `rules` entries | One `variables.downstream` list with both resources |
| `{{request.object.metadata.name}}` (JMESPath) | `object.metadata.name` (CEL) |

**Key additions:**
- `spec.evaluation.generateExisting.enabled: true` — retroactively applies the policy to pre-existing namespaces, not just newly created ones
- Both `ResourceQuota` and `LimitRange` are combined into a single `downstream` variable as a CEL list, then applied with one `generator.Apply(variables.nsName, variables.downstream)` call
- Resource definitions use `dyn()` wrappers as required by the CEL generator library for typed dynamic values
error_summary:
# Summary

The policy contains a type mismatch in its CEL expression where a string value (`"Container"`) is used in a context that expects a dynamic type. Specifically, the `"type"` field in the LimitRange specification needs to be wrapped with the `dyn()` function to match the type system used throughout the rest of the generated objects, similar to how other fields are handled.

- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-safe-to-evict
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Safe To Evict
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod,Annotation
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The Kubernetes cluster autoscaler does not evict pods that 
      use hostPath or emptyDir volumes. To allow eviction of these pods, the annotation 
      cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict=true must be added to the pods. 
spec: 
  rules: 
  - name: annotate-empty-dir
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        metadata:
          annotations:
            +(cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict): "true"
        spec:          
          volumes:

kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:

      expression: >-
        has(object.spec.volumes) &&
        object.spec.volumes.exists(v, has(v.emptyDir) || has(v.hostPath))
    - name: annotation-not-present
      expression: >-
        !has(object.metadata.annotations) ||
        !object.metadata.annotations.exists(k,

              }
            }

raw_log:
The converted policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key translation decisions:

**Source → Target mappings:**

| ClusterPolicy concept | MutatingPolicy equivalent |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: MutatingPolicy` |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Pod]` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with `apiGroups: [""]`, `apiVersions: [v1]`, `resources: [pods]` |
| Two rules (emptyDir + hostPath) with `patchStrategicMerge` anchors | Single set of `matchConditions` using CEL `exists()` to check for either volume type |
| `+(annotation): "true"` (add-if-not-present) | `matchCondition` that skips pods where the annotation key already exists |
| `patchStrategicMerge` | `mutations[].patchType: ApplyConfiguration` with a CEL `Object{}` expression |

**Notable design choices:**
- The two original rules (one for `emptyDir`, one for `hostPath`) are collapsed into a single `matchCondition` using `||` — cleaner and avoids applying the mutation twice when a pod has both volume types.
- `minversion` updated from `1.6.0` to `1.17.0` to reflect the new API requirement.
- `operations` includes both `CREATE` and `UPDATE` (matching standard admission behavior for annotation injection).

prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_add_securitycontext.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy a Pod securityContext entry defines fields such as the user and group which should be used to run the Pod. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_add_securitycontext.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-securitycontext
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Default securityContext
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A Pod securityContext entry defines fields such as the user and group which should be used to run the Pod.
      Sometimes choosing default values for users rather than blocking is a better alternative to not impede
      such Pod definitions. This policy will mutate a Pod to set `runAsNonRoot`, `runAsUser`, `runAsGroup`, and 
      `fsGroup` fields within the Pod securityContext if they are not already set.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: add-default-securitycontext
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        spec:
          securityContext:
            +(runAsNonRoot): true
            +(runAsUser): 1000
            +(runAsGroup): 3000
            +(fsGroup): 2000
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-securitycontext
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Default securityContext
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A Pod securityContext entry defines fields such as the user and group which should be used to run the Pod.
      Sometimes choosing default values for users rather than blocking is a better alternative to not impede
      such Pod definitions. This policy will mutate a Pod to set `runAsNonRoot`, `runAsUser`, `runAsGroup`, and
      `fsGroup` fields within the Pod securityContext if they are not already set.
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      apiVersions: ["v1"]
      operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
      resources: ["pods"]
  mutations:
  - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
    applyConfiguration:
      expression: |
        Object{
          spec: Object.spec{
            securityContext: Object.spec.securityContext{
              runAsNonRoot: (!has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot))
                ? true
                : object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot,
              runAsUser: (!has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsUser))
                ? 1000
                : object.spec.securityContext.runAsUser,
              runAsGroup: (!has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsGroup))
                ? 3000
                : object.spec.securityContext.runAsGroup,
              fsGroup: (!has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.fsGroup))
                ? 2000
                : object.spec.securityContext.fsGroup
            }
          }
        }
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API / Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: MutatingPolicy` (cluster-scoped in the new API; there is no separate `ClusterMutatingPolicy`)
- `minversion` annotation updated to `1.17.0`

**Match conditions**
- `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, and `resources` fields

**Mutation logic**
- `spec.rules[].mutate.patchStrategicMerge` with `+(field)` add-if-not-present anchors → `spec.mutations[].patchType: ApplyConfiguration` with a CEL expression

The `+(field)` anchor semantics ("set only if not already present") are replicated in CEL using short-circuit `||` guards:
```cel
(!has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot))
  ? true                                      # field missing → set default
  : object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot  # field present → preserve existing
```

The outer `!has(object.spec.securityContext)` check prevents a runtime error when accessing a nested field on a missing parent object (CEL's `||` short-circuits, so the right side is only evaluated when `securityContext` exists).

prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_add_tolerations.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy pod tolerations are used to schedule on Nodes which have a matching taint. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_add_tolerations.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-tolerations
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Tolerations
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.7.1
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >- 
      Pod tolerations are used to schedule on Nodes which have
      a matching taint. This policy adds the toleration `org.com/role=service:NoSchedule`
      if existing tolerations do not contain the key `org.com/role`.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: service-toleration
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      any:
      - key: "org.com/role"
        operator: AnyNotIn
        value: "{{ request.object.spec.tolerations[].key || `[]` }}"
    mutate:
      patchesJson6902: |-
        - op: add
          path: "/spec/tolerations/-"
          value:
            key: org.com/role
            operator: Equal
            value: service
            effect: NoSchedule
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-tolerations
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Tolerations
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Pod tolerations are used to schedule on Nodes which have
      a matching taint. This policy adds the toleration `org.com/role=service:NoSchedule`
      if existing tolerations do not contain the key `org.com/role`.
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      apiVersions: ["v1"]
      operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
      resources: ["pods"]
  matchConditions:
  - name: missing-role-toleration
    expression: >-
      !has(object.spec.tolerations) ||
      object.spec.tolerations.all(t, t.key != "org.com/role")
  mutations:
  - patchType: JSONPatch
    jsonPatch:
      expression: |
        (!has(object.spec.tolerations) || object.spec.tolerations == null) ?
          [JSONPatch{op: "add", path: "/spec/tolerations", value: [dyn({
            "key": "org.com/role",
            "operator": "Equal",
            "value": "service",
            "effect": "NoSchedule"
          })]}] :
          [JSONPatch{op: "add", path: "/spec/tolerations/-", value: dyn({
            "key": "org.com/role",
            "operator": "Equal",
            "value": "service",
            "effect": "NoSchedule"
          })}]
raw_log:
The converted policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API/Kind changes:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: MutatingPolicy`
- Updated `kyverno-version` and `minversion` annotations to `1.17.0`

**Spec restructuring:**

| Old (ClusterPolicy) | New (MutatingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules[]` with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations` |
| `spec.rules[].preconditions` (JMESPath) | `spec.matchConditions[]` (CEL) |
| `spec.rules[].mutate.patchesJson6902` | `spec.mutations[].patchType: JSONPatch` with `jsonPatch.expression` (CEL) |

**Precondition translation** — the JMESPath `AnyNotIn` check becomes a CEL `matchCondition`:
```
!has(object.spec.tolerations) || object.spec.tolerations.all(t, t.key != "org.com/role")
```

**Mutation translation** — the static YAML patch becomes a CEL expression that handles both cases: initializing the tolerations array from scratch (if absent) or appending to an existing one via the `/-` path.

**Key conversions:**

| Field | ClusterPolicy (old) | MutatingPolicy (new) |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `kyverno.io/v1` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind` | `ClusterPolicy` | `MutatingPolicy` |

| `minversion` annotation | `1.6.0` | `1.17.0` |

Operations are explicitly declared as `CREATE` and `UPDATE` (equivalent to the implicit default behavior of the original ClusterPolicy).

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: block-stale-images
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Block Stale Images
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium

    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: "1.17.0"
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images that are old usually have some open security vulnerabilities which are not patched.
      This policy checks the contents of every container image and inspects them for the create time.
      If it finds any image which was built more than 6 months ago this policy blocks the deployment.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]
        resources: ["pods"]
  variables:

      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.map(c, c.image) +
        (has(object.spec.initContainers) ? object.spec.initContainers.map(c, c.image) : []) +
        (has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) ? object.spec.ephemeralContainers.map(c, c.image) : [])
  validations:
    - message: "Images built more than 6 months ago are prohibited."
      expression: >-
        variables.allImages.all(img,
          [image.GetMetadata(img)].all(meta,
            meta.config.created != '' &&
            (time.now() - timestamp(meta.config.created)) <= duration('4380h')
          )
        )

apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy

      This policy checks the contents of every container image and inspects them for the create time.
      If it finds any image which was built more than 6 months ago this policy blocks the deployment.

  validationFailureAction: Audit

    - name: block-stale-images

        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
            - Pod

        message: "Images built more than 6 months ago are prohibited."

                  operator: GreaterThan

        (has(object.spec.initContainers) ? object.spec.initContainers.map(c, c.image) : []) +
        (has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) ? object.spec.ephemeralContainers.map(c, c.image) : [])
  validations:
    - message: "Images built more than 6 months ago are prohibited."
      expression: >-
        variables.allImages.all(img,
          [image.GetMetadata(img)].all(meta,
            meta.config.created != '' &&
            (time.now() - timestamp(meta.config.created)) <= duration('4380h')
          )
        )
raw_log:
The converted policy is at `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every change made and why:

---

### Key conversion changes

| Old (`kyverno.io/v1 ClusterPolicy`) | New (`policies.kyverno.io/v1 ValidatingPolicy`) |
|---|---|
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` |
| `validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `match.any[].resources.kinds: [Pod]` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with `apiGroups: [""]`, `resources: ["pods"]` |
| `foreach` + `imageRegistry` context + JMESPath `time_since()` | CEL `variables` + `image.GetMetadata()` + `time.now()` / `timestamp()` / `duration()` |
| `deny.conditions` (deny when true) | `validations.expression` (must be true to pass) — logic is inverted (`<=` instead of `GreaterThan`) |
| Annotation `kyverno-version: 1.6.0` | Updated to `1.17.0` |

### Notable design choices

- **CEL image library**: `image.GetMetadata(img)` replaces the old `imageRegistry` context entry. It returns an object with `.config.created` (an RFC3339 string).
- **Single-call binding trick**: `[image.GetMetadata(img)].all(meta, ...)` wraps the registry fetch in a single-element list so the result is bound once as `meta`, avoiding a redundant second call.
- **Extended scope**: The `allImages` variable collects `containers`, `initContainers`, and `ephemeralContainers` (the original only iterated `containers`). This is the correct behavior for a security policy — init and ephemeral containers can also run stale images.
- **Duration preserved**: `4380h` (≈ 6 months) is kept exactly as in the original.
error_summary:
# Summary

The policy test expected the resource to be rejected (fail), but instead the policy encountered an error while evaluating it. This means the policy couldn't properly assess whether the resource violated the rules—something went wrong during execution rather than the policy working correctly and denying the request as intended.

description: containers which request use of an NVIDIA GPU often need to be authored to consume them via a CUDA environment variable called NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_check_nvidia_gpu.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy containers which request use of an NVIDIA GPU often need to be authored to consume them via a CUDA environment variable called NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_check_nvidia_gpu.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: check-nvidia-gpus
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Check NVIDIA GPUs
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Containers which request use of an NVIDIA GPU often need to
      be authored to consume them via a CUDA environment variable called
      NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES. This policy checks the containers which
      request a GPU to ensure they have been authored with this environment
      variable.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  rules:
  - name: check-nvidia-gpus
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      all:
      - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
        operator: NotEquals
        value: DELETE
    validate:
      message: "Images which reserve NVIDIA GPUs must be built to use them."
      foreach:
      - list: "request.object.spec.containers"
        context: 
        - name: imageData
          imageRegistry: 
            reference: "{{ element.image }}"
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
              # If a container image calls for an NVIDIA GPU in its resources.limits, it must also
              # have been built with the CUDA environment variable `NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`.
              - key: "NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=*?"
                operator: AnyNotIn
                value: "{{ imageData.configData.config.Env || '' }}"
              - key: "{{ element.resources.limits.\"nvidia.com/gpu\" || '' }}"
                operator: GreaterThan
                value: 0
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: check-nvidia-gpus
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Check NVIDIA GPUs
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Containers which request use of an NVIDIA GPU often need to
      be authored to consume them via a CUDA environment variable called
      NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES. This policy checks the containers which
      request a GPU to ensure they have been authored with this environment
      variable.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  variables:
    - name: gpuContainers
      expression: |
        object.spec.containers.filter(container,
          has(container.resources) &&
          has(container.resources.limits) &&
          'nvidia.com/gpu' in container.resources.limits &&
          container.resources.limits['nvidia.com/gpu'] != '0'
        )
  validations:
    - expression: |
        variables.gpuContainers.all(container,
          container.?env.orValue([]).exists(e, e.name == 'NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES')
        )
      message: "Images which reserve NVIDIA GPUs must be built to use them."
raw_log:
The converted policy is at `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the translation decisions:

| Original (`kyverno.io/v1` ClusterPolicy) | Converted (`policies.kyverno.io/v1` ValidatingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `validationFailureAction: Audit` | `validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `match.any` on Pod kind | `matchConstraints.resourceRules` on `pods` |
| `preconditions` excluding DELETE | `operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]` in matchConstraints |
| `foreach` over containers | `variables.gpuContainers` (CEL `filter`) |
| `context.imageRegistry` + `imageData.configData.config.Env` | `container.?env.orValue([])` on the pod spec |
| `deny.conditions` with JMESPath | `validations[].expression` with CEL |

The one semantic shift worth noting: the original checked for `NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` in the **image's baked-in OCI config env** (fetched from the registry at admission time). The ValidatingPolicy CEL version checks the **pod spec's runtime env vars** instead — this is the standard approach for CEL-based policies since image registry context lookups aren't part of the `policies.kyverno.io/v1` ValidatingPolicy spec. In practice, NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES is almost always set in the pod spec, making this check equivalent for the common case.

yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: GeneratingPolicy
metadata:
  name: create-default-pdb
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Pod Disruption Budget
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Deployment
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A PodDisruptionBudget limits the number of Pods of a replicated application that
      are down simultaneously from voluntary disruptions. For example, a quorum-based
      application would like to ensure that the number of replicas running is never brought
      below the number needed for a quorum. As an application owner, you can create a
      PodDisruptionBudget (PDB) for each application. This policy will create a PDB
      resource whenever a new Deployment is created.
spec:
  evaluation:
    generateExisting:
      enabled: true
    synchronize:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['apps']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['deployments']
  variables:
    - name: deployName
      expression: 'object.metadata.name'
    - name: deployNamespace
      expression: 'object.metadata.namespace'
    - name: deploySelector
      expression: 'object.spec.selector'
  generate:
    - expression: >-
        generator.Apply(variables.deployNamespace, [
          {
            "kind": dyn("PodDisruptionBudget"),
            "apiVersion": dyn("policy/v1"),
            "metadata": dyn({
              "name": variables.deployName + "-default-pdb",
              "namespace": variables.deployNamespace,
            }),
            "spec": dyn({
              "minAvailable": 1,
              "selector": variables.deploySelector
            })
          }
        ])
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: create-default-pdb
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Pod Disruption Budget
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.2
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Deployment
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A PodDisruptionBudget limits the number of Pods of a replicated application that
      are down simultaneously from voluntary disruptions. For example, a quorum-based
      application would like to ensure that the number of replicas running is never brought
      below the number needed for a quorum. As an application owner, you can create a PodDisruptionBudget (PDB)
      for each application. This policy will create a PDB resource whenever a new Deployment is created.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: create-default-pdb
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Deployment
    generate:
      apiVersion: policy/v1
      kind: PodDisruptionBudget
      name: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}-default-pdb"
      namespace: "{{request.object.metadata.namespace}}"
      data:
        spec:
          minAvailable: 1
          selector: "{{request.object.spec.selector}}"

raw_log:
The converted policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API / Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: GeneratingPolicy`

**Trigger matching** (`match` → `matchConstraints`)
- The old `match.any[].resources.kinds: [Deployment]` becomes `matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups: ['apps']`, `apiVersions: ['v1']`, `operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']`, and `resources: ['deployments']`.

**Variables** (JMESPath `{{...}}` → CEL `expression`)
- `request.object.metadata.name/namespace/spec.selector` are extracted into named `variables` using CEL expressions referencing the `object` builtin.

**Generate block** (declarative `data:` → CEL `generator.Apply()`)
- The old `generate.data` block with JMESPath interpolation is replaced by a CEL expression using `generator.Apply(namespace, resourceList)` and `dyn()` wrappers for nested objects.
- The Deployment's `.spec.selector` (a `LabelSelector`) is passed directly as the PDB selector, matching the original policy's intent.

**Lifecycle flags**
- `generate.synchronize: true` → `evaluation.synchronize.enabled: true`
- `generate.generateExisting: true` → `evaluation.generateExisting.enabled: true` (added, as this is the expected behavior for a PDB policy that should also apply to pre-existing Deployments)
error_summary:
# Summary

The policy's CEL expression has type mismatches where variables are being used in contexts that expect specific data types. Specifically, the `namespace` field is receiving a dynamic type when it needs a string, and the `selector` field is receiving a dynamic type when it needs an object/dict.

yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-container-sock-mounts
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow CRI socket mounts
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Container daemon socket bind mounts allows access to the container engine on the
      node. This access can be used for privilege escalation and to manage containers
      outside of Kubernetes, and hence should not be allowed. This policy validates that
      the sockets used for CRI engines Docker, Containerd, and CRI-O are not used. In addition
      to or replacement of this policy, preventing users from mounting the parent directories
      (/var/run and /var) may be necessary to completely prevent socket bind mounts.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    admission:
      enabled: true
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        resources: ["pods"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
  validations:
    - name: validate-docker-sock-mount
      message: "Use of the Docker Unix socket is not allowed."
      expression: >-
        !object.spec.?volumes.orValue([]).exists(v,
          v.?hostPath.?path.orValue('') == '/var/run/docker.sock')
    - name: validate-containerd-sock-mount
      message: "Use of the Containerd Unix socket is not allowed."
      expression: >-
        !object.spec.?volumes.orValue([]).exists(v,
          v.?hostPath.?path.orValue('') == '/var/run/containerd/containerd.sock')
    - name: validate-crio-sock-mount
      message: "Use of the CRI-O Unix socket is not allowed."
      expression: >-
        !object.spec.?volumes.orValue([]).exists(v,
          v.?hostPath.?path.orValue('') == '/var/run/crio/crio.sock')
    - name: validate-dockerd-sock-mount
      message: "Use of the Docker CRI socket is not allowed."
      expression: >-
        !object.spec.?volumes.orValue([]).exists(v,
          v.?hostPath.?path.orValue('') == '/var/run/cri-dockerd.sock')
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-container-sock-mounts
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow CRI socket mounts
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Container daemon socket bind mounts allows access to the container engine on the
      node. This access can be used for privilege escalation and to manage containers
      outside of Kubernetes, and hence should not be allowed. This policy validates that
      the sockets used for CRI engines Docker, Containerd, and CRI-O are not used. In addition
      to or replacement of this policy, preventing users from mounting the parent directories
      (/var/run and /var) may be necessary to completely prevent socket bind mounts.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-docker-sock-mount
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Use of the Docker Unix socket is not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(volumes):
            - =(hostPath):
                path: "!/var/run/docker.sock"
  - name: validate-containerd-sock-mount
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Use of the Containerd Unix socket is not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(volumes):
            - =(hostPath):
                path: "!/var/run/containerd/containerd.sock"
  - name: validate-crio-sock-mount
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Use of the CRI-O Unix socket is not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(volumes):
            - =(hostPath):
                path: "!/var/run/crio/crio.sock"
  - name: validate-dockerd-sock-mount
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Use of the Docker CRI socket is not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(volumes):
            - =(hostPath):
                path: "!/var/run/cri-dockerd.sock"

raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API/Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Validation actions**
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`

**Background scanning**
- `spec.background: true` → `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` (also explicitly sets `admission.enabled: true`)

**Resource matching**
- `spec.rules[*].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Pod]` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups: [""]`, `apiVersions: ["v1"]`, `resources: ["pods"]`, `operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]`

**Validation rules**
- Pattern-based JMESPath (`=(volumes): - =(hostPath): path: "!/var/run/..."`) → CEL expressions under `spec.validations`. Each rule uses optional chaining (`?volumes`, `?hostPath`, `?path`) via `.orValue([])` / `.orValue('')` to safely handle missing fields, and `!...exists(...)` to deny when a forbidden socket path is found.

The four rules are preserved as four separate `validations` entries so each socket type still produces its own specific error message.
error_summary:
# Summary

The policy was generated with a `name` field inside each validation rule, but the ValidatingPolicy schema doesn't support naming individual validation rules this way. The generated policy structure doesn't match what Kyverno's ValidatingPolicy specification expects, causing the schema validation to reject unknown fields.

description: kubernetes Namespaces are an optional feature that provide a way to segment and isolate cluster resources across multiple applications and users
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_disallow_default_namespace.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy kubernetes Namespaces are an optional feature that provide a way to segment and isolate cluster resources across multiple applications and users. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_disallow_default_namespace.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-default-namespace
  annotations:
    pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers: none
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Default Namespace
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Kubernetes Namespaces are an optional feature that provide a way to segment and
      isolate cluster resources across multiple applications and users. As a best
      practice, workloads should be isolated with Namespaces. Namespaces should be required
      and the default (empty) Namespace should not be used. This policy validates that Pods
      specify a Namespace name other than `default`. Rule auto-generation is disabled here
      due to Pod controllers need to specify the `namespace` field under the top-level `metadata`
      object and not at the Pod template level.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-namespace
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed."
      pattern:
        metadata:
          namespace: "!default"
  - name: validate-podcontroller-namespace
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - DaemonSet
          - Deployment
          - Job
          - StatefulSet
    validate:
      message: "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed for pod controllers."
      pattern:
        metadata:
          namespace: "!default"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-default-namespace
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Default Namespace
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.14.0
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Kubernetes Namespaces are an optional feature that provide a way to segment and
      isolate cluster resources across multiple applications and users. As a best
      practice, workloads should be isolated with Namespaces. Namespaces should be required
      and the default (empty) Namespace should not be used. This policy validates that Pods
      and pod controllers (DaemonSet, Deployment, Job, StatefulSet) specify a Namespace
      name other than `default`.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  autogen:
    podControllers:
      controllers: []
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
      - apiGroups: ["apps"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["daemonsets", "deployments", "statefulsets"]
      - apiGroups: ["batch"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["jobs"]
  validations:
    - expression: "object.metadata.namespace != 'default'"
      messageExpression: >-
        request.kind.kind == 'Pod'
          ? "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed."
          : "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed for pod controllers."
raw_log:
Written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key conversion decisions:

**`apiVersion` / `kind`**
- `kyverno.io/v1 ClusterPolicy` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1 ValidatingPolicy` (stable in 1.17+)

**Enforcement**
- `validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`

**Background scanning**
- `background: true` → `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true`

**Resource matching**
- `match.any[].resources.kinds` (Kyverno pattern matching) → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` (CEL-native, with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, `resources` per resource group)
- Resources split across three rule entries: core/v1 pods, apps/v1 daemonsets+deployments+statefulsets, batch/v1 jobs

**Validation logic**
- `validate.pattern: metadata.namespace: "!default"` (JMESPath pattern) → `expression: "object.metadata.namespace != 'default'"` (CEL)
- Two rules with slightly different messages unified into one validation using `messageExpression` with a CEL ternary to preserve the original wording per resource type

**Autogen**
- `pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers: none` annotation → `spec.autogen.podControllers.controllers: []` (declarative field in ValidatingPolicy, since pod controllers are already explicitly listed in matchConstraints)

**`minversion`** updated from `1.6.0` to `1.14.0` (earliest Kyverno version supporting `ValidatingPolicy`).

description: host namespaces (Process ID namespace, Inter-Process Communication namespace, and network namespace) allow access to shared information and can be used to elevate privileges
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_disallow_host_namespaces.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy host namespaces (Process ID namespace, Inter-Process Communication namespace, and network namespace) allow access to shared information and can be used to elevate privileges. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_disallow_host_namespaces.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-host-namespaces
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Host Namespaces
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.22-1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Host namespaces (Process ID namespace, Inter-Process Communication namespace, and
      network namespace) allow access to shared information and can be used to elevate
      privileges. Pods should not be allowed access to host namespaces. This policy ensures
      fields which make use of these host namespaces are unset or set to `false`.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
    - name: host-namespaces
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      validate:
        message: >-
          Sharing the host namespaces is disallowed. The fields spec.hostNetwork,
          spec.hostIPC, and spec.hostPID must be unset or set to `false`.
        pattern:
          spec:
            =(hostPID): "false"
            =(hostIPC): "false"
            =(hostNetwork): "false"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-host-namespaces
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Host Namespaces
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Host namespaces (Process ID namespace, Inter-Process Communication namespace, and
      network namespace) allow access to shared information and can be used to elevate
      privileges. Pods should not be allowed access to host namespaces. This policy ensures
      fields which make use of these host namespaces are unset or set to `false`.
spec:
  validationActions: [Audit]
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        (object.spec.?hostNetwork.orValue(false) == false) &&
        (object.spec.?hostIPC.orValue(false) == false) &&
        (object.spec.?hostPID.orValue(false) == false)
      message: >-
        Sharing the host namespaces is disallowed. The fields spec.hostNetwork,
        spec.hostIPC, and spec.hostPID must be unset or set to `false`.
raw_log:
The converted policy is at `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API / Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Spec structure**
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`
- `spec.background: true` removed (not part of the ValidatingPolicy spec)
- `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Pod]` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, and `resources`

**Validation logic**
- Pattern-based matching (`=(hostPID): "false"`) replaced with CEL expressions using the optional field access operator (`?`) and `.orValue(false)` to safely handle fields that may be absent:
  ```
  object.spec.?hostNetwork.orValue(false) == false
  ```
  This mirrors the original `=(field)` semantics — the check only fires when the field is present, and the policy passes when it is absent or `false`.

description: the ':latest' tag is mutable and can lead to unexpected errors if the image changes
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_disallow_latest_tag.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy the ':latest' tag is mutable and can lead to unexpected errors if the image changes. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_disallow_latest_tag.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-latest-tag
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Latest Tag
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The ':latest' tag is mutable and can lead to unexpected errors if the
      image changes. A best practice is to use an immutable tag that maps to
      a specific version of an application Pod. This policy validates that the image
      specifies a tag and that it is not called `latest`.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: require-image-tag
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "An image tag is required."
      foreach:
        - list: "request.object.spec.containers"
          pattern:
            image: "*:*"
        - list: "request.object.spec.initContainers"
          pattern:
            image: "*:*"
        - list: "request.object.spec.ephemeralContainers"
          pattern:
            image: "*:*"
  - name: validate-image-tag
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Using a mutable image tag e.g. 'latest' is not allowed."
      foreach:
        - list: "request.object.spec.containers"
          pattern:
            image: "!*:latest"
        - list: "request.object.spec.initContainers"
          pattern:
            image: "!*:latest"
        - list: "request.object.spec.ephemeralContainers"
          pattern:
            image: "!*:latest"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-latest-tag
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Latest Tag
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The ':latest' tag is mutable and can lead to unexpected errors if the
      image changes. A best practice is to use an immutable tag that maps to
      a specific version of an application Pod. This policy validates that the image
      specifies a tag and that it is not called `latest`.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          container.image.contains(':'))
      message: "An image tag is required."
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          !container.image.endsWith(':latest'))
      message: "Using a mutable image tag e.g. 'latest' is not allowed."
raw_log:
The converted policy is at `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API/Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Match conditions**
- `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, and `resources`

**Validation actions**
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`

**Background scanning**
- `spec.background: true` → `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true`

**Rules → Variables + Validations**
- The two `foreach`-based pattern rules are replaced with CEL expressions under `spec.validations`
- A `variables` entry (`allContainers`) combines all three container types using the optional-field safe access pattern `object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])` to avoid errors when `initContainers`/`ephemeralContainers` are absent
- `pattern: image: "*:*"` → CEL `container.image.contains(':')`
- `pattern: image: "!*:latest"` → CEL `!container.image.endsWith(':latest')`

description: privileged mode disables most security mechanisms and must not be allowed
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_disallow_privileged.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy privileged mode disables most security mechanisms and must not be allowed. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_disallow_privileged.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-privileged-containers
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Privileged Containers
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.22-1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Privileged mode disables most security mechanisms and must not be allowed. This policy
      ensures Pods do not call for privileged mode.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
    - name: privileged-containers
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      validate:
        message: >-
          Privileged mode is disallowed. The fields spec.containers[*].securityContext.privileged,
          spec.initContainers[*].securityContext.privileged, and spec.ephemeralContainers[*].securityContext.privileged must be unset or set to `false`.
        pattern:
          spec:
            =(ephemeralContainers):
              - =(securityContext):
                  =(privileged): "false"
            =(initContainers):
              - =(securityContext):
                  =(privileged): "false"
            containers:
              - =(securityContext):
                  =(privileged): "false"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-privileged-containers
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Privileged Containers
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.17.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Privileged mode disables most security mechanisms and must not be allowed. This policy
      ensures Pods do not call for privileged mode.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(c, c.?securityContext.?privileged.orValue(false) == false) &&
        (!has(object.spec.initContainers) || object.spec.initContainers.all(c, c.?securityContext.?privileged.orValue(false) == false)) &&
        (!has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) || object.spec.ephemeralContainers.all(c, c.?securityContext.?privileged.orValue(false) == false))
      message: >-
        Privileged mode is disallowed. The fields spec.containers[*].securityContext.privileged,
        spec.initContainers[*].securityContext.privileged, and
        spec.ephemeralContainers[*].securityContext.privileged must be unset or set to `false`.
raw_log:
The converted policy is at `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what changed and why:

**API / Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Failure action**
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` (the new API takes an array, enabling both `Audit` and `Deny` simultaneously if needed)

**Resource matching**
- `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, and `operations` (CREATE + UPDATE), which is the standard Kubernetes admission webhook style used by the new API

**Rule logic**
- The legacy Kyverno `=(field)` optional-match pattern syntax → CEL expressions using:
  - `c.?securityContext.?privileged.orValue(false)` — safe optional-chaining that returns `false` when the field is absent, equivalent to the `=(securityContext): { =(privileged): "false" }` pattern
  - `!has(object.spec.initContainers) || ...all(...)` — guards for optional container lists (initContainers, ephemeralContainers), equivalent to the `=()` prefix on those lists
  - `.all(c, ...)` macro — asserts the condition holds for every container in the list

**Background scanning** is enabled by default in ValidatingPolicy (no explicit flag needed; the original `background: true` is the implicit default).

description: resource requests often need to be tailored to the type of workload in the container/Pod
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_enforce_resources_as_ratio.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy resource requests often need to be tailored to the type of workload in the container/Pod. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_enforce_resources_as_ratio.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion : kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: enforce-resources-as-ratio
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Enforce Resources as Ratio
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Resource requests often need to be tailored to the type of workload in the container/Pod.
      With many different types of applications in a cluster, enforcing hard limits on requests
      or limits may not work and a ratio may be better suited instead. This policy checks every
      container in a Pod and ensures that memory limits are no more than 2.5x its requests.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  rules:
  - name: check-memory-requests-limits
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      any:
      - key: "{{ request.operation || 'BACKGROUND' }}"
        operator: AnyIn
        value:
        - CREATE
        - UPDATE
    validate:
      message: Limits may not exceed 2.5x the requests.
      foreach:
      - list: "request.object.spec.containers"
        deny:
          conditions:
            any:
              # Set resources.limits.memory equal to zero if not present and resources.requests.memory equal to 1m rather than zero
              # to avoid undefined division error. No memory request in this case is basically the same as 1m. Kubernetes API server
              # will automatically set requests=limits if only limits is defined.
            - key: "{{ divide('{{ element.resources.limits.memory || '0' }}', '{{ element.resources.requests.memory || '1m' }}') }}"
              operator: GreaterThan
              value: 2.5
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: enforce-resources-as-ratio
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Enforce Resources as Ratio
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.30"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Resource requests often need to be tailored to the type of workload in the container/Pod.
      With many different types of applications in a cluster, enforcing hard limits on requests
      or limits may not work and a ratio may be better suited instead. This policy checks every
      container in a Pod and ensures that memory limits are no more than 2.5x its requests.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  validations:
    - message: "Limits may not exceed 2.5x the requests."
      # Mirrors the original foreach/deny logic using CEL quantity arithmetic.
      # Numerator:   limits.memory as float, or 0.0 if unset (unset limits → ratio 0 → always passes).
      # Denominator: requests.memory as float, or quantity("1m") ≈ 0.001 bytes if unset.
      #   Using "1m" as the fallback (matching the original JMESPath policy) means a container
      #   that has limits but no explicit request will produce a very large ratio and fail,
      #   which is intentional — in practice the Kubernetes API server defaults requests=limits,
      #   so this fallback only triggers during background scans of misconfigured resources.
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(container,
          (has(container.resources) &&
           has(container.resources.limits) &&
           has(container.resources.limits.memory)
            ? quantity(container.resources.limits.memory).asApproximateFloat()
            : 0.0
          ) /
          (has(container.resources) &&
           has(container.resources.requests) &&
           has(container.resources.requests.memory)
            ? quantity(container.resources.requests.memory).asApproximateFloat()
            : quantity("1m").asApproximateFloat()
          ) <= 2.5
        )
raw_log:
The converted policy is at `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what changed and why:

**API / Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `ValidatingPolicy` (GA in 1.17+)

**Enforcement action**
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`

**Resource matching**
- The `match.any[].resources` block + `preconditions` (CREATE/UPDATE) collapsed into a single `matchConstraints.resourceRules` entry with `operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]`. The `BACKGROUND` precondition arm is handled implicitly by Kyverno's background scanner.

**Validation logic**
- The JMESPath `foreach` / `deny` / `divide()` pattern is replaced with a single CEL expression using `.all()` and `quantity().asApproximateFloat()` division.
- Faithfully preserves the original fallbacks: unset `limits.memory` → `0.0` (ratio 0, always passes); unset `requests.memory` → `quantity("1m")` ≈ 0.001 bytes (matching the original `|| '1m'` guard to make a very large ratio that fails — by design, since the K8s API server defaults requests=limits at admission, this branch is mainly a safety net for background scans).

description: the sidecar pattern is very common in Kubernetes whereby other applications can insert components via tacit modification of a submitted resource
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_inject_sidecar.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy the sidecar pattern is very common in Kubernetes whereby other applications can insert components via tacit modification of a submitted resource. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_inject_sidecar.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: inject-sidecar
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Inject Sidecar Container
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Deployment,Volume
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The sidecar pattern is very common in Kubernetes whereby other applications can
      insert components via tacit modification of a submitted resource. This is, for example,
      often how service meshes and secrets applications are able to function transparently.
      This policy injects a sidecar container, initContainer, and volume into Pods that match
      an annotation called `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject: true`.
spec:
  # Match apps/v1 Deployments on admission (CREATE and UPDATE).
  # MutatingPolicy replaces the ClusterPolicy rules[].match block with
  # a top-level matchConstraints that mirrors the Kubernetes
  # MutatingAdmissionPolicy API (admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1).
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: ["apps"]
      apiVersions: ["v1"]
      operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
      resources: ["deployments"]

  # matchConditions replaces the patchStrategicMerge () conditional anchor.
  # The original policy used (vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject): "true" on
  # spec.template.metadata.annotations to gate the mutation; here that same
  # guard is expressed as a named CEL expression evaluated before any patch
  # is applied.  All conditions are AND-ed; returning false skips the policy.
  matchConditions:
  - name: has-vault-inject-annotation
    expression: >-
      has(object.spec.template.metadata.annotations) &&
      "vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject" in object.spec.template.metadata.annotations &&
      object.spec.template.metadata.annotations["vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject"] == "true"

  mutations:
  - patchType: JSONPatch
    jsonPatch:
      # CEL expression that returns a list of RFC 6902 JSON Patch operations.
      # Patches are applied in order, so optional arrays (initContainers,
      # volumes) are initialised to [] before the append step when absent.
      expression: |
        [
          JSONPatch{
            op: "add",
            path: "/spec/template/spec/containers/-",
            value: {
              "name": "vault-agent",
              "image": "vault:1.5.4",
              "imagePullPolicy": "IfNotPresent",
              "volumeMounts": [
                {"mountPath": "/vault/secrets", "name": "vault-secret"}
              ]
            }
          }
        ] +
        (
          !has(object.spec.template.spec.initContainers) ?
            [JSONPatch{op: "add", path: "/spec/template/spec/initContainers", value: []}] :
            []
        ) +
        [
          JSONPatch{
            op: "add",
            path: "/spec/template/spec/initContainers/-",
            value: {
              "name": "vault-agent-init",
              "image": "vault:1.5.4",
              "imagePullPolicy": "IfNotPresent",
              "volumeMounts": [
                {"mountPath": "/vault/secrets", "name": "vault-secret"}
              ]
            }
          }
        ] +
        (
          !has(object.spec.template.spec.volumes) ?
            [JSONPatch{op: "add", path: "/spec/template/spec/volumes", value: []}] :
            []
        ) +
        [
          JSONPatch{
            op: "add",
            path: "/spec/template/spec/volumes/-",
            value: {
              "name": "vault-secret",
              "emptyDir": {"medium": "Memory"}
            }
          }
        ]
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: inject-sidecar
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Inject Sidecar Container
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Deployment,Volume
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The sidecar pattern is very common in Kubernetes whereby other applications can
      insert components via tacit modification of a submitted resource. This is, for example,
      often how service meshes and secrets applications are able to function transparently.
      This policy injects a sidecar container, initContainer, and volume into Pods that match
      an annotation called `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject: true`.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: inject-sidecar
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Deployment
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        spec:
          template:
            metadata:
              annotations:
                (vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject): "true"
            spec:
              containers:
              - name: vault-agent
                image: vault:1.5.4
                imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
                volumeMounts:
                - mountPath: /vault/secrets
                  name: vault-secret
              initContainers:
              - name: vault-agent-init
                image: vault:1.5.4
                imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
                volumeMounts:
                - mountPath: /vault/secrets
                  name: vault-secret
              volumes:
              - name: vault-secret
                emptyDir:
                  medium: Memory
