raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every change made and why:

**API/Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Validation action**
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` — the field was renamed and is now a list to allow combining `Audit`, `Deny`, and `Warn` independently.

**Resource matching + operations**
- `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Pod]` + `preconditions` (operation AnyIn CREATE/UPDATE) → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with `operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]`. Operations are now first-class in `matchConstraints`, so the JMESPath precondition is no longer needed.

**Background scanning**
- `spec.background: true` → `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true`

**Autogeneration**
- The old annotation `pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers: DaemonSet,Deployment,StatefulSet` is replaced by `spec.autogen: true`, which is the native mechanism in `ValidatingPolicy` for auto-generating matching rules against pod-controller types.

**Validation logic**
- The JMESPath `foreach`/`deny` pattern (checking `element.keys(@)[]` for each probe) is replaced by a single CEL expression using `object.spec.containers.all(container, has(container.livenessProbe) || has(container.readinessProbe) || has(container.startupProbe))`. CEL's `has()` macro is the idiomatic way to check for optional field presence, and `.all()` replaces the `foreach` + deny-all-conditions pattern.
error_summary:
# Summary

The policy file contains a schema error where `spec.autogen` is set to a boolean value (true/false), but Kyverno expects it to be an object with specific configuration properties instead. Because the policy itself fails to load due to this structural mistake, it cannot be applied to any of the test resources, causing all six test cases to fail with "Not found" errors.

description: as application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources requested and consumed by each Pod
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_resource_limits.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy as application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources requested and consumed by each Pod. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_require_resource_limits.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-requests-limits
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Limits and Requests
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      As application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources
      requested and consumed by each Pod. It is recommended to require resource requests and
      limits per Pod, especially for memory and CPU. If a Namespace level request or limit is specified,
      defaults will automatically be applied to each Pod based on the LimitRange configuration.
      This policy validates that all containers have something specified for memory and CPU
      requests and memory limits.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-resources
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "CPU and memory resource requests and memory limits are required for containers."
      pattern:
        spec:
          containers:
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
          =(initContainers):
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
          =(ephemeralContainers):
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-requests-limits
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Limits and Requests
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: "1.17.0"
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      As application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources
      requested and consumed by each Pod. It is recommended to require resource requests and
      limits per Pod, especially for memory and CPU. If a Namespace level request or limit is specified,
      defaults will automatically be applied to each Pod based on the LimitRange configuration.
      This policy validates that all containers have something specified for memory and CPU
      requests and memory limits.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  evaluation:
    admission:
      enabled: true
    background:
      enabled: true
  validations:
    - message: "CPU and memory resource requests and memory limits are required for all containers."
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(c,
          has(c.resources) &&
          has(c.resources.requests) &&
          'memory' in c.resources.requests &&
          'cpu' in c.resources.requests &&
          has(c.resources.limits) &&
          'memory' in c.resources.limits
        )
    - message: "CPU and memory resource requests and memory limits are required for all init containers."
      expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.initContainers) ||
        object.spec.initContainers.all(c,
          has(c.resources) &&
          has(c.resources.requests) &&
          'memory' in c.resources.requests &&
          'cpu' in c.resources.requests &&
          has(c.resources.limits) &&
          'memory' in c.resources.limits
        )
    - message: "CPU and memory resource requests and memory limits are required for all ephemeral containers."
      expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) ||
        object.spec.ephemeralContainers.all(c,
          has(c.resources) &&
          has(c.resources.requests) &&
          'memory' in c.resources.requests &&
          'cpu' in c.resources.requests &&
          has(c.resources.limits) &&
          'memory' in c.resources.limits
        )
raw_log:
The converted policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made during conversion:

| Original (`ClusterPolicy`) | Converted (`ValidatingPolicy`) |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` |
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `spec.background: true` | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` (with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`) |
| Pattern-based `validate.pattern` | CEL `validations[].expression` |
| `=(initContainers)` (optional match) | `!has(object.spec.initContainers) \|\|` guard in CEL |
| `=(ephemeralContainers)` (optional match) | `!has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) \|\|` guard in CEL |

The three CEL validations map directly to the three container types (containers, initContainers, ephemeralContainers). Each uses:
- `has(c.resources)` and `has(c.resources.requests/limits)` to guard against missing fields
- `'memory' in c.resources.requests` / `'cpu' in c.resources.requests` to check map keys are present (equivalent to the original `"?*"` pattern)

description: a read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy; the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_ro_rootfs.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy a read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy; the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_require_ro_rootfs.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-ro-rootfs
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Read-Only Root Filesystem
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices, PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy;
      the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state.
      An immutable root filesystem can also prevent malicious binaries from writing to the
      host system. This policy validates that containers define a securityContext
      with `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-readOnlyRootFilesystem
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Root filesystem must be read-only."
      pattern:
        spec:
          containers:
          - securityContext:
              readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-ro-rootfs
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Read-Only Root Filesystem
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices, PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy;
      the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state.
      An immutable root filesystem can also prevent malicious binaries from writing to the
      host system. This policy validates that containers define a securityContext
      with `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  validations:
    - message: "Root filesystem must be read-only."
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(c,
          c.?securityContext.?readOnlyRootFilesystem.orValue(false) == true
        )
raw_log:
The converted policy is at `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what changed and why:

**`apiVersion` / `kind`**
- `kyverno.io/v1` / `ClusterPolicy` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `ValidatingPolicy`

**`spec.validationFailureAction: Audit`**
- Replaced by `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` — the new API uses a list to allow combining `Audit`, `Deny`, and `Warn` in a single policy.

**`spec.background: true`**
- Replaced by `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` — the explicit evaluation block in the new API.

**`spec.rules[].match`**
- Replaced by `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` using the Kubernetes-native `ValidatingAdmissionPolicy` format, scoping to core API group (`""`), `v1`, and `CREATE`/`UPDATE` operations on `pods`.

**`spec.rules[].validate.pattern`**
- The pattern-matching rule is replaced by a CEL expression in `spec.validations[].expression`. The expression uses optional field access (`?securityContext.?readOnlyRootFilesystem`) with `.orValue(false)` to safely handle containers that omit the field, then checks all containers via `.all()`.

**`minversion` annotation**
- Updated from `1.6.0` to `1.17.0` since `ValidatingPolicy` with `policies.kyverno.io/v1` is a 1.17+ construct.

description: adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels of ability than those with which they may be provisioned
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_restrict_capabilities.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels of ability than those with which they may be provisioned. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_restrict_capabilities.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: psp-restrict-adding-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Adding Capabilities
    policies.kyverno.io/category: PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels
      of ability than those with which they may be provisioned. Many capabilities
      allow system-level control and should be prevented. Pod Security Policies (PSP)
      allowed a list of "good" capabilities to be added. This policy checks
      ephemeralContainers, initContainers, and containers to ensure the only
      capabilities that can be added are either NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
    - name: allowed-capabilities
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{ request.operation || 'BACKGROUND' }}"
          operator: NotEquals
          value: DELETE
      validate:
        message: >-
          Any capabilities added other than NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN are disallowed.
        foreach:
          - list: request.object.spec.[ephemeralContainers, initContainers, containers][]
            deny:
              conditions:
                all:
                - key: "{{ element.securityContext.capabilities.add[] || '' }}"
                  operator: AnyNotIn
                  value:
                  - NET_BIND_SERVICE
                  - CAP_CHOWN
                  - ''
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: psp-restrict-adding-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Adding Capabilities
    policies.kyverno.io/category: PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.30+"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels
      of ability than those with which they may be provisioned. Many capabilities
      allow system-level control and should be prevented. Pod Security Policies (PSP)
      allowed a list of "good" capabilities to be added. This policy checks
      ephemeralContainers, initContainers, and containers to ensure the only
      capabilities that can be added are either NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  variables:
    - name: allowedCapabilities
      expression: "['NET_BIND_SERVICE', 'CAP_CHOWN']"
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers +
        object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([]) +
        object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          container.?securityContext.?capabilities.?add.orValue([]).all(
            capability, capability == '' || capability in variables.allowedCapabilities
          )
        )
      message: >-
        Any capabilities added other than NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN are disallowed.
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made during conversion:

| Aspect | Old `ClusterPolicy` | New `ValidatingPolicy` |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `kyverno.io/v1` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind` | `ClusterPolicy` | `ValidatingPolicy` |
| Failure action | `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| Background scan | `spec.background: true` | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` |
| Resource matching | `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules[]` |
| DELETE precondition | `preconditions` with JMESPath filter | Implicit — `operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]` excludes DELETE |
| Container iteration | `validate.foreach` with JMESPath `request.object.spec.[…][]` | CEL variable `allContainers` using list concatenation |
| Validation logic | JMESPath `AnyNotIn` operator | CEL `all()` with `capability in variables.allowedCapabilities` |
| Allowed capabilities | Inline in condition | `variables.allowedCapabilities` CEL expression |

The CEL expression mirrors the original `foreach` deny logic exactly: it checks all three container types (regular, init, ephemeral) and asserts every added capability is either an empty string or one of `NET_BIND_SERVICE` / `CAP_CHOWN`.

description: images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_restrict_image_registries.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_restrict_image_registries.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-image-registries
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Image Registries
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be
      scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk. Requiring use of known, approved
      registries helps reduce threat exposure by ensuring image pulls only come from them. This
      policy validates that container images only originate from the registry `eu.foo.io` or
      `bar.io`. Use of this policy requires customization to define your allowable registries.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-registries
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Unknown image registry."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(ephemeralContainers):
          - image: "eu.foo.io/* | bar.io/*"
          =(initContainers):
          - image: "eu.foo.io/* | bar.io/*"
          containers:
          - image: "eu.foo.io/* | bar.io/*"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-image-registries
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Image Registries
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be
      scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk. Requiring use of known, approved
      registries helps reduce threat exposure by ensuring image pulls only come from them. This
      policy validates that container images only originate from the registry `eu.foo.io` or
      `bar.io`. Use of this policy requires customization to define your allowable registries.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:   [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations:  ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources:   ["pods"]
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          container.image.startsWith('eu.foo.io/') ||
          container.image.startsWith('bar.io/')
        )
      message: "Unknown image registry."
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
raw_log:
The converted policy is at `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what changed and why:

**API / Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ValidatingPolicy` (GA in Kyverno 1.17)

**Enforcement**
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`

**Background scanning**
- `spec.background: true` → `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true`

**Resource matching**
- `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Pod]` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, and `resources` (the new format requires Kubernetes admission-style rules)

**Validation logic**
- The old Kyverno pattern syntax (`eu.foo.io/* | bar.io/*` with `=(initContainers)` optional fields) is replaced by a CEL expression.
- A `variables.allContainers` variable combines all three container types using optional chaining (`?initContainers.orValue([])` / `?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])`) to gracefully handle pods that omit those fields — equivalent to the `=(field)` optional-field syntax in the original.
- The CEL `all()` macro with `startsWith()` calls mirrors the allowlist logic; the expression must return `true` to pass, so the allowlist check is expressed positively (pass if image starts with an approved prefix).

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: unique-ingress-host
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Unique Ingress Host
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Ingress
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      An Ingress host is a URL at which services may be made available
      externally. In most cases, these hosts should be unique across the
      cluster to ensure no routing conflicts occur. This policy checks an
      incoming Ingress resource to ensure its hosts are unique to the
      cluster. It also ensures that only a single host may be specified
      in a given manifest.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - networking.k8s.io
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - ingresses
  variables:
    - name: allIngresses
      expression: >-
        resource.List("networking.k8s.io/v1", "ingresses", "").items
  validations:
    - message: "The Ingress host name must be unique."
      expression: >-
        variables.allIngresses.all(ing,
          (oldObject != null && ing.metadata.uid == object.metadata.uid) ||
          !ing.spec.?rules.orValue([]).exists(existingRule,
            has(existingRule.host) &&
            object.spec.?rules.orValue([]).exists(reqRule,
              has(reqRule.host) && reqRule.host == existingRule.host
            )
          )
        )
    - message: "An Ingress resource may only contain a single host entry."
      expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.rules) ||
        object.spec.rules.filter(r, has(r.host)).size() <= 1
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: unique-ingress-host
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Unique Ingress Host
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Ingress
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      An Ingress host is a URL at which services may be made available externally. In most cases,
      these hosts should be unique across the cluster to ensure no routing conflicts occur.
      This policy checks an incoming Ingress resource to ensure its hosts are unique to the cluster.
      It also ensures that only a single host may be specified in a given manifest.      
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: false
  rules:
    - name: check-single-host-create
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Ingress
      context:
        - name: hosts
          apiCall:
            urlPath: "/apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingresses"
            jmesPath: "items[].spec.rules[].host"
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
          operator: Equals
          value: CREATE
      validate:
        message: "The Ingress host name must be unique."
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
              - key: "{{ request.object.spec.rules[].host }}"
                operator: AnyIn
                value: "{{ hosts }}"
    - name: check-single-host-update
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Ingress
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
          operator: Equals
          value: UPDATE
      context:
        - name: allhosts
          apiCall:
            urlPath: "/apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingresses"
            jmesPath: "items[?metadata.uid!='{{ request.object.metadata.uid }}'].spec.rules[].host"
      validate:
        message: "The Ingress host name must be unique."
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
              - key: "{{ request.object.spec.rules[].host }}"
                operator: AnyIn
                value: "{{ allhosts }}"
    - name: deny-multiple-hosts
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Ingress
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
          operator: AnyIn
          value:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        - key: "{{ request.object.spec.rules[].host | length(@)}}"
          operator: GreaterThan
          value: 1
      validate:
        message: "An Ingress resource may only contain a single host entry."
        deny: {}

kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: unique-ingress-host
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Unique Ingress Host

    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Ingress
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      An Ingress host is a URL at which services may be made available
      externally. In most cases, these hosts should be unique across the
      cluster to ensure no routing conflicts occur. This policy checks an
      incoming Ingress resource to ensure its hosts are unique to the
      cluster. It also ensures that only a single host may be specified
      in a given manifest.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - networking.k8s.io
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - ingresses
  variables:
    - name: allIngresses
      expression: >-
        resource.List("networking.k8s.io/v1", "ingresses", "").items
  validations:
    - message: "The Ingress host name must be unique."
      expression: >-
        variables.allIngresses.all(ing,
          (oldObject != null && ing.metadata.uid == object.metadata.uid) ||
          !ing.spec.?rules.orValue([]).exists(existingRule,
            has(existingRule.host) &&
            object.spec.?rules.orValue([]).exists(reqRule,
              has(reqRule.host) && reqRule.host == existingRule.host
            )
          )
        )
    - message: "An Ingress resource may only contain a single host entry."
      expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.rules) ||
        object.spec.rules.filter(r, has(r.host)).size() <= 1
raw_log:
The converted policy is at `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what changed and why:

**API / Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `ValidatingPolicy`

**Matching**
- `spec.rules[].match` blocks → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, and `resources`
- Three separate rules with `preconditions` on CREATE/UPDATE → a single `matchConstraints` block covering both operations

**Failure action**
- `validationFailureAction: Audit` → `validationActions: [Audit]`

**Background scanning**
- `background: false` → `evaluation.background.enabled: false`

**Host uniqueness (rules 1 & 2 merged)**
The two separate CREATE/UPDATE rules with JMESPath `apiCall` context are replaced by a single CEL validation using `resource.List()` — Kyverno 1.14+'s CEL library for cluster-wide resource lookups:
```yaml
variables:
  - name: allIngresses
    expression: resource.List("networking.k8s.io/v1", "ingresses", "").items
```
The CEL expression then uses `oldObject != null && ing.metadata.uid == object.metadata.uid` to skip the ingress being updated (replicating the original `metadata.uid != '...'` JMESPath filter), covering both CREATE and UPDATE in one rule.

**Multiple hosts (rule 3)**
- JMESPath precondition + `deny: {}` → a clean CEL expression: `object.spec.rules.filter(r, has(r.host)).size() <= 1`
error_summary:
# Summary

The policy's CEL expression is trying to loop through `variables.allIngresses` using the `.all()` function, but the variable isn't being recognized as a list—it's being treated as an untyped value instead. This type mismatch prevents the CEL compiler from understanding how to iterate over the data, causing the compilation to fail.

input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-nodeport
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow NodePort
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Service
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A Kubernetes Service of type NodePort uses a host port to receive traffic from
      any source. A NetworkPolicy cannot be used to control traffic to host ports.
      Although NodePort Services can be useful, their use must be limited to Services
      with additional upstream security checks. This policy validates that any new Services
      do not use the `NodePort` type.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-nodeport
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Service
    validate:
      message: "Services of type NodePort are not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(type): "!NodePort"

| Aspect | ClusterPolicy (old) | ValidatingPolicy (new) |

yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-apt-get-force-yes
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow apt-get --force-yes in Dockerfiles
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Dockerfile Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: high
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The --force-yes option in apt-get overrides safety checks and prompts,
      allowing package installation or upgrades even when they conflict with
      other packages or require explicit user confirmation. This can lead to
      system instability or unexpected behavior. This policy prohibits the use
      of --force-yes in any apt-get command within a Dockerfile.
spec:
  evaluation:
    mode: JSON
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - "*"
        apiVersions:
          - "*"
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - "*"
  matchConditions:
    - name: is-dockerfile
      expression: "has(object.Stages)"
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  validations:
    - message: >-
        apt-get --force-yes is not permitted. It bypasses safety checks and
        conflict resolution prompts, which can cause system instability or
        unexpected package state. Use apt-get install -y instead.
      expression: |
        object.Stages.all(stage,
          stage.Commands.all(cmd,
            !(cmd.Name == "run" &&
              cmd.CmdLine.exists(line,
                line.contains("apt-get") && line.contains("--force-yes")
              )
            )
          )
        )
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-apt-get-force-yes
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow apt-get --force-yes in Dockerfiles
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Dockerfile Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: high
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The --force-yes option in apt-get overrides safety checks and prompts,
      allowing package installation or upgrades even when they conflict with
      other packages or require explicit user confirmation. This can lead to
      system instability or unexpected behavior. This policy prohibits the use
      of --force-yes in any apt-get command within a Dockerfile.
spec:
  evaluation:
    mode: JSON
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - "*"
        apiVersions:
          - "*"
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - "*"
  matchConditions:
    - name: is-dockerfile
      expression: "has(object.Stages)"
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  validations:
    - message: >-
        apt-get --force-yes is not permitted. It bypasses safety checks and
        conflict resolution prompts, which can cause system instability or
        unexpected package state. Use apt-get install -y instead.
      expression: |
        object.Stages.all(stage,
          stage.Commands.all(cmd,
            !(cmd.Name == "run" &&
              cmd.CmdLine.exists(line,
                line.contains("apt-get") && line.contains("--force-yes")
              )
            )
          )
        )
raw_log:
The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key design decisions:

**Structure:**
- `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1` — per your spec (the v1alpha1 API in Kyverno 1.17, later promoted to `v1` in 1.18)
- `evaluation.mode: JSON` — tells Kyverno to evaluate arbitrary JSON payloads rather than Kubernetes objects

**Matching:**
- `matchConstraints.resourceRules` with `apiGroups: ["*"]` and `resources: ["*"]` — applies the policy to all resource types
- `matchConditions` with `has(object.Stages)` — gates evaluation on Dockerfile-shaped payloads (those that have a `Stages` field), so non-Dockerfile JSON is skipped cleanly

**Validation CEL expression:**
```cel
object.Stages.all(stage,
  stage.Commands.all(cmd,
    !(cmd.Name == "run" &&
      cmd.CmdLine.exists(line,
        line.contains("apt-get") && line.contains("--force-yes")
      )
    )
  )
)
```
This walks every `Stage → Command`, looks for `run` instructions (lowercase, as the Moby/BuildKit parser normalizes them), and asserts that none of the shell lines contain both `apt-get` and `--force-yes`. The policy fires a `Deny` action with an explanatory message pointing users to `-y` as the safe alternative.
error_summary:
# Summary

The policy `disallow-apt-get-force-yes` is supposed to reject Dockerfiles that use `apt-get install -y` (automatic yes to prompts), but it's incorrectly allowing a bad Dockerfile that should have been blocked. The policy either isn't detecting the problematic `apt-get -y` command properly, or its validation rules aren't configured to actually enforce the rejection.

description: mutates Pods and Services to add the label 'foo: bar' to metadata.labels. The label must be added if absent and left unchanged if already present.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.17+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that mutates Pods and Services to add the label 'foo: bar' to metadata.labels. The label must be added if absent and left unchanged if already present. Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/gen_add_default_labels.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-foo-label
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        resources: ["pods", "services"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
  mutations:
    - patchType: JSONPatch
      jsonPatch:
        expression: |
          !has(object.metadata.labels) ?
            [JSONPatch{op: "add", path: "/metadata/labels", value: {"foo": "bar"}}] :
          !has(object.metadata.labels.foo) ?
            [JSONPatch{op: "add", path: "/metadata/labels/foo", value: "bar"}] :
            []
raw_log:
The policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the design decisions:

**`apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `kind: MutatingPolicy`** — the new stable API introduced in Kyverno 1.12+ and the recommended form in 1.17+.

**`matchConstraints.resourceRules`** — targets both `pods` and `services` in the core API group (`""`), on `CREATE` and `UPDATE` operations.

**`mutations[].patchType: JSONPatch`** with a CEL conditional expression — this is the correct approach to implement "add if absent, leave unchanged if present":

| Condition | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| No `metadata.labels` at all | `add` the entire labels map `{"foo":"bar"}` |
| Labels exist but no `foo` key | `add` just the `/metadata/labels/foo` key |
| `foo` key already exists (any value) | Return `[]` — no patch applied |

Using `ApplyConfiguration` (SSA-style merge) was intentionally avoided here because it would overwrite an existing `foo` label with a different value, violating the "leave unchanged if already present" requirement.

The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a breakdown of the key design decisions based on the Kyverno 1.17+ docs:

- **`apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `kind: GeneratingPolicy`** — the new CEL-native generating policy type introduced in Kyverno 1.17, replacing the older `ClusterPolicy` generate rules.
- **`matchConstraints`** — triggers on `CREATE` of `namespaces` (core API group `''`, version `v1`).
- **`variables.nsName`** — captures the new namespace's name from `object.metadata.name` via CEL.
- **`variables.defaultDenyPolicy`** — builds the NetworkPolicy manifest using `dyn()` for JSON object literals and `string(variables.nsName)` for the namespace field.
- **Generated NetworkPolicy** — empty `podSelector: {}` selects all pods; `policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]` with no `ingress` or `egress` rule arrays means all traffic is denied by default.
- **`evaluation.synchronize.enabled: true`** — keeps the generated resource in sync if the policy changes.
- **`evaluation.generateExisting.enabled: true`** — retroactively generates the NetworkPolicy in already-existing namespaces when the policy is first applied.
error_summary:
# Summary

The policy uses CEL (Common Expression Language) to generate a NetworkPolicy resource, but there's a type mismatch in how the `policyTypes` field is defined. The compiler expects all values within the dynamic object to be of type `dyn` (dynamic), but the policy provides a plain list of strings instead, which the compiler cannot accept in this context.

description: denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs whose containers, initContainers, or ephemeralContainers add any Linux capability via securityContext.capabilities.add beyond the Pod Security Standards baseline allow-list (AUDIT_WRITE, CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, FOWNER, FSETID, KILL, MKNOD, NET_BIND_SERVICE, SETFCAP, SETGID, SETPCAP, SETUID, SYS_CHROOT).
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs whose containers, initContainers, or ephemeralContainers add any Linux capability via securityContext.capabilities.add beyond the Pod Security Standards baseline allow-list (AUDIT_WRITE, CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, FOWNER, FSETID, KILL, MKNOD, NET_BIND_SERVICE, SETFCAP, SETGID, SETPCAP, SETUID, SYS_CHROOT). Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/gen_disallow_capabilities.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-capabilities-beyond-baseline
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Capabilities Beyond PSS Baseline
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod, Deployment, CronJob
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: "1.17.0"
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Adding Linux capabilities beyond those listed in the Pod Security Standards
      (PSS) baseline allow-list is disallowed. This policy covers Pods directly
      and uses autogen to extend coverage to Deployments and CronJobs. Allowed
      capabilities: AUDIT_WRITE, CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, FOWNER, FSETID, KILL,
      MKNOD, NET_BIND_SERVICE, SETFCAP, SETGID, SETPCAP, SETUID, SYS_CHROOT.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  autogen:
    podControllers:
      controllers:
        - deployments
        - cronjobs
  variables:
    - name: allowedCaps
      expression: >-
        ['AUDIT_WRITE', 'CHOWN', 'DAC_OVERRIDE', 'FOWNER', 'FSETID', 'KILL',
        'MKNOD', 'NET_BIND_SERVICE', 'SETFCAP', 'SETGID', 'SETPCAP',
        'SETUID', 'SYS_CHROOT']
  validations:
    - message: >-
        One or more containers add a Linux capability that is not in the PSS
        baseline allow-list. Remove the disallowed capability from
        securityContext.capabilities.add or restrict it to: AUDIT_WRITE,
        CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, FOWNER, FSETID, KILL, MKNOD, NET_BIND_SERVICE,
        SETFCAP, SETGID, SETPCAP, SETUID, SYS_CHROOT.
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(c,
          !has(c.securityContext) ||
          !has(c.securityContext.capabilities) ||
          !has(c.securityContext.capabilities.add) ||
          c.securityContext.capabilities.add.all(cap, cap in variables.allowedCaps)
        ) &&
        (
          !has(object.spec.initContainers) ||
          object.spec.initContainers.all(c,
            !has(c.securityContext) ||
            !has(c.securityContext.capabilities) ||
            !has(c.securityContext.capabilities.add) ||
            c.securityContext.capabilities.add.all(cap, cap in variables.allowedCaps)
          )
        ) &&
        (
          !has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) ||
          object.spec.ephemeralContainers.all(c,
            !has(c.securityContext) ||
            !has(c.securityContext.capabilities) ||
            !has(c.securityContext.capabilities.add) ||
            c.securityContext.capabilities.add.all(cap, cap in variables.allowedCaps)
          )
        )
raw_log:
The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what it does and the key design decisions:

---

## Policy: `disallow-capabilities-beyond-baseline`

**`apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`** — uses the new Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy API (not the legacy `kyverno.io/v1` ClusterPolicy).

### Structure

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `spec.validationActions: [Deny]` | Hard-deny on admission; no audit-only mode |
| `spec.matchConstraints` | Matches `pods` on `CREATE`/`UPDATE` in core API group |
| `spec.autogen.podControllers.controllers` | Kyverno auto-generates equivalent rules for `deployments` and `cronjobs`, rewriting `object.spec.containers` to the correct nested path (`spec.template.spec.*` for Deployments, `spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.*` for CronJobs) |
| `spec.variables[allowedCaps]` | CEL list of the 13 PSS baseline-permitted capabilities — defined once, referenced three times |
| `spec.validations[0].expression` | CEL expression that returns `true` (allow) only when every capability in every container is in `allowedCaps` |

### CEL validation logic (per container list)

```cel
containerList.all(c,
  !has(c.securityContext) ||
  !has(c.securityContext.capabilities) ||
  !has(c.securityContext.capabilities.add) ||
  c.securityContext.capabilities.add.all(cap, cap in variables.allowedCaps)
)
```

Passes when: the container has no `securityContext`, or no `capabilities`, or no `add` list, or every listed capability is in the allow-list. Denies as soon as any single capability outside the list is found.

All three container types (`containers`, `initContainers`, `ephemeralContainers`) are checked. `initContainers` and `ephemeralContainers` are guarded with `!has(...)` since they are optional fields.

Write a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs that set spec.hostNetwork, spec.hostPID, or spec.hostIPC to true. All three host namespace fields must be checked. Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/gen_disallow_host_namespaces.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-host-namespaces
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Host Namespaces
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Sharing the host namespaces is disallowed. The fields spec.hostNetwork,

spec:

  validations:

    - message: "spec.hostPID must not be set to true."

raw_log:
The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key design decisions:

**`apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `kind: ValidatingPolicy`** — the new Kyverno 1.17+ API (not `kyverno.io/v1` ClusterPolicy).

**`matchConstraints.resourceRules`** — three explicit entries covering all three resource types across the correct API groups:
- `""` (core) → `pods`
- `apps` → `deployments`
- `batch` → `cronjobs`

Both `CREATE` and `UPDATE` are covered so mutations to existing resources are also denied.

**`variables.podSpec`** — a single CEL variable that resolves to the right pod spec path for each resource kind:
- Pod → `object.spec`
- Deployment → `object.spec.template.spec`
- CronJob → `object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec`

This avoids duplicating the three field checks for each resource type.

**Three separate `validations`** — one per field (`hostNetwork`, `hostPID`, `hostIPC`) so violations produce distinct, actionable error messages. Each uses `has()` to guard against the field being absent (since all three are optional and default to `false`), then checks the value itself.

**`validationActions: [Deny]`** — hard enforcement (admission is rejected), not just audit logging.

description: denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs that use any hostPath volume. The check must reject any volume in spec.volumes whose hostPath field is set, regardless of the hostPath value.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs that use any hostPath volume. The check must reject any volume in spec.volumes whose hostPath field is set, regardless of the hostPath value. Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/gen_disallow_host_path.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-host-path
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow hostPath Volumes
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      HostPath volumes expose the underlying node filesystem to a container,
      which can be used to escape isolation or access sensitive host data.
      This policy denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs that declare any
      volume whose hostPath field is set, regardless of the path value.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
      - apiGroups: ["apps"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["deployments"]
      - apiGroups: ["batch"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["cronjobs"]
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        object.kind != "Pod" ||
        !has(object.spec.volumes) ||
        !object.spec.volumes.exists(v, has(v.hostPath))
      message: "hostPath volumes are not allowed in Pods"
    - expression: >-
        object.kind != "Deployment" ||
        !has(object.spec.template.spec.volumes) ||
        !object.spec.template.spec.volumes.exists(v, has(v.hostPath))
      message: "hostPath volumes are not allowed in Deployments"
    - expression: >-
        object.kind != "CronJob" ||
        !has(object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.volumes) ||
        !object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.volumes.exists(v, has(v.hostPath))
      message: "hostPath volumes are not allowed in CronJobs"
raw_log:
Policy written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a breakdown of every design decision:

---

### Structure

| Field | Value | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` | Required for Kyverno 1.17+ `ValidatingPolicy` |
| `validationActions` | `[Deny]` | Hard-blocks the request; use `[Audit]` if you want warn-only first |
| `operations` | `CREATE, UPDATE` | Catches both new resources and edits that add volumes |

### `matchConstraints` — three explicit resource rules

- **Pods** → `""` / `v1` / `pods`
- **Deployments** → `apps` / `v1` / `deployments`
- **CronJobs** → `batch` / `v1` / `cronjobs`

All three groups must be listed separately because they live in different API groups.

### `validations` — one CEL expression per resource kind

Each expression is guarded with `object.kind != "<Kind>" ||` so it short-circuits to `true` (allow) when the wrong kind is being evaluated. This avoids CEL type errors from accessing paths that don't exist on other resource structures.

| Resource | Volume path checked |
|---|---|
| Pod | `object.spec.volumes` |
| Deployment | `object.spec.template.spec.volumes` |
| CronJob | `object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.volumes` |

The core check is:
```
!has(object.spec.volumes) || !object.spec.volumes.exists(v, has(v.hostPath))
```
- `!has(...)` passes cleanly when no `volumes` field is present (nil/absent list)
- `has(v.hostPath)` detects whether the field is **set** on any volume, regardless of its value — satisfying the "reject any volume whose hostPath field is set" requirement

      - apiGroups: ["batch"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["cronjobs"]
  variables:
    - name: podSpec
      expression: >-
        object.kind == "Pod" ? object.spec :
        object.kind == "Deployment" ? object.spec.template.spec :
        object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec
  validations:
    - message: >-
        Privileged containers are not allowed. The fields
        securityContext.privileged must be unset or false across all
        containers, initContainers, and ephemeralContainers.
      messageExpression: >-
        "'" + object.kind + "/" + object.metadata.name + "' has a privileged container. " +
        "Set securityContext.privileged to false or remove the field."
      expression: >-
        variables.podSpec.containers.all(c,
          c.?securityContext.?privileged.orValue(false) == false
        ) &&
        variables.podSpec.?initContainers.orValue([]).all(c,
          c.?securityContext.?privileged.orValue(false) == false
        ) &&
        variables.podSpec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([]).all(c,
          c.?securityContext.?privileged.orValue(false) == false
        )
raw_log:
The policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a breakdown of the key design decisions:

**`matchConstraints.resourceRules`** — three explicit rules covering each resource type with its correct API group:
- Pods: `""` / `v1`
- Deployments: `apps` / `v1`
- CronJobs: `batch` / `v1`

**`variables.podSpec`** — a single CEL ternary that normalises the pod spec path for all three kinds so the validation expression doesn't need to branch:
- Pod → `object.spec`
- Deployment → `object.spec.template.spec`
- CronJob → `object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec`

**`validations.expression`** — uses `all()` across all three container lists with optional chaining (`?securityContext.?privileged.orValue(false)`) so the check is safe when either field is absent, without needing verbose `has()` guards.

**`messageExpression`** — produces a dynamic denial message that names the offending object kind and name, surfaced to the user by the admission webhook.

output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-app-kubernetes-io-name-label
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require app.kubernetes.io/name Label
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Requires all Pods to have the label 'app.kubernetes.io/name' set to a
      non-empty value. Admission is denied if the label is missing or empty.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        has(object.metadata.labels) &&
        'app.kubernetes.io/name' in object.metadata.labels &&
        object.metadata.labels['app.kubernetes.io/name'] != ''
      message: >-
        Pod must have the label 'app.kubernetes.io/name' set to a non-empty value.
raw_log:
The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what it does and why each field was chosen:

**Key fields:**

| Field | Value | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` | Required for the new Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy API |
| `kind` | `ValidatingPolicy` | Cluster-scoped; distinct from the legacy `ClusterPolicy` |
| `validationActions` | `[Deny]` | Blocks admission immediately on violation (not just audit) |
| `matchConstraints.resourceRules` | Pods, `""` apiGroup, v1, CREATE+UPDATE | Targets all Pod admissions |
| `validations[0].expression` | CEL expression (see below) | Enforces the label rule |

**CEL expression logic** — three conditions must all be true:
1. `has(object.metadata.labels)` — the labels map is present (not nil)
2. `'app.kubernetes.io/name' in object.metadata.labels` — the specific key exists
3. `object.metadata.labels['app.kubernetes.io/name'] != ''` — the value is non-empty

All three are required because CEL short-circuits left-to-right, so accessing the map key without first confirming the map exists would panic at runtime.

The policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's what it does and why each choice was made:

**`apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `kind: ValidatingPolicy`** — the GA CEL-based policy API introduced in Kyverno 1.12 and stabilized at GA in 1.17, replacing the older `kyverno.io/v1` + `ClusterPolicy` approach for admission validation.

**`autogen: true`** — Kyverno will automatically generate matching rules for pod-controller resources (Deployment, DaemonSet, StatefulSet, Job, CronJob) so the policy also covers pods created indirectly.

**`matchConstraints`** — targets core API group (`""`), `v1` Pods on `CREATE` and `UPDATE` operations.

**Three separate `validations` entries** — one per required field — so the admission denial message is specific about *which* field is missing:
1. `resources.requests.cpu`
2. `resources.requests.memory`
3. `resources.limits.memory`

**CEL `has()` guards** — each expression defensively checks `has(container.resources)` and `has(container.resources.requests/limits)` before checking the leaf field, because accessing a missing parent in CEL would panic rather than return false.

**CPU limit intentionally omitted** — per your requirement, only memory limit is enforced.

# Summary

The policy file has a configuration error: the `autogen` field is set to a boolean value (true/false) when it should be an object with specific properties. Because the policy failed to load due to this schema mismatch, Kyverno couldn't apply it to any of the test resources, causing all tests to fail with "Not found" errors.

description: only allows Pod container images that begin with the prefix 'eu.foo.io/' or 'bar.io/'. Deny any Pod whose containers use images from any other registry.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that only allows Pod container images that begin with the prefix 'eu.foo.io/' or 'bar.io/'. Deny any Pod whose containers use images from any other registry. Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/gen_restrict_registries.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-image-registries
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Image Registries
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: high
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Restricts container images to those sourced from approved registries.
      Only images beginning with 'eu.foo.io/' or 'bar.io/' are permitted.
      Applies to containers, initContainers, and ephemeralContainers.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  validations:
    - message: >-
        Container images must come from an approved registry.
        Only images prefixed with 'eu.foo.io/' or 'bar.io/' are allowed.
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(c,
          c.image.startsWith('eu.foo.io/') || c.image.startsWith('bar.io/')
        ) &&
        (
          !has(object.spec.initContainers) ||
          object.spec.initContainers.all(c,
            c.image.startsWith('eu.foo.io/') || c.image.startsWith('bar.io/')
          )
        ) &&
        (
          !has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) ||
          object.spec.ephemeralContainers.all(c,
            c.image.startsWith('eu.foo.io/') || c.image.startsWith('bar.io/')
          )
        )
raw_log:
The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what it does and why each choice was made:

**Policy design:**

- `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `kind: ValidatingPolicy` — the new Kyverno 1.17+ policy type (a superset of Kubernetes `ValidatingAdmissionPolicy`, backed by CEL)
- `validationActions: [Deny]` — hard deny on admission; any non-matching image blocks the Pod immediately
- `matchConstraints` — targets core API group (`""`), `v1` Pods on `CREATE` and `UPDATE` operations
- Single `validations` expression using CEL's `all()` macro with `startsWith()` to enforce the two allowed prefixes

**Container coverage:**

The CEL expression checks all three container types a Pod can have:
1. `spec.containers` — always present, checked unconditionally
2. `spec.initContainers` — optional; guarded by `!has(...)` so the rule doesn't fail on Pods that omit it
3. `spec.ephemeralContainers` — optional; same guard pattern

Any Pod where even one container uses an image not beginning with `eu.foo.io/` or `bar.io/` will be denied at admission.

description: validates that the Principal in S3 bucket IAM policies is not set to '*', which would allow any principal access to the resource.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1) that evaluates Terraform plan JSON files (object.planned_values.root_module.resources contains the planned resources). The policy must set evaluation.mode: JSON, match all resource types (apiGroups: ["*"], resources: ["*"]), and use matchConditions to identify Terraform plans by checking has(object.planned_values) && has(object.terraform_version). The policy validates that the Principal in S3 bucket IAM policies is not set to '*', which would allow any principal access to the resource. Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/tf_s3_no_wildcard_principal.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-s3-wildcard-principal
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Deny S3 Bucket Wildcard Principal
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Security, Terraform
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: high
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: S3 Bucket IAM Policy
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Validates Terraform plan JSON files to ensure that S3 bucket IAM policies
      do not grant access to all principals ('*'). A wildcard principal allows
      any AWS identity, including unauthenticated (anonymous) users, to perform
      actions on the bucket and is a critical security misconfiguration.
spec:
  evaluation:
    mode: JSON
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ["*"]
        apiVersions: ["*"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["*"]
  matchConditions:
    - name: is-terraform-plan
      expression: 'has(object.planned_values) && has(object.terraform_version)'
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  validations:
    - message: >-
        S3 bucket IAM policies must not set Principal to '*'. A wildcard
        principal grants unrestricted access to all AWS identities, including
        anonymous users. Restrict the Principal to specific AWS accounts,
        IAM roles, or IAM users.
      expression: |
        !has(object.planned_values.root_module) ||
        !has(object.planned_values.root_module.resources) ||
        object.planned_values.root_module.resources
          .filter(r,
            r.type == 'aws_s3_bucket_policy' ||
            r.type == 'aws_s3_bucket'
          )
          .all(r,
            !has(r.values) ||
            !has(r.values.policy) ||
            r.values.policy == null ||
            r.values.policy == '' ||
            (
              !r.values.policy.contains('"Principal":"*"') &&
              !r.values.policy.contains('"Principal": "*"') &&
              !r.values.policy.contains('"AWS":"*"') &&
              !r.values.policy.contains('"AWS": "*"')
            )
          )
raw_log:
Written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a breakdown of every design decision:

---

### Policy structure

| Field | Value | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1` | Requested version; introduced with Kyverno's CEL-based JSON evaluation engine |
| `evaluation.mode` | `JSON` | Treats the payload as an arbitrary JSON document rather than a Kubernetes object |
| `matchConstraints` | `apiGroups/resources: ["*"]` | Wildcard catch-all so the policy fires regardless of how the plan is submitted |
| `matchConditions` | `has(object.planned_values) && has(object.terraform_version)` | Fingerprints the payload as a Terraform plan JSON before applying any validation |
| `validationActions` | `Deny` | Fails the scan/admission check when the rule expression returns false |

---

### Validation logic

The CEL expression in `validations[0].expression` works in layers:

1. **Guard clauses** — short-circuit to `true` (pass) when `root_module` or `resources` is absent, so non-S3 plans are never falsely rejected.
2. **Resource filter** — selects only `aws_s3_bucket_policy` (explicit bucket policy resource) and `aws_s3_bucket` (legacy inline `policy` attribute).
3. **Null-safety** — each inner `!has()` / `== null` / `== ''` guard prevents CEL type errors when a resource has no policy value.
4. **Wildcard checks** — four `contains()` patterns catch both compact and space-padded JSON serialisations of the two common wildcard forms:
   - `"Principal":"*"` / `"Principal": "*"` — direct string wildcard principal
   - `"AWS":"*"` / `"AWS": "*"` — wildcard inside a `Principal` object (`{"Principal":{"AWS":"*"}}`)

description: generate one Chainsaw test set with exactly two scenarios for Kyverno Helm install; scenario A must pass with policy smoke test and scenario B must fail with invalid image/resource pressure as expected
prompt:
You are generating Kubernetes end-to-end tests using Kyverno Chainsaw.

Create a clean test setup with exactly two scenarios to verify Kyverno Helm deployment.

Source policy path: /Users/shutingzhao/go/src/github.com/nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_resource_limits.yaml
Output directory: /Users/shutingzhao/go/src/github.com/nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/ch_kyverno_helm_install
(The source policy is copied into the output directory as policy.yaml.)

Goals:
- Scenario A (pass): Helm install succeeds and Kyverno becomes healthy.
- Scenario A must also include a policy enforcement smoke test after install (one denied and one allowed resource) and it must pass.
- Scenario B (fail): Helm install is intentionally broken by invalid image override and must fail readiness.
- Scenario B must also include a resource pressure failure (unschedulable Kyverno pods via impossible CPU/memory requests/limits) and it must fail as expected.

Requirements:
- Generate all required Chainsaw assets for both scenarios.
- Use separate namespaces and release names:
  - pass namespace: kyverno-pass
  - fail namespace: kyverno-fail
  - pass release: kyverno-pass
  - fail release: kyverno-fail
- Use Helm chart kyverno/kyverno.
- For pass scenario, use valid values and wait for readiness.
- For pass scenario, apply a simple Kyverno validating policy and verify enforcement behavior (deny bad resource, allow good resource).
- For fail scenario, override image repository/tag to an invalid value so pods hit image pull errors.
- For fail scenario, include a resource-pressure variant with unrealistic resource settings that leads to Pending/Unschedulable pods.
- For EVERY fail-scenario Helm install/upgrade command, include `--skip-crds` so cluster-scoped Kyverno CRDs are never re-owned by the fail release.
What to generate:
- A Chainsaw test suite for pass scenario.
- A Chainsaw test suite for fail scenario.
- Any values files, manifests, and helper scripts needed to run both tests.
- A simple runner script that executes both Chainsaw tests and prints clear PASS/FAIL summary.
- Ensure /Users/shutingzhao/go/src/github.com/nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/ch_kyverno_helm_install/chainsaw-test.yaml exists as the primary test entry file.

File/path consistency rules (REQUIRED):
- In chainsaw-test.yaml scripts, reference generated assets using paths relative to the test dir (for example `./policy.yaml`, `./pass/values-pass.yaml`, `./pass/manifests/bad-pod.yaml`).
- Do NOT hardcode `/workspace/output` (or any absolute path) in Helm/Kubectl commands.
- Every referenced file path must exist in the generated output tree with the exact same filename.

Assertions required:
- Pass scenario assertions:
  - Helm release status is deployed.
  - Kyverno controller pod(s) are Ready.
  - No CrashLoopBackOff or ImagePullBackOff in kyverno-pass namespace.
  - Policy enforcement smoke test passes (bad resource denied, good resource allowed).
- Fail scenario assertions:
  - Helm install or rollout readiness fails as expected.
  - At least one Kyverno pod in kyverno-fail shows ErrImagePull or ImagePullBackOff.
  - At least one Kyverno pod in kyverno-fail is Pending with Unschedulable events caused by impossible resource settings.
  - Failure is marked as expected behavior, not an unexpected infra failure.

Output format:
- First, show the test matrix with the two scenarios and expected outcomes.
- Then provide generated file contents.
- Then provide exact commands to run:
  - helm repo add/update
  - chainsaw test commands
- Finally, provide expected output snippets for both PASS and expected FAIL.

Important:
- Keep everything reproducible on a local cluster.
- Do not add extra scenarios.
- Do not leave placeholders. Fill concrete names and values.
- Both Tests share cluster-scoped Kyverno CRDs and webhooks. They MUST run serially.
  Set `spec.concurrent: false` on every Test in chainsaw-test.yaml.
- The pass scenario installs Kyverno via Helm, which creates cluster-scoped
  resources (CRDs in `*.kyverno.io` and `*.wgpolicyk8s.io`, webhooks labeled
  `webhook.kyverno.io/managed-by=kyverno`) that `helm uninstall` does NOT
  remove. If they survive into the fail scenario, the second `helm install`
  will fail with `invalid ownership metadata: ... must equal "<fail-release>":
  current value is "<pass-release>"`.
  The pass scenario's final cleanup step MUST therefore (in this order):
    1. `helm uninstall <pass-release> -n <pass-ns>`
    2. `kubectl delete ns <pass-ns> kyverno-test --ignore-not-found`
    3. `kubectl delete validatingwebhookconfigurations,mutatingwebhookconfigurations \
         -l webhook.kyverno.io/managed-by=kyverno --ignore-not-found`
    4. `kubectl get crds -o name | grep -E '\.(kyverno\.io|wgpolicyk8s\.io)$' \
         | xargs -r kubectl delete --ignore-not-found --timeout=60s`
  Put this in the pass Test's last step (use `finally:` so it runs even on
  failure) with `timeouts: { exec: 4m, cleanup: 4m, delete: 2m }`.
- After pass cleanup, the fail scenario must be resilient to partial cleanup of
  cluster-scoped artifacts. Therefore fail-scenario Helm commands MUST include
  `--skip-crds` (required even if you already delete CRDs in pass cleanup).
- Use deployment label selectors, NOT guessed names, when waiting for
  Kyverno controllers. The Kyverno chart names deployments
  `kyverno-admission-controller` (not `<release>-admission-controller`).
  Prefer:
    kubectl wait --for=condition=Available deployment \
      -l app.kubernetes.io/component=admission-controller \
      -n <ns> --timeout=3m
- Chainsaw default timeouts are aggressive (ApplyTimeout 5s, AssertTimeout 30s,
  CleanupTimeout 30s, ExecTimeout 5s). Long-running scripts (helm install/uninstall,
  kubectl wait, polling loops) WILL be killed unless you override timeouts.
  Required overrides:
    - On any step containing a `helm install`/`helm upgrade`: set
        timeouts:
          exec: 6m
    - On any cleanup block (or step doing `helm uninstall` + ns deletes): set
        timeouts:
          cleanup: 4m
          exec: 4m
          delete: 2m
    - For polling loops or `kubectl wait` scripts longer than 5s, set
        timeouts:
          exec: 4m
  Per-step `timeouts:` is a sibling of `try:`, not nested under it.

Chainsaw schema rules (Chainsaw v0.2.x — STRICT, violating these causes parse errors):
- A Test manifest has only these top-level spec fields: `description`, `concurrent`, `skip`, `skipDelete`, `template`, `timeouts`, `cluster`, `clusters`, `namespace`, `bindings`, `catch`, `steps`. NOTHING ELSE.
- DO NOT put `cleanup:` at `spec` level. There is no `spec.cleanup`. Cleanup must be expressed as either:
    (a) `cleanup:` block INSIDE a `spec.steps[]` entry (runs after that step), or
    (b) a final dedicated step whose `try:` actions delete the resources, or
    (c) rely on Chainsaw's automatic per-test resource cleanup (default behavior).
- Inside a step, valid keys are: `name`, `description`, `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `cleanup`, `bindings`, `timeouts`, `skipDelete`, `cluster`, `clusters`, `template`, `use`. NOTHING ELSE. IMPORTANT: `continueOnError` is NOT valid at step level — it belongs on individual try/catch action items.
- Inside `try`/`catch`/`finally`/`cleanup` action items, valid operations/fields are: `apply`, `assert`, `error`, `command`, `script`, `delete`, `create`, `update`, `patch`, `get`, `describe`, `events`, `logs`, `podLogs`, `sleep`, `wait`, `proxy`, `description`, `continueOnError`. Use ONLY these names.
- `continueOnError: true` is a SIBLING of the operation (e.g. sibling of `script:`), NOT nested inside the operation. Example:
    try:
    - continueOnError: true
      script:
        content: |
          helm install ...
- For `delete:`, use `ref:` (with apiVersion/kind/name/namespace), NOT `resource:`.
- For `describe:`, the operation takes `apiVersion`, `kind`, `name`, `namespace`, `selector`, `showEvents` — these are direct fields on `describe:`, not nested under another key.
- Do not invent fields. When in doubt, omit.

Validation:
- Generate all files as fully specified above.
- Ensure /Users/shutingzhao/go/src/github.com/nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/ch_kyverno_helm_install/chainsaw-test.yaml is valid Chainsaw YAML (check syntax).
- The generated files will be validated separately via chainsaw test CLI.
- Focus on correctness of the manifest content, not on runtime execution.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ Chainsaw examples before writing the test:
- https://kyverno.github.io/chainsaw/latest/
- https://github.com/kyverno/chainsaw

input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-requests-limits
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Limits and Requests
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      As application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources
      requested and consumed by each Pod. It is recommended to require resource requests and
      limits per Pod, especially for memory and CPU. If a Namespace level request or limit is specified,
      defaults will automatically be applied to each Pod based on the LimitRange configuration.
      This policy validates that all containers have something specified for memory and CPU
      requests and memory limits.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-resources
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "CPU and memory resource requests and memory limits are required for containers."
      pattern:
        spec:
          containers:
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
          =(initContainers):
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
          =(ephemeralContainers):
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
