input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-default-namespace
  annotations:
    pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers: none
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Default Namespace
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Kubernetes Namespaces are an optional feature that provide a way to segment and
      isolate cluster resources across multiple applications and users. As a best
      practice, workloads should be isolated with Namespaces. Namespaces should be required
      and the default (empty) Namespace should not be used. This policy validates that Pods
      specify a Namespace name other than `default`. Rule auto-generation is disabled here
      due to Pod controllers need to specify the `namespace` field under the top-level `metadata`
      object and not at the Pod template level.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-namespace
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed."
      pattern:
        metadata:
          namespace: "!default"
  - name: validate-podcontroller-namespace
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - DaemonSet
          - Deployment
          - Job
          - StatefulSet
    validate:
      message: "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed for pod controllers."
      pattern:
        metadata:
          namespace: "!default"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: cli.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: Test
metadata:
  name: disallow-default-namespace-tests
policies:
  - policy.yaml
resources:
  - resources.yaml
results:
  # validate-namespace: Pod NOT in default namespace → pass
  - policy: disallow-default-namespace
    rule: validate-namespace
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-in-production
    result: pass

  # validate-namespace: Pod in default namespace → fail
  - policy: disallow-default-namespace
    rule: validate-namespace
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-in-default
    result: fail

  # validate-podcontroller-namespace: Deployment NOT in default namespace → pass
  - policy: disallow-default-namespace
    rule: validate-podcontroller-namespace
    kind: Deployment
    resources:
      - deployment-in-production
    result: pass

  # validate-podcontroller-namespace: Deployment in default namespace → fail
  - policy: disallow-default-namespace
    rule: validate-podcontroller-namespace
    kind: Deployment
    resources:
      - deployment-in-default
    result: fail

  # validate-podcontroller-namespace: DaemonSet in default namespace → fail
  - policy: disallow-default-namespace
    rule: validate-podcontroller-namespace
    kind: DaemonSet
    resources:
      - daemonset-in-default
    result: fail

  # validate-podcontroller-namespace: StatefulSet NOT in default namespace → pass
  - policy: disallow-default-namespace
    rule: validate-podcontroller-namespace
    kind: StatefulSet
    resources:
      - statefulset-in-production
    result: pass

  # validate-podcontroller-namespace: Job in default namespace → fail
  - policy: disallow-default-namespace
    rule: validate-podcontroller-namespace
    kind: Job
    resources:
      - job-in-default
    result: fail
raw_log:
Both files are written. Here's a summary of what was created:

**`/workspace/output/resources.yaml`** — 7 manifests covering all resource kinds the policy targets:

| Name | Kind | Namespace |
|---|---|---|
| `pod-in-production` | Pod | production |
| `pod-in-default` | Pod | default |
| `deployment-in-production` | Deployment | production |
| `deployment-in-default` | Deployment | default |
| `daemonset-in-default` | DaemonSet | default |
| `statefulset-in-production` | StatefulSet | production |
| `job-in-default` | Job | default |

**`/workspace/output/kyverno-test.yaml`** — 7 result entries for the `disallow-default-namespace` ClusterPolicy:

- `validate-namespace` rule: 1 pass (Pod in `production`), 1 fail (Pod in `default`)
- `validate-podcontroller-namespace` rule: 2 pass (Deployment + StatefulSet in `production`), 4 fail (Deployment, DaemonSet, Job in `default`)

Since the policy is a `ClusterPolicy`, each result uses `rule:` matching the exact rule names from the policy, with no `isValidatingPolicy` field.

input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: inject-sidecar
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Inject Sidecar Container
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Deployment,Volume
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The sidecar pattern is very common in Kubernetes whereby other applications can
      insert components via tacit modification of a submitted resource. This is, for example,
      often how service meshes and secrets applications are able to function transparently.
      This policy injects a sidecar container, initContainer, and volume into Pods that match
      an annotation called `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject: true`.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: inject-sidecar
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Deployment
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        spec:
          template:
            metadata:
              annotations:
                (vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject): "true"
            spec:
              containers:
              - name: vault-agent
                image: vault:1.5.4
                imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
                volumeMounts:
                - mountPath: /vault/secrets
                  name: vault-secret
              initContainers:
              - name: vault-agent-init
                image: vault:1.5.4
                imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
                volumeMounts:
                - mountPath: /vault/secrets
                  name: vault-secret
              volumes:
              - name: vault-secret
                emptyDir:
                  medium: Memory
output_yaml:
apiVersion: cli.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: Test
metadata:
  name: inject-sidecar-test
policies:
- policy.yaml
resources:
- resources.yaml
results:
- policy: inject-sidecar
  rule: inject-sidecar
  kind: Deployment
  resources:
  - deployment-with-vault-annotation
  result: pass
- policy: inject-sidecar
  rule: inject-sidecar
  kind: Deployment
  resources:
  - deployment-without-annotation
  result: skip
raw_log:
Both files are in place. Here's a summary of what was written and why:

**Policy context:** `inject-sidecar` is a `ClusterPolicy` with a `mutate` rule using `patchStrategicMerge`. The patch has a conditional anchor `(vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject): "true"` on the pod template's annotations — meaning the sidecar injection only fires when that annotation is present and set to `"true"`.

**`resources.yaml`** — two Deployments:
- `deployment-with-vault-annotation`: pod template has `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject: "true"` → triggers mutation
- `deployment-without-annotation`: no annotation → mutation condition not met

**`kyverno-test.yaml`** — two result entries:
| Resource | Result | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `deployment-with-vault-annotation` | `pass` | Annotation matches the conditional anchor; vault-agent sidecar, initContainer, and volume are injected |
| `deployment-without-annotation` | `skip` | Annotation absent; conditional anchor fails, rule is skipped entirely |

Since this is a `ClusterPolicy`, each result includes the `rule: inject-sidecar` field (matching the exact rule name) and omits `isValidatingPolicy`.

input_snippet:
# This is an example rule intended to be cloned & modified to meet organizational requirements.
# The `dataprotetion` label value can be changed to correspond with specific policy templates.
# 
# NOTE: Use of this policy will require granting the Kyverno background-controller additional privileges required to generate Kasten resources. An example ClusterRole to provide required privileges is provided within the comments of the policy manifest.
#
# apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
# kind: ClusterRole
# metadata:
#   labels:
#     app.kubernetes.io/component: background-controller
#     app.kubernetes.io/instance: kyverno
#     app.kubernetes.io/part-of: kyverno
#   name: kyverno:create-kasten-policies
# rules:
# - apiGroups:
#   - config.kio.kasten.io
#   resources:
#   - policies
#   verbs:
#   - create
#   - update
#   - delete
#
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Generate Kasten Backup Policy Based on Resource Label
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Veeam Kasten
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.12.1
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.12.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.24-1.30"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Policy
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Generates a Kasten policy for a namespace that includes any Deployment or StatefulSet with a "dataprotection=kasten-example" label, if the policy does not already exist. This Kyverno policy can be used in combination with the "kasten-data-protection-by-label" policy to require "dataprotection" labeling on workloads.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
            - Deployment
            - StatefulSet
          selector:
            matchLabels:
              dataprotection: kasten-example
    context:
    - name: dataprotectionLabelValue
      variable:
        value: "kasten-example"
    - name: kyvernoPolicyName
      variable:
        value: "kasten-generate-example-backup-policy"
    - name: existingPolicy
      apiCall:
        urlPath: "/apis/config.kio.kasten.io/v1alpha1/namespaces/kasten-io/policies" # returns list of Kasten policies from kasten-io namespace
        jmesPath: "items[][[@.metadata.labels.\"generate.kyverno.io/policy-name\"=='{{ kyvernoPolicyName }}'] && [@.spec.selector.matchExpressions[].values[?@=='{{ request.namespace }}']]][][][][] | length(@)" # queries if a Kasten policy protecting the namespace generated by this Kyverno policy already exists 
    preconditions:
      any:
      - key: "{{ existingPolicy }}"
        operator: Equals
        value: 0 # Only generate the policy if it does not already exist
    generate:
      apiVersion: config.kio.kasten.io/v1alpha1
      kind: Policy
      name: "{{ request.namespace }}-{{ dataprotectionLabelValue }}-policy"
      namespace: kasten-io
      data:   
        metadata: 
          name: "{{ request.namespace }}-{{ dataprotectionLabelValue }}-policy"
          namespace: kasten-io
        spec:
          comment: "Auto-generated by Kyverno"
          frequency: '@daily'
          retention:
            daily: 7
            weekly: 4
            monthly: 12
            yearly: 7
          actions:
          - action: backup
          - action: export
            exportParameters:
              frequency: '@daily'
              profile:
                name: test
                namespace: kasten-io
              exportData:
                enabled: true
          selector:
            matchExpressions:
              - key: k10.kasten.io/appNamespace
                operator: In
                values:
                  - "{{ request.namespace }}"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: cli.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: Test
metadata:
  name: kasten-generate-backup-policy-test
policies:
  - policy.yaml
resources:
  - resources.yaml
results:
  # ── Passing cases ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Deployment with dataprotection=kasten-example triggers the generate rule.
  - policy: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    rule: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    kind: Deployment
    resources:
      - deployment-matching
    result: pass

  # StatefulSet with dataprotection=kasten-example also triggers the rule.
  - policy: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    rule: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    kind: StatefulSet
    resources:
      - statefulset-matching
    result: pass

  # ── Failing (skip) cases ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Deployment without the required label: match block does not fire.
  - policy: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    rule: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    kind: Deployment
    resources:
      - deployment-no-label
    result: skip

  # Deployment with a different dataprotection value: selector does not match.
  - policy: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    rule: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    kind: Deployment
    resources:
      - deployment-wrong-label
    result: skip

  # StatefulSet without the required label: match block does not fire.
  - policy: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    rule: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    kind: StatefulSet
    resources:
      - statefulset-no-label
    result: skip
raw_log:
Every name in `results[].resources` has an exact match in `resources.yaml`. Here's a summary of what was written:

---

**`/workspace/output/resources.yaml`** — 5 manifests:

| Name | Kind | Label | Expected behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| `deployment-matching` | Deployment | `dataprotection: kasten-example` | triggers generate |
| `statefulset-matching` | StatefulSet | `dataprotection: kasten-example` | triggers generate |
| `deployment-no-label` | Deployment | *(none)* | rule skipped |
| `deployment-wrong-label` | Deployment | `dataprotection: no-backup` | rule skipped |
| `statefulset-no-label` | StatefulSet | *(none)* | rule skipped |

**`/workspace/output/kyverno-test.yaml`** — 5 result entries, all for `ClusterPolicy` so they include `rule: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy` (no `isValidatingPolicy`):
- 2 × `result: pass` — resources with the `dataprotection: kasten-example` label on a Deployment or StatefulSet satisfy the `match` block and fire the generate rule
- 3 × `result: skip` — resources without the label (or with the wrong value) don't match, so the rule is skipped

**Note on the apiCall precondition:** When run via `kyverno test` (no live cluster), the API call for `existingPolicy` returns an empty list, so `length(@)` evaluates to `0`, satisfying the precondition `existingPolicy == 0` and allowing the generate rule to fire for matching resources.

kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: drop-all-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Drop All Capabilities
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices

    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Capabilities permit privileged actions without giving full root access. All
      capabilities should be dropped from a Pod, with only those required added back.
      This policy ensures that all containers explicitly specify the `drop: ["ALL"]`
      ability. Note that this policy also illustrates how to cover drop entries in any
      case although this may not strictly conform to the Pod Security Standards.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
    - name: require-drop-all
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{ request.operation || 'BACKGROUND' }}"
          operator: NotEquals
          value: DELETE
      validate:
        message: >-
          Containers must drop `ALL` capabilities.
        foreach:
          - list: request.object.spec.[ephemeralContainers, initContainers, containers][]
            deny:
              conditions:
                all:
                - key: ALL
                  operator: AnyNotIn
                  value: "{{ element.securityContext.capabilities.drop[].to_upper(@) || `[]` }}"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: cli.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: Test
metadata:
  name: drop-all-capabilities-test
policies:
  - policy.yaml
resources:
  - resources.yaml
results:
  # --- Passing cases ---

  # Single container explicitly dropping ALL (uppercase)
  - policy: drop-all-capabilities
    rule: require-drop-all
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-drop-all-uppercase
    result: pass

  # Single container dropping all (lowercase) — policy normalises via to_upper so this passes
  - policy: drop-all-capabilities
    rule: require-drop-all
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-drop-all-lowercase
    result: pass

  # Init container + multiple regular containers all dropping ALL
  - policy: drop-all-capabilities
    rule: require-drop-all
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-multi-container-drop-all
    result: pass

  # --- Failing cases ---

  # No securityContext at all — capabilities.drop is absent
  - policy: drop-all-capabilities
    rule: require-drop-all
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-no-security-context
    result: fail

  # securityContext present but capabilities.drop entirely missing
  - policy: drop-all-capabilities
    rule: require-drop-all
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-no-capabilities-drop
    result: fail

  # capabilities.drop set to specific caps (NET_RAW, SYS_ADMIN) but not ALL
  - policy: drop-all-capabilities
    rule: require-drop-all
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-partial-capabilities-drop
    result: fail

  # One container compliant, one container only drops NET_RAW — policy must deny
  - policy: drop-all-capabilities
    rule: require-drop-all
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-mixed-capabilities
    result: fail

apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Labels
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod, Label
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Define and use labels that identify semantic attributes of your application or Deployment.
      A common set of labels allows tools to work collaboratively, describing objects in a common manner that
      all tools can understand. The recommended labels describe applications in a way that can be
      queried. This policy validates that the label `app.kubernetes.io/name` is specified with some value.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: check-for-labels
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "The label `app.kubernetes.io/name` is required."
      pattern:
        metadata:
          labels:
            app.kubernetes.io/name: "?*"

apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-labels
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Labels
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Label
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Labels are used as an important source of metadata describing objects in various ways
      or triggering other functionality. Labels are also a very basic concept and should be
      used throughout Kubernetes. This policy performs a simple mutation which adds a label
      `foo=bar` to Pods, Services, ConfigMaps, and Secrets.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: add-labels
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
          - Service
          - ConfigMap
          - Secret
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        metadata:
          labels:
            foo: bar

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-labels
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Labels
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Label
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Labels are used as an important source of metadata describing objects in various ways
      or triggering other functionality. Labels are also a very basic concept and should be
      used throughout Kubernetes. This policy performs a simple mutation which adds a label
      `foo=bar` to Pods, Services, ConfigMaps, and Secrets.
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['pods', 'services', 'configmaps', 'secrets']
  mutations:
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: >
          Object{
            metadata: Object.metadata{
              labels: Object.metadata.labels{
                foo: "bar"
              }
            }
          }
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API & Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: MutatingPolicy` (cluster-scoped equivalent in the new API)

**Annotations**
- All `policies.kyverno.io/*` annotations are preserved verbatim — they carry important metadata (title, category, severity, subject, description) as noted in your requirements. `minversion` updated to `1.17.0`.

**Match constraints**
- `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Pod, Service, ConfigMap, Secret]` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with a single entry covering all four resource types (lowercased plural, as required by the Kubernetes API machinery). All four are core `v1` resources with `apiGroup: ""`.
- `operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']` added explicitly — the original ClusterPolicy had no operation filter, meaning it applied on both CREATE and UPDATE.

**Mutation**
- `spec.rules[].mutate.patchStrategicMerge` → `spec.mutations[].patchType: ApplyConfiguration` with a CEL `applyConfiguration.expression` returning the equivalent `Object{ metadata: Object.metadata{ labels: Object.metadata.labels{ foo: "bar" } } }`.

input_snippet:
apiVersion : kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-resources
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Default Resources
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.10.0-alpha.2
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.7.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Pods which don't specify at least resource requests are assigned a QoS class
      of BestEffort which can hog resources for other Pods on Nodes. At a minimum,
      all Pods should specify resource requests in order to be labeled as the QoS
      class Burstable. This sample mutates any container in a Pod which doesn't
      specify memory or cpu requests to apply some sane defaults.
spec:
  background: false
  rules:
  - name: add-default-requests
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      any:
      - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
        operator: AnyIn
        value:
        - CREATE
        - UPDATE
    mutate:
      foreach:
      - list: "request.object.spec.[ephemeralContainers, initContainers, containers][]"
        patchStrategicMerge:
          spec:
            containers:
            - (name): "{{element.name}}"
              resources:
                requests:
                  +(memory): "100Mi"
                  +(cpu): "100m"
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of all the changes made and why:

**API / Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: MutatingPolicy`

**Match constraints**
- The old JMESPath-based `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources` block is replaced by `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules`, which mirrors the Kubernetes `MutatingAdmissionPolicy` structure and takes `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, and `resources`.

**Preconditions → matchConditions / operations**
- The old `preconditions` that filtered on `CREATE`/`UPDATE` are now expressed directly as `operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']` inside `matchConstraints.resourceRules`, which is the idiomatic CEL-policy approach.

**`spec.background: false` → `spec.evaluation`**
- Replaced with the explicit `evaluation.admission.enabled: true` / `evaluation.mutateExisting.enabled: false` fields.

**`mutate.foreach` + `patchStrategicMerge` → `mutations` with CEL `map()`**
- `patchStrategicMerge` is not supported in `MutatingPolicy`; mutations must use `patchType: ApplyConfiguration` (CEL `Object{}` expressions) or `patchType: JSONPatch`.
- The original single `foreach` loop across all container lists is split into three separate `ApplyConfiguration` mutations — one for `containers` (always present), one for `initContainers`, and one for `ephemeralContainers` (both guarded with `.orValue([]).size() > 0` to produce an empty `Object{}` when absent).
- The `+(memory)` / `+(cpu)` anchors (only set if missing) are replicated in CEL via a ternary: if the field already exists keep it, otherwise default to `"100Mi"` / `"100m"`.
error_summary:
# Test Failure Summary

The policy `add-default-resources` is applying resource defaults to a Pod (`nginx-demo2`) when it shouldn't be. The test expected this particular Pod to be skipped by the policy, but instead the policy processed it and added default resources, causing the test to fail.

      This policy mutates all Pods to add the ndots option with a value of 1.

description: to better control the number of resources that can be created in a given Namespace and provide default resource consumption limits for Pods, ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources are recommended
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_add_ns_quota.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ GeneratingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy to better control the number of resources that can be created in a given Namespace and provide default resource consumption limits for Pods, ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources are recommended. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_add_ns_quota.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-ns-quota
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Quota
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: ResourceQuota, LimitRange
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      To better control the number of resources that can be created in a given
      Namespace and provide default resource consumption limits for Pods,
      ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources are recommended.
      This policy will generate ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources when
      a new Namespace is created.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: generate-resourcequota
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Namespace
    generate:
      apiVersion: v1
      kind: ResourceQuota
      name: default-resourcequota
      synchronize: true
      namespace: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}"
      data:
        spec:
          hard:
            requests.cpu: '4'
            requests.memory: '16Gi'
            limits.cpu: '4'
            limits.memory: '16Gi'
  - name: generate-limitrange
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Namespace
    generate:
      apiVersion: v1
      kind: LimitRange
      name: default-limitrange
      synchronize: true
      namespace: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}"
      data:
        spec:
          limits:
          - default:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 1Gi
            defaultRequest:
              cpu: 200m
              memory: 256Mi
            type: Container
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API changes:**
- `apiVersion`: `kyverno.io/v1` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind`: `ClusterPolicy` → `GeneratingPolicy`
- `minversion`: `1.6.0` → `1.17.0`

**Spec restructuring:**
- The two separate `rules` (one for ResourceQuota, one for LimitRange) are collapsed into a single `generate` expression — both downstream resources are defined together in a `downstream` variable as a list of CEL objects.
- `match.any[].resources.kinds` → `matchConstraints.resourceRules` (Kubernetes-native VAP-style constraint syntax)
- `generate.synchronize: true` → `evaluation.synchronize.enabled: true`
- `generate.namespace: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}"` (JMESPath) → `variables.nsName` using CEL expression `object.metadata.name`
- `generate.data` blocks → CEL object literals using `dyn()` wrappers for dynamic typing
- Generation is invoked via `generator.Apply(variables.nsName, variables.downstream)`, passing the target namespace and both downstream resource objects in one call
error_summary:
# Summary

The policy's CEL expression is incorrectly wrapping object values with the `dyn()` function in two places (`default` and `defaultRequest` fields). The CEL compiler expects these fields to contain strings (like `"500m"` and `"1Gi"`), but instead received dynamic-typed objects, causing a type mismatch error.

description: the Kubernetes cluster autoscaler does not evict pods that  use hostPath or emptyDir volumes
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_add_safe_to_evict.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy the Kubernetes cluster autoscaler does not evict pods that  use hostPath or emptyDir volumes. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_add_safe_to_evict.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-safe-to-evict
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Safe To Evict
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod,Annotation
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The Kubernetes cluster autoscaler does not evict pods that 
      use hostPath or emptyDir volumes. To allow eviction of these pods, the annotation 
      cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict=true must be added to the pods. 
spec: 
  rules: 
  - name: annotate-empty-dir
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        metadata:
          annotations:
            +(cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict): "true"
        spec:          
          volumes: 
          - <(emptyDir): {}
  - name: annotate-host-path
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        metadata:
          annotations:
            +(cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict): "true"
        spec:          
          volumes: 
          - hostPath:
              <(path): "*"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-safe-to-evict
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Safe To Evict
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod,Annotation
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: "1.17.0"
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The Kubernetes cluster autoscaler does not evict pods that use hostPath or
      emptyDir volumes. To allow eviction of these pods, the annotation
      cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict=true must be added to the
      pods. This policy mutates pods that use either volume type to add the
      annotation if it is not already present.
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  # Only mutate pods that have at least one emptyDir or hostPath volume and
  # do not already carry the safe-to-evict annotation.
  matchConditions:
    - name: has-emptydir-or-hostpath
      expression: >-
        has(object.spec.volumes) &&
        object.spec.volumes.exists(v,
          has(v.emptyDir) || has(v.hostPath)
        )
    - name: annotation-not-set
      expression: >-
        !has(object.metadata.annotations) ||
        !(
          "cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict" in
          object.metadata.annotations
        )
  mutations:
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: >-
          Object{
            metadata: Object.metadata{
              annotations: {
                "cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict": "true"
              }
            }
          }
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made during the conversion:

**API / Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: MutatingPolicy`

**Match rules**
- The old `spec.rules[].match` with `kinds: [Pod]` is replaced by `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` (the Kubernetes-native CEL admission style), specifying group/version/operations/resources explicitly.

**Condition logic (two rules → matchConditions)**
- The original policy had two separate rules to handle emptyDir and hostPath volumes. In the new format, both are collapsed into a single mutation with two `matchConditions`:
  1. `has-emptydir-or-hostpath` — CEL expression checking whether any volume is `emptyDir` or `hostPath` (equivalent to the old `<(emptyDir)` / `<(path)` anchors).
  2. `annotation-not-set` — guards against overwriting an explicitly set annotation (equivalent to the old `+(…)` "add-if-not-present" anchor).

**Mutation expression**
- `patchStrategicMerge` with YAML anchors → `patchType: ApplyConfiguration` with a CEL `Object{}` expression that adds the annotation.

description: a Pod securityContext entry defines fields such as the user and group which should be used to run the Pod
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_add_securitycontext.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy a Pod securityContext entry defines fields such as the user and group which should be used to run the Pod. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_add_securitycontext.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-securitycontext
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Default securityContext
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A Pod securityContext entry defines fields such as the user and group which should be used to run the Pod.
      Sometimes choosing default values for users rather than blocking is a better alternative to not impede
      such Pod definitions. This policy will mutate a Pod to set `runAsNonRoot`, `runAsUser`, `runAsGroup`, and 
      `fsGroup` fields within the Pod securityContext if they are not already set.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: add-default-securitycontext
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        spec:
          securityContext:
            +(runAsNonRoot): true
            +(runAsUser): 1000
            +(runAsGroup): 3000
            +(fsGroup): 2000
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-securitycontext
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Default securityContext
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A Pod securityContext entry defines fields such as the user and group which
      should be used to run the Pod. Sometimes choosing default values for users
      rather than blocking is a better alternative to not impede such Pod
      definitions. This policy will mutate a Pod to set `runAsNonRoot`,
      `runAsUser`, `runAsGroup`, and `fsGroup` fields within the Pod
      securityContext if they are not already set.
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  mutations:
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: |
          Object{
            spec: Object.spec{
              securityContext: Object.spec.securityContext{
                runAsNonRoot: (!has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot)) ? true : object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot,
                runAsUser:    (!has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsUser))    ? 1000 : object.spec.securityContext.runAsUser,
                runAsGroup:   (!has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsGroup))   ? 3000 : object.spec.securityContext.runAsGroup,
                fsGroup:      (!has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.fsGroup))      ? 2000 : object.spec.securityContext.fsGroup
              }
            }
          }
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API / kind changes:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: MutatingPolicy`
- `minversion` annotation updated to `1.17.0`

**Spec structure changes:**
- `spec.rules[].match` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules[]` (standard Kubernetes `MutatingAdmissionPolicy` style, with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, and `resources`)
- `spec.rules[].mutate.patchStrategicMerge` → `spec.mutations[].patchType: ApplyConfiguration` with a CEL `applyConfiguration.expression`

**Anchor (`+(field)`) → CEL conditional logic:**

The old `+(field): value` anchor (set only if the field is absent) is replicated in CEL using `has()` checks:

```yaml
runAsNonRoot: (!has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot)) ? true : object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot
```

This preserves the original semantics exactly — each field is only set to its default when it is not already present on the incoming Pod.

apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-tolerations
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Tolerations
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.7.1
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >- 
      Pod tolerations are used to schedule on Nodes which have
      a matching taint. This policy adds the toleration `org.com/role=service:NoSchedule`
      if existing tolerations do not contain the key `org.com/role`.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: service-toleration
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      any:
      - key: "org.com/role"
        operator: AnyNotIn
        value: "{{ request.object.spec.tolerations[].key || `[]` }}"
    mutate:
      patchesJson6902: |-
        - op: add
          path: "/spec/tolerations/-"
          value:
            key: org.com/role
            operator: Equal
            value: service
            effect: NoSchedule

yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: block-stale-images
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Block Stale Images
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images that are old usually have some open security vulnerabilities which are not patched.
      This policy checks the contents of every container image and inspects them for the create time.
      If it finds any image which was built more than 6 months ago this policy blocks the deployment.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['pods']
  variables:
    # Collect all container types into a single list for uniform processing.
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
    # 6 months expressed as a duration (matches original 4380h threshold).
    - name: maxAge
      expression: "duration('4380h')"
  validations:
    # The original policy used a deny rule (block when age > 4380h), so the
    # CEL expression must return true when the image is NOT stale (age <= 4380h).
    # Images with no created timestamp are skipped (allowed through).
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(c,
          image.GetMetadata(c.image).config.created == "" ||
          now() - timestamp(image.GetMetadata(c.image).config.created) <= variables.maxAge
        )
      messageExpression: >-
        "Images built more than 6 months ago are prohibited. Stale container(s): " +
        variables.allContainers.filter(c,
          image.GetMetadata(c.image).config.created != "" &&
          now() - timestamp(image.GetMetadata(c.image).config.created) > variables.maxAge
        ).map(c, c.name + " (" + c.image + ")").join(", ")
      message: "Images built more than 6 months ago are prohibited."

apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: block-stale-images
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Block Stale Images
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images that are old usually have some open security vulnerabilities which are not patched.
      This policy checks the contents of every container image and inspects them for the create time.
      If it finds any image which was built more than 6 months ago this policy blocks the deployment.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit 
  rules:
    - name: block-stale-images
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Images built more than 6 months ago are prohibited."
        foreach:
        - list: "request.object.spec.containers"
          context:
          - name: imageData
            imageRegistry:
              reference: "{{ element.image }}"
          deny:
            conditions:
              all:
                - key: "{{ time_since('', '{{ imageData.configData.created }}', '') }}"
                  operator: GreaterThan
                  value: 4380h

raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every change made and why:

**`apiVersion` / `kind`**
- `kyverno.io/v1` + `ClusterPolicy` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `ValidatingPolicy` (the new GA CEL-based type introduced in Kyverno 1.17)

**`spec.validationFailureAction`**
- `Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` (new field name and list format; `Enforce` maps to `Deny`, `Audit` stays `Audit`)

**`spec.rules[].match`**
- Replaced with `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules`, which is the Kubernetes `ValidatingAdmissionPolicy`-compatible structure using `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, and `resources`

**`spec.rules[].validate.foreach` + `context[].imageRegistry`**
- The `foreach` loop with JMESPath `imageRegistry` context (Kyverno-specific) is replaced by:
  - A `spec.variables` entry collecting all containers (`allContainers`) including init and ephemeral containers via CEL optional chaining (`?initContainers.orValue([])`)
  - A `spec.variables` entry for `maxAge` as a CEL `duration('4380h')` value
  - `image.GetMetadata(c.image).config.created` from the **ImageData CEL library** — the direct equivalent of `imageRegistry.reference` + `configData.created`

**`validate.deny.conditions` (logic inversion)**
- The original **denied** when age `> 4380h`. CEL `validations` expressions must return `true` to **pass**, so the condition is inverted: validation passes when `now() - timestamp(created) <= maxAge`
- Images with an empty `created` field are explicitly allowed through (same defensive behavior as the original)

**`validate.message`**
- Static `message` is kept as a fallback; a dynamic `messageExpression` is added that names the specific stale containers and their images, which is surfaced in `PolicyReport` entries
error_summary:
# Summary

The policy uses a function called `now()` to get the current time, but this function doesn't exist in Kyverno's CEL expression language. The policy is trying to calculate how old a container image is by comparing its creation timestamp to the current time, but it's using an unsupported method to reference "now."

yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: check-nvidia-gpus
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Check NVIDIA GPUs
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.27"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Containers which request use of an NVIDIA GPU often need to
      be authored to consume them via a CUDA environment variable called
      NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES. This policy checks the containers which
      request a GPU to ensure they have been authored with this environment
      variable.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  variables:
    - name: containersRequestingGPU
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.filter(c,
          has(c.resources) &&
          has(c.resources.limits) &&
          has(c.resources.limits["nvidia.com/gpu"]) &&
          int(c.resources.limits["nvidia.com/gpu"]) > 0
        )
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.containersRequestingGPU.all(c,
          image.GetMetadata(c.image).config.env.exists(e,
            e.startsWith("NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=")
          )
        )
      message: "Images which reserve NVIDIA GPUs must be built to use them."
      messageExpression: >-
        "Container '" +
        variables.containersRequestingGPU.filter(c,
          !image.GetMetadata(c.image).config.env.exists(e,
            e.startsWith("NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=")
          )
        )[0].name +
        "' requests an NVIDIA GPU but its image was not built with the CUDA environment variable NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES."
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: check-nvidia-gpus
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Check NVIDIA GPUs
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Containers which request use of an NVIDIA GPU often need to
      be authored to consume them via a CUDA environment variable called
      NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES. This policy checks the containers which
      request a GPU to ensure they have been authored with this environment
      variable.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  rules:
  - name: check-nvidia-gpus
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      all:
      - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
        operator: NotEquals
        value: DELETE
    validate:
      message: "Images which reserve NVIDIA GPUs must be built to use them."
      foreach:
      - list: "request.object.spec.containers"
        context: 
        - name: imageData
          imageRegistry: 
            reference: "{{ element.image }}"
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
              # If a container image calls for an NVIDIA GPU in its resources.limits, it must also
              # have been built with the CUDA environment variable `NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`.
              - key: "NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=*?"
                operator: AnyNotIn
                value: "{{ imageData.configData.config.Env || '' }}"
              - key: "{{ element.resources.limits.\"nvidia.com/gpu\" || '' }}"
                operator: GreaterThan
                value: 0

raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made during conversion:

**API / Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `ValidatingPolicy`

**Validation action**
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`

**Match constraints**
- The old JMESPath-based `match.any[].resources` block is replaced by `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules`, which mirrors the Kubernetes `ValidatingAdmissionPolicy` structure. The `DELETE` precondition is dropped naturally since only `CREATE` and `UPDATE` are listed in `operations`.

**Image metadata (key change)**
- The old policy used a JMESPath `foreach` loop with an `imageRegistry` context entry to fetch `configData.config.Env` from the OCI registry.
- The new policy uses the Kyverno CEL **ImageData library** function `image.GetMetadata(c.image).config.env`, which provides the same OCI image config environment variables in native CEL.

**Logic**
- A `variables` entry pre-filters `containersRequestingGPU` (containers whose `resources.limits["nvidia.com/gpu"] > 0`), replacing the old inline `deny.conditions`.
- The validation expression uses `.all()` + `.exists()` CEL macros to assert every GPU-requesting container's image was built with `NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=*`.
- A dynamic `messageExpression` names the offending container in the failure message.
error_summary: The policy uses an invalid syntax for checking if a dictionary key exists in Kyverno's CEL language. The `has()` function cannot be used with bracket notation (like `has(c.resources.limits["nvidia.com/gpu"])`) to check for specific keys in a map; it only works with direct object properties. This syntax mismatch prevents the policy from being compiled and executed.

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: GeneratingPolicy
metadata:
  name: create-default-pdb
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Pod Disruption Budget
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Deployment
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A PodDisruptionBudget limits the number of Pods of a replicated application that
      are down simultaneously from voluntary disruptions. For example, a quorum-based
      application would like to ensure that the number of replicas running is never brought
      below the number needed for a quorum. As an application owner, you can create a PodDisruptionBudget (PDB)
      for each application. This policy will create a PDB resource whenever a new Deployment is created.
spec:
  evaluation:
    synchronize:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['apps']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE']
        resources: ['deployments']
  variables:
    - name: deploymentName
      expression: 'object.metadata.name'
    - name: deploymentNamespace
      expression: 'object.metadata.namespace'
    - name: deploymentSelector
      expression: 'object.spec.selector'
    - name: downstream
      expression: >-
        [
          {
            "kind": dyn("PodDisruptionBudget"),
            "apiVersion": dyn("policy/v1"),
            "metadata": dyn({
              "name": variables.deploymentName + "-default-pdb",
              "namespace": variables.deploymentNamespace
            }),
            "spec": dyn({
              "minAvailable": 1,
              "selector": dyn(variables.deploymentSelector)
            })
          }
        ]
  generate:
    - expression: generator.Apply(variables.deploymentNamespace, variables.downstream)
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: create-default-pdb
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Pod Disruption Budget
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.2
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Deployment
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A PodDisruptionBudget limits the number of Pods of a replicated application that
      are down simultaneously from voluntary disruptions. For example, a quorum-based
      application would like to ensure that the number of replicas running is never brought
      below the number needed for a quorum. As an application owner, you can create a PodDisruptionBudget (PDB)
      for each application. This policy will create a PDB resource whenever a new Deployment is created.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: create-default-pdb
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Deployment
    generate:
      apiVersion: policy/v1
      kind: PodDisruptionBudget
      name: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}-default-pdb"
      namespace: "{{request.object.metadata.namespace}}"
      data:
        spec:
          minAvailable: 1
          selector: "{{request.object.spec.selector}}"

raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made during the conversion:

**`apiVersion` & `kind`**
- `kyverno.io/v1` / `ClusterPolicy` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `GeneratingPolicy`

**`spec.rules[]` → flat `spec` fields**
The old `rules[].match` + `rules[].generate` structure is replaced by top-level CEL-based fields:

| Old (ClusterPolicy) | New (GeneratingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Deployment]` | `matchConstraints.resourceRules` with `apiGroups: [apps]`, `resources: [deployments]` |
| `rules[].generate.data.spec.*` | `variables[].expression` using CEL + `dyn()` for dynamic typing |
| `rules[].generate.name/namespace` using JMESPath `{{...}}` | CEL string concatenation in `variables` |
| implicit no-sync | `evaluation.synchronize.enabled: true` (keeps PDB in sync with trigger) |

**`generate` expression**
Uses `generator.Apply(namespace, resources)` — the canonical GeneratingPolicy function — to create the PDB in the deployment's namespace, with `minAvailable: 1` and the deployment's label selector copied via `dyn(variables.deploymentSelector)`.
error_summary: The policy's CEL expression is trying to use variables in places where the compiler expects concrete values with specific types. Specifically, the `namespace` field needs a string value and the `selector` field needs an integer, but the policy is passing in dynamic variables instead, which the type checker cannot validate ahead of time.

yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-container-sock-mounts
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow CRI socket mounts
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Container daemon socket bind mounts allows access to the container engine on the
      node. This access can be used for privilege escalation and to manage containers
      outside of Kubernetes, and hence should not be allowed. This policy validates that
      the sockets used for CRI engines Docker, Containerd, and CRI-O are not used. In addition
      to or replacement of this policy, preventing users from mounting the parent directories
      (/var/run and /var) may be necessary to completely prevent socket bind mounts.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  variables:
    - name: hostPathVolumes
      expression: >-
        object.spec.?volumes.orValue([]).filter(v, has(v.hostPath))
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.hostPathVolumes.all(v,
          v.hostPath.path != '/var/run/docker.sock' &&
          v.hostPath.path != '/var/run/containerd/containerd.sock' &&
          v.hostPath.path != '/var/run/crio/crio.sock' &&
          v.hostPath.path != '/var/run/cri-dockerd.sock'
        )
      message: >-
        Use of container runtime Unix sockets as host path mounts is not allowed.
        The following paths are forbidden: /var/run/docker.sock,
        /var/run/containerd/containerd.sock, /var/run/crio/crio.sock,
        /var/run/cri-dockerd.sock.
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-container-sock-mounts
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow CRI socket mounts
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Container daemon socket bind mounts allows access to the container engine on the
      node. This access can be used for privilege escalation and to manage containers
      outside of Kubernetes, and hence should not be allowed. This policy validates that
      the sockets used for CRI engines Docker, Containerd, and CRI-O are not used. In addition
      to or replacement of this policy, preventing users from mounting the parent directories
      (/var/run and /var) may be necessary to completely prevent socket bind mounts.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-docker-sock-mount
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Use of the Docker Unix socket is not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(volumes):
            - =(hostPath):
                path: "!/var/run/docker.sock"
  - name: validate-containerd-sock-mount
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Use of the Containerd Unix socket is not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(volumes):
            - =(hostPath):
                path: "!/var/run/containerd/containerd.sock"
  - name: validate-crio-sock-mount
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Use of the CRI-O Unix socket is not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(volumes):
            - =(hostPath):
                path: "!/var/run/crio/crio.sock"
  - name: validate-dockerd-sock-mount
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Use of the Docker CRI socket is not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(volumes):
            - =(hostPath):
                path: "!/var/run/cri-dockerd.sock"

raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API/Kind changes:**
- `apiVersion`: `kyverno.io/v1` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind`: `ClusterPolicy` → `ValidatingPolicy`
- Updated `policies.kyverno.io/minversion` to `1.17.0`

**Spec restructuring:**
- `validationFailureAction: Audit` → `validationActions: [Audit]` (the new field name and list format)
- `background: true` → `evaluation.background.enabled: true`
- Added `matchConstraints.resourceRules` (the Kubernetes VAP-style match structure) replacing per-rule `match.any` blocks

**Rule consolidation:**
- The four separate pattern-based rules (one per socket path) were merged into a single CEL `validations` entry. A `variables` stanza pre-filters volumes that have a `hostPath` field using `object.spec.?volumes.orValue([]).filter(...)`, then the validation expression asserts that none of those volumes point to any of the four forbidden socket paths.
error_summary:
# Summary

The policy test failed because a pod named `goodpod01` was expected to be rejected (blocked) by the policy, but it was instead allowed to pass through. This suggests the policy rules are not correctly identifying or blocking the container socket mounts they're designed to prevent.

yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-default-namespace
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Default Namespace
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Kubernetes Namespaces are an optional feature that provide a way to segment and
      isolate cluster resources across multiple applications and users. As a best
      practice, workloads should be isolated with Namespaces. Namespaces should be required
      and the default (empty) Namespace should not be used. This policy validates that Pods
      and pod controllers specify a Namespace name other than `default`.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
      - apiGroups: ["apps"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["daemonsets", "deployments", "statefulsets"]
      - apiGroups: ["batch"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["jobs"]
  validations:
    - expression: "namespaceObject.metadata.name != 'default'"
      message: "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed."
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-default-namespace
  annotations:
    pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers: none
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Default Namespace
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Kubernetes Namespaces are an optional feature that provide a way to segment and
      isolate cluster resources across multiple applications and users. As a best
      practice, workloads should be isolated with Namespaces. Namespaces should be required
      and the default (empty) Namespace should not be used. This policy validates that Pods
      specify a Namespace name other than `default`. Rule auto-generation is disabled here
      due to Pod controllers need to specify the `namespace` field under the top-level `metadata`
      object and not at the Pod template level.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-namespace
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed."
      pattern:
        metadata:
          namespace: "!default"
  - name: validate-podcontroller-namespace
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - DaemonSet
          - Deployment
          - Job
          - StatefulSet
    validate:
      message: "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed for pod controllers."
      pattern:
        metadata:
          namespace: "!default"

**API changes:**
- `apiVersion`: `kyverno.io/v1` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1`

**Spec restructuring:**
