raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made in the conversion:

**API/Kind changes:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Structure changes:**

| Original (ClusterPolicy) | Converted (ValidatingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `spec.background: false` | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: false` |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` (CEL VAP-style) |
| `spec.rules[].preconditions` (JMESPath) | `spec.matchConditions` (CEL expressions) |
| `spec.rules[].context[].apiCall` (JMESPath + urlPath) | `spec.variables` using `resource.List()` CEL library |
| `spec.rules[].validate.deny.conditions` (JMESPath) | `spec.validations[].expression` (CEL) |

**Logic preserved:**
- Only fires on `CREATE` operations (via `matchConstraints.operations`)
- Only fires when `replicas >= 3` (now a `matchCondition` so the policy is skipped entirely for smaller workloads)
- Uses `resource.List("policy/v1", "poddisruptionbudgets", object.metadata.namespace)` to fetch PDBs in the same namespace
- Filters PDBs whose `spec.selector.matchLabels` are a subset of the workload's pod template labels, ensuring at least one match exists
- Added a dynamic `messageExpression` for more informative failure messages
error_summary:
# Summary

The policy's CEL expression is trying to filter through a variable called `pdbs`, but that variable contains data in a format (type `any`) that the CEL compiler doesn't recognize as a list or map, so it cannot iterate over it. The compiler needs the `pdbs` variable to be explicitly defined as a list type before the filter operation can work on it.

description: liveness and readiness probes need to be configured to correctly manage a Pod's lifecycle during deployments, restarts, and upgrades
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_probes.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy liveness and readiness probes need to be configured to correctly manage a Pod's lifecycle during deployments, restarts, and upgrades. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_require_probes.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-pod-probes
  annotations:
    pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers: DaemonSet,Deployment,StatefulSet
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Pod Probes
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Liveness and readiness probes need to be configured to correctly manage a Pod's
      lifecycle during deployments, restarts, and upgrades. For each Pod, a periodic
      `livenessProbe` is performed by the kubelet to determine if the Pod's containers
      are running or need to be restarted. A `readinessProbe` is used by Services
      and Deployments to determine if the Pod is ready to receive network traffic.
      This policy validates that all containers have one of livenessProbe, readinessProbe,
      or startupProbe defined.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-probes
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      all:
      - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
        operator: AnyIn
        value:
        - CREATE
        - UPDATE
    validate:
      message: "Liveness, readiness, or startup probes are required for all containers."
      foreach:
      - list: request.object.spec.containers[]
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
            - key: livenessProbe
              operator: AllNotIn
              value: "{{ element.keys(@)[] }}"
            - key: startupProbe
              operator: AllNotIn
              value: "{{ element.keys(@)[] }}"
            - key: readinessProbe
              operator: AllNotIn
              value: "{{ element.keys(@)[] }}"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-pod-probes
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Pod Probes
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Liveness and readiness probes need to be configured to correctly manage a Pod's
      lifecycle during deployments, restarts, and upgrades. For each Pod, a periodic
      `livenessProbe` is performed by the kubelet to determine if the Pod's containers
      are running or need to be restarted. A `readinessProbe` is used by Services
      and Deployments to determine if the Pod is ready to receive network traffic.
      This policy validates that all containers have one of livenessProbe, readinessProbe,
      or startupProbe defined.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['pods']
  autogen:
    podControllers:
      controllers:
        - daemonsets
        - deployments
        - statefulsets
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          has(container.livenessProbe) ||
          has(container.startupProbe) ||
          has(container.readinessProbe))
      messageExpression: >-
        "Liveness, readiness, or startup probes are required for all containers. " +
        "Containers missing a probe: [" +
        variables.allContainers.filter(container,
          !has(container.livenessProbe) &&
          !has(container.startupProbe) &&
          !has(container.readinessProbe)
        ).map(container, container.name).join(", ") + "]"
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API/Kind changes:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Spec restructuring:**
- `validationFailureAction: Audit` → `validationActions: [Audit]` (new field name/structure)
- `spec.rules[].match` + `spec.background` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` (Kubernetes VAP-style matching)
- Removed `preconditions` block — operations are now declared directly in `matchConstraints.resourceRules`

**Validation logic:**
- Replaced the JMESPath-based `foreach`/`deny` rule with a CEL `validations` expression: `variables.allContainers.all(container, has(container.livenessProbe) || has(container.startupProbe) || has(container.readinessProbe))`
- Added a `variables` block to capture all containers (including `initContainers` and `ephemeralContainers`) using `orValue([])` for optional fields
- Replaced the static `message` with a dynamic `messageExpression` that lists the names of offending containers

**Auto-generation:**
- Replaced the `pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers` annotation with the structured `spec.autogen.podControllers.controllers` field (the native 1.17+ mechanism)

description: as application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources requested and consumed by each Pod
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_resource_limits.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy as application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources requested and consumed by each Pod. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_require_resource_limits.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-requests-limits
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Limits and Requests
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      As application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources
      requested and consumed by each Pod. It is recommended to require resource requests and
      limits per Pod, especially for memory and CPU. If a Namespace level request or limit is specified,
      defaults will automatically be applied to each Pod based on the LimitRange configuration.
      This policy validates that all containers have something specified for memory and CPU
      requests and memory limits.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-resources
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "CPU and memory resource requests and memory limits are required for containers."
      pattern:
        spec:
          containers:
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
          =(initContainers):
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
          =(ephemeralContainers):
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-requests-limits
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Limits and Requests
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      As application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources
      requested and consumed by each Pod. It is recommended to require resource requests and
      limits per Pod, especially for memory and CPU. If a Namespace level request or limit is specified,
      defaults will automatically be applied to each Pod based on the LimitRange configuration.
      This policy validates that all containers have something specified for memory and CPU
      requests and memory limits.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['pods']
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          has(container.resources) &&
          has(container.resources.requests) &&
          has(container.resources.requests.memory) &&
          has(container.resources.requests.cpu) &&
          has(container.resources.limits) &&
          has(container.resources.limits.memory))
      message: "CPU and memory resource requests and memory limits are required for all containers."

The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what changed and why:

**API/Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Enforcement mode**
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` (the `ValidatingPolicy` uses the Kubernetes VAP-style `validationActions` list; `Audit` records violations in PolicyReports without blocking)

**Background scanning**
- `spec.background: true` → `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` (same semantics, new field path)

**Match constraints**
- The Kyverno-pattern `match.any[].resources.kinds[Pod]` → `matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, and `resources` (standard Kubernetes VAP format)

**Validation rule**
- The old pattern-based `validate.pattern` (with `=(initContainers)` / `=(ephemeralContainers)` optional anchors) → a single CEL `validations` expression
- A `variables.allContainers` variable is defined first to collect all container types using `object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])` / `object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])` (the `?` optional field access + `.orValue([])` is the CEL idiom equivalent to the old `=()` optional anchors)
- The `.all()` macro with `has()` checks replicates the original requirement: memory request, CPU request, and memory limit must be present on every container

description: a read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy; the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_ro_rootfs.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy a read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy; the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_require_ro_rootfs.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-ro-rootfs
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Read-Only Root Filesystem
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices, PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy;
      the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state.
      An immutable root filesystem can also prevent malicious binaries from writing to the
      host system. This policy validates that containers define a securityContext
      with `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-readOnlyRootFilesystem
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Root filesystem must be read-only."
      pattern:
        spec:
          containers:
          - securityContext:
              readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-ro-rootfs
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Read-Only Root Filesystem
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices, PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy;
      the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state.
      An immutable root filesystem can also prevent malicious binaries from writing to the
      host system. This policy validates that containers define a securityContext
      with `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(container,
          container.?securityContext.?readOnlyRootFilesystem.orValue(false) == true)
      message: "Root filesystem must be read-only."
    - expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.initContainers) ||
        object.spec.initContainers.all(container,
          container.?securityContext.?readOnlyRootFilesystem.orValue(false) == true)
      message: "Root filesystem must be read-only."
    - expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) ||
        object.spec.ephemeralContainers.all(container,
          container.?securityContext.?readOnlyRootFilesystem.orValue(false) == true)
      message: "Root filesystem must be read-only."
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API and Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Spec structure**
- `validationFailureAction: Audit` → `validationActions: [Audit]` (the new list-based field)
- `background: true` → moved under `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true`
- `rules[].match` → replaced by top-level `matchConstraints.resourceRules` using the Kubernetes VAP structure (apiGroups, apiVersions, operations, resources)
- `rules[].validate.pattern` (Kyverno pattern matching) → `validations[]` with **CEL expressions**

**CEL expressions**
The pattern `containers[*].securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` is translated into three separate `validations` entries covering all container types:
1. `containers` — required by the original policy
2. `initContainers` — guarded with `!has(...)` since they are optional
3. `ephemeralContainers` — guarded with `!has(...)` since they are optional

Each uses `container.?securityContext.?readOnlyRootFilesystem.orValue(false) == true` — the optional chaining (`?`) safely handles containers that omit the `securityContext` or `readOnlyRootFilesystem` field, defaulting to `false` (i.e., a violation).

description: adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels of ability than those with which they may be provisioned
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_restrict_capabilities.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels of ability than those with which they may be provisioned. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_restrict_capabilities.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: psp-restrict-adding-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Adding Capabilities
    policies.kyverno.io/category: PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels
      of ability than those with which they may be provisioned. Many capabilities
      allow system-level control and should be prevented. Pod Security Policies (PSP)
      allowed a list of "good" capabilities to be added. This policy checks
      ephemeralContainers, initContainers, and containers to ensure the only
      capabilities that can be added are either NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
    - name: allowed-capabilities
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{ request.operation || 'BACKGROUND' }}"
          operator: NotEquals
          value: DELETE
      validate:
        message: >-
          Any capabilities added other than NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN are disallowed.
        foreach:
          - list: request.object.spec.[ephemeralContainers, initContainers, containers][]
            deny:
              conditions:
                all:
                - key: "{{ element.securityContext.capabilities.add[] || '' }}"
                  operator: AnyNotIn
                  value:
                  - NET_BIND_SERVICE
                  - CAP_CHOWN
                  - ''
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: psp-restrict-adding-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Adding Capabilities
    policies.kyverno.io/category: PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.30"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels
      of ability than those with which they may be provisioned. Many capabilities
      allow system-level control and should be prevented. Pod Security Policies (PSP)
      allowed a list of "good" capabilities to be added. This policy checks
      ephemeralContainers, initContainers, and containers to ensure the only
      capabilities that can be added are either NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          container.?securityContext.?capabilities.?add.orValue([]).all(cap,
            cap == 'NET_BIND_SERVICE' || cap == 'CAP_CHOWN'
          )
        )
      message: >-
        Any capabilities added other than NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN are disallowed.
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what changed and why:

**Key conversions made:**

| Original (`kyverno.io/v1` ClusterPolicy) | Converted (`policies.kyverno.io/v1` ValidatingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` |
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules[]` |
| `spec.rules[].preconditions` (skip DELETE) | Replaced by only matching `CREATE` and `UPDATE` operations in `matchConstraints` |
| `spec.rules[].validate.foreach` with JMESPath | `spec.variables` + `spec.validations` using CEL |
| JMESPath `AnyNotIn` deny logic | CEL `.all()` with `||` allow-list check |

**CEL expression logic:**
- A `variables.allContainers` variable collects all container types (containers + initContainers + ephemeralContainers) using the optional chaining `?` operator with `.orValue([])` as the safe fallback for absent fields.
- The validation uses `.all()` to assert every container's added capabilities (if any) are only `NET_BIND_SERVICE` or `CAP_CHOWN`, using safe optional access (`?securityContext.?capabilities.?add`) to gracefully handle containers with no security context set.

description: images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_restrict_image_registries.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_restrict_image_registries.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-image-registries
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Image Registries
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be
      scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk. Requiring use of known, approved
      registries helps reduce threat exposure by ensuring image pulls only come from them. This
      policy validates that container images only originate from the registry `eu.foo.io` or
      `bar.io`. Use of this policy requires customization to define your allowable registries.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-registries
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Unknown image registry."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(ephemeralContainers):
          - image: "eu.foo.io/* | bar.io/*"
          =(initContainers):
          - image: "eu.foo.io/* | bar.io/*"
          containers:
          - image: "eu.foo.io/* | bar.io/*"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-image-registries
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Image Registries
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be
      scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk. Requiring use of known, approved
      registries helps reduce threat exposure by ensuring image pulls only come from them. This
      policy validates that container images only originate from the registry `eu.foo.io` or
      `bar.io`. Use of this policy requires customization to define your allowable registries.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(c,
          image(c.image).registry() + "/" == "eu.foo.io/" ||
          image(c.image).registry() == "eu.foo.io" ||
          image(c.image).registry() + "/" == "bar.io/" ||
          image(c.image).registry() == "bar.io"
        )
      messageExpression: >-
        "Unknown image registry. All images must originate from 'eu.foo.io' or 'bar.io'. " +
        "Offending image(s): " +
        variables.allContainers.filter(c,
          !(image(c.image).registry() == "eu.foo.io" || image(c.image).registry() == "bar.io")
        ).map(c, c.image).join(", ")

- `kind`: `ClusterPolicy` → `ValidatingPolicy`
- `policies.kyverno.io/minversion`: updated to `1.17.0`

**Spec restructuring:**
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` (new field name and list format)
- `spec.background: true` and `spec.rules[].match` block are replaced by the Kubernetes-native `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` structure, targeting `pods` in core API group with `CREATE`/`UPDATE` operations
- The pattern-based `validate.pattern` rule is replaced with a CEL `variables` + `validations` block

**Logic translation:**

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: unique-ingress-host
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Unique Ingress Host
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Ingress
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      An Ingress host is a URL at which services may be made available externally. In most cases,
      these hosts should be unique across the cluster to ensure no routing conflicts occur.
      This policy checks an incoming Ingress resource to ensure its hosts are unique to the cluster.
      It also ensures that only a single host may be specified in a given manifest.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ["networking.k8s.io"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["ingresses"]
  variables:
    - name: incomingHosts
      expression: >-
        object.spec.?rules.orValue([]).map(r, r.?host.orValue(""))
    - name: allIngresses
      expression: >-
        resource.List("networking.k8s.io/v1", "ingresses", "").items
    - name: otherIngresses
      expression: >-
        variables.allIngresses.filter(i, i.metadata.uid != object.metadata.?uid.orValue(""))
    - name: existingHostsOnCreate
      expression: >-
        variables.allIngresses.map(i,
          i.spec.?rules.orValue([]).map(r, r.?host.orValue(""))
        ).flatten()
    - name: existingHostsOnUpdate
      expression: >-
        variables.otherIngresses.map(i,
          i.spec.?rules.orValue([]).map(r, r.?host.orValue(""))
        ).flatten()
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.incomingHosts.size() <= 1
      message: "An Ingress resource may only contain a single host entry."
    - expression: >-
        request.operation == "UPDATE"
        ? !variables.incomingHosts.exists(h, h in variables.existingHostsOnUpdate)
        : !variables.incomingHosts.exists(h, h in variables.existingHostsOnCreate)
      message: "The Ingress host name must be unique."
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: unique-ingress-host
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Unique Ingress Host
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Ingress
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      An Ingress host is a URL at which services may be made available externally. In most cases,
      these hosts should be unique across the cluster to ensure no routing conflicts occur.
      This policy checks an incoming Ingress resource to ensure its hosts are unique to the cluster.
      It also ensures that only a single host may be specified in a given manifest.      
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: false
  rules:
    - name: check-single-host-create
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Ingress
      context:
        - name: hosts
          apiCall:
            urlPath: "/apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingresses"
            jmesPath: "items[].spec.rules[].host"
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
          operator: Equals
          value: CREATE
      validate:
        message: "The Ingress host name must be unique."
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
              - key: "{{ request.object.spec.rules[].host }}"
                operator: AnyIn
                value: "{{ hosts }}"
    - name: check-single-host-update
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Ingress
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
          operator: Equals
          value: UPDATE
      context:
        - name: allhosts
          apiCall:
            urlPath: "/apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingresses"
            jmesPath: "items[?metadata.uid!='{{ request.object.metadata.uid }}'].spec.rules[].host"
      validate:
        message: "The Ingress host name must be unique."
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
              - key: "{{ request.object.spec.rules[].host }}"
                operator: AnyIn
                value: "{{ allhosts }}"
    - name: deny-multiple-hosts
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Ingress
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
          operator: AnyIn
          value:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        - key: "{{ request.object.spec.rules[].host | length(@)}}"
          operator: GreaterThan
          value: 1
      validate:
        message: "An Ingress resource may only contain a single host entry."
        deny: {}

The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what changed and why:

**API changes:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` / `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `kind: ValidatingPolicy`
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`
- `spec.background: false` → `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: false`

**Rules → matchConstraints + variables + validations:**
- The three separate rules with JMESPath `context`/`apiCall` entries are replaced with CEL `variables` using the `resource.List()` Kyverno CEL library, which is the idiomatic 1.17+ approach for fetching live cluster data
- `incomingHosts` — extracts the host list from the incoming Ingress
- `allIngresses` / `otherIngresses` — fetches all cluster Ingresses; `otherIngresses` filters out the current object by UID (replicating the UPDATE-specific logic from the original)
- `existingHostsOnCreate` / `existingHostsOnUpdate` — flattens host lists from the relevant ingress sets
- The two `deny` rules (single-host check and uniqueness check) are expressed as CEL `validations`, with the CREATE vs. UPDATE branching handled inline via a ternary expression (`request.operation == "UPDATE" ? ... : ...`)
error_summary:
# Summary

The policy's CEL expressions are treating `variables.allIngresses` as a single object rather than a list, so operations like `.filter()` and `.map()` that expect to iterate over multiple items are failing. Additionally, one of the expressions references a variable called `otherIngresses` that was never defined in the variables section, causing it to be undefined at runtime.

input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-nodeport
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow NodePort
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Service
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A Kubernetes Service of type NodePort uses a host port to receive traffic from
      any source. A NetworkPolicy cannot be used to control traffic to host ports.
      Although NodePort Services can be useful, their use must be limited to Services
      with additional upstream security checks. This policy validates that any new Services
      do not use the `NodePort` type.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-nodeport
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Service
    validate:
      message: "Services of type NodePort are not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(type): "!NodePort"

The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API/Kind changes:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Spec restructuring:**
- `validationFailureAction: Audit` → `validationActions: [Audit]` (list format)
- `background: true` → `evaluation.background.enabled: true`
- The `rules[].match` block using Kyverno's pattern syntax is replaced by `matchConstraints.resourceRules`, which follows the Kubernetes `ValidatingAdmissionPolicy` structure (explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, `resources`)

**Validation logic:**
- The pattern-based `=(type): "!NodePort"` (which conditionally matches only if `type` is present) is replaced with a CEL expression: `!has(object.spec.type) || object.spec.type != 'NodePort'`
- This preserves the same semantics — Services without an explicit `type` (which default to `ClusterIP`) are allowed, only Services explicitly set to `NodePort` are rejected

**Removed:**
- `policies.kyverno.io/minversion` annotation — no longer applicable since the new policy targets Kyverno 1.17+

description: The --force-yes option in apt-get is used to override some safety checks and prompts, allowing the installation or upgrade of packages even if they require additional user confirmation or if they conflict with other packages. This can potentially lead to system instability or unexpected behavior.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1) that evaluates Dockerfiles represented as parsed JSON (object.Stages contains the build stages and commands). The policy must set evaluation.mode: JSON, match all resource types (apiGroups: ["*"], resources: ["*"]), and use matchConditions to identify Dockerfile resources by checking has(object.Stages). The policy The --force-yes option in apt-get is used to override some safety checks and prompts, allowing the installation or upgrade of packages even if they require additional user confirmation or if they conflict with other packages. This can potentially lead to system instability or unexpected behavior. Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/df_check_apt_force_yes.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-apt-get-force-yes
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow apt-get --force-yes
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Dockerfile Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The --force-yes option in apt-get overrides safety checks and prompts,
      allowing installation or upgrade of packages even when they require
      additional user confirmation or conflict with other packages. This can
      lead to system instability or unexpected behavior. This policy ensures
      that Dockerfiles do not use apt-get with the --force-yes flag in any
      RUN instruction.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    mode: JSON
    background:
      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ["*"]
        apiVersions: ["*"]
        resources: ["*"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
  matchConditions:
    - name: has-dockerfile-structure
      expression: "has(object.Stages)"
    - name: has-run-commands
      expression: >-
        has(object.Stages) &&
        object.Stages.exists(stage,
          has(stage.Commands) &&
          stage.Commands.exists(cmd,
            cmd.Name == 'RUN' && has(cmd.CmdLine) && size(cmd.CmdLine) > 0
          )
        )
  validations:
    - expression: |
        !object.Stages.exists(stage,
          has(stage.Commands) && stage.Commands.exists(cmd,
            cmd.Name == 'RUN' && has(cmd.CmdLine) && cmd.CmdLine.exists(cmdLine,
              (cmdLine.startsWith('apt-get ') || cmdLine.contains(' apt-get ')) &&
              cmdLine.contains('--force-yes')
            )
          )
        )
      message: >-
        Using 'apt-get --force-yes' is not allowed. The --force-yes flag
        bypasses safety checks and can lead to system instability or
        unexpected behavior. Use 'apt-get --yes' or '-y' instead.
raw_log:
The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what was produced and why each part was chosen, based on the official Kyverno 1.17+ docs and the nirmata Dockerfile policy examples:

**Policy structure:**

- `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1` — the correct API version for `ValidatingPolicy` in Kyverno 1.17+
- `evaluation.mode: JSON` — enables non-Kubernetes JSON payload processing (required for Dockerfile analysis)
- `evaluation.background.enabled: false` — background scanning is not meaningful for Dockerfile linting pipelines
- `matchConstraints` with `apiGroups: ["*"]`, `resources: ["*"]` — matches all resource types since Dockerfiles are submitted as arbitrary JSON, not typed Kubernetes resources

**`matchConditions`** (CEL guards that filter which payloads this policy evaluates):
1. `has(object.Stages)` — identifies the payload as a parsed Dockerfile
2. A second condition confirms at least one `RUN` command with a non-empty `CmdLine` exists, avoiding false evaluation on empty Dockerfiles

**`validations`** (the actual rule):
- Traverses every stage → every command → every command-line token, asserting that no `apt-get` invocation contains `--force-yes`. The check handles both leading (`apt-get ...`) and inline (e.g. `bash -c "apt-get ..."`) usage via `startsWith` and `contains`.

description: mutates Pods and Services to add the label 'foo: bar' to metadata.labels. The label must be added if absent and left unchanged if already present.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.17+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that mutates Pods and Services to add the label 'foo: bar' to metadata.labels. The label must be added if absent and left unchanged if already present. Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/gen_add_default_labels.yaml.

output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-foo-bar-label
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['pods', 'services']
  mutations:
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: >
          Object{
            metadata: Object.metadata{
              labels: Object.metadata.labels{
                foo: "bar"
              }
            }
          }
raw_log:
The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's what it does and why each choice was made:

**Key design decisions:**

- `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` — the stable v1 API introduced in Kyverno 1.17+.
- `kind: MutatingPolicy` — cluster-scoped, applies across all namespaces.
- `resources: ['pods', 'services']` — both resource types covered in a single rule.
- `operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']` — label is enforced on both creation and updates.
- `patchType: ApplyConfiguration` — uses a **merge/apply** strategy. The `Object{...}` CEL expression merges into the existing object, so:
  - If `foo` label is **absent**, it is added with value `"bar"`.
  - If `foo` label is **already present**, the existing value is preserved (apply semantics don't overwrite existing keys with a merge patch).

description: generates a NetworkPolicy named 'default-deny' in any newly-created Namespace. The generated NetworkPolicy must select all Pods (empty podSelector), include both 'Ingress' and 'Egress' policyTypes, and define no ingress or egress rules so all traffic is denied by default.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.17+ GeneratingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that generates a NetworkPolicy named 'default-deny' in any newly-created Namespace. The generated NetworkPolicy must select all Pods (empty podSelector), include both 'Ingress' and 'Egress' policyTypes, and define no ingress or egress rules so all traffic is denied by default. Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/gen_create_networkpolicy.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: GeneratingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-networkpolicy
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Network Policy
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: NetworkPolicy
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      By default, Kubernetes allows communications across all Pods within a
      cluster. The NetworkPolicy resource and a CNI plug-in that supports
      NetworkPolicy must be used to restrict communications. A default
      NetworkPolicy should be configured for each Namespace to default deny all
      ingress and egress traffic to the Pods in the Namespace. Application teams
      can then configure additional NetworkPolicy resources to allow desired
      traffic to application Pods from select sources. This policy will create a
      new NetworkPolicy resource named `default-deny` which will deny all traffic
      anytime a new Namespace is created.
spec:
  evaluation:
    synchronize:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - namespaces
  variables:
    - name: targetNs
      expression: "object.metadata.name"
    - name: downstream
      expression: |-
        [
          {
            "kind": dyn("NetworkPolicy"),
            "apiVersion": dyn("networking.k8s.io/v1"),
            "metadata": dyn({
              "name": "default-deny"
            }),
            "spec": dyn({
              "podSelector": dyn({}),
              "policyTypes": dyn(["Ingress", "Egress"])
            })
          }
        ]
  generate:
    - expression: "generator.Apply(variables.targetNs, variables.downstream)"
raw_log:
The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what it does and the key design decisions:

**Policy structure:**

```1:56:workspace/output/converted.yaml
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: GeneratingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-networkpolicy
  ...
spec:
  evaluation:
    synchronize:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]
        resources: [namespaces]
  variables:
    - name: targetNs
      expression: "object.metadata.name"
    - name: downstream
      expression: |-
        [{ "kind": dyn("NetworkPolicy"), ... }]
  generate:
    - expression: "generator.Apply(variables.targetNs, variables.downstream)"
```

Key details:

- **`apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`** — the stable v1 API introduced in Kyverno 1.17+
- **`kind: GeneratingPolicy`** — cluster-scoped, applies across all namespaces
- **Trigger**: any Namespace `CREATE` or `UPDATE` event
- **`synchronize: enabled: true`** — keeps the generated NetworkPolicy in sync with the policy; if the downstream resource is deleted, it is recreated
- **`podSelector: {}`** — selects all Pods in the namespace
- **`policyTypes: ["Ingress", "Egress"]`** — both directions listed, with no `ingress` or `egress` rules defined, which causes Kubernetes to deny all traffic by default
- **CEL `dyn()`** — required to wrap dynamically typed values in CEL expressions within `GeneratingPolicy`

prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs whose containers, initContainers, or ephemeralContainers add any Linux capability via securityContext.capabilities.add beyond the Pod Security Standards baseline allow-list (AUDIT_WRITE, CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, FOWNER, FSETID, KILL, MKNOD, NET_BIND_SERVICE, SETFCAP, SETGID, SETPCAP, SETUID, SYS_CHROOT). Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/gen_disallow_capabilities.yaml.

schema_errors: validations[0]: messageExpression contains invalid string conversion of map/list
yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Capabilities Beyond PSS Baseline
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.30+"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod,Deployment,CronJob
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Adding capabilities beyond the Pod Security Standards baseline allow-list
      (AUDIT_WRITE, CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, FOWNER, FSETID, KILL, MKNOD,
      NET_BIND_SERVICE, SETFCAP, SETGID, SETPCAP, SETUID, SYS_CHROOT) is
      disallowed. This policy checks containers, initContainers, and
      ephemeralContainers in Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  # autogen generates matching rules for Deployments and CronJobs automatically.
  autogen:
    podControllers:
      controllers:
        - deployments
        - cronjobs
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  variables:
    - name: allowedCapabilities
      expression: >-
        [
          'AUDIT_WRITE', 'CHOWN', 'DAC_OVERRIDE', 'FOWNER', 'FSETID',
          'KILL', 'MKNOD', 'NET_BIND_SERVICE', 'SETFCAP', 'SETGID',
          'SETPCAP', 'SETUID', 'SYS_CHROOT'
        ]
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          container.?securityContext.?capabilities.?add.orValue([]).all(capability,
            capability == '' || capability in variables.allowedCapabilities
          )
        )
      messageExpression: >-
        "One or more containers add Linux capabilities beyond the PSS baseline "
        + "allow-list. Disallowed capabilities found: ["
        + variables.allContainers
            .filter(container,
              container.?securityContext.?capabilities.?add.orValue([]).exists(capability,
                capability != '' && !(capability in variables.allowedCapabilities)
              )
            )
            .map(container,
              container.name + ": ["
              + container.?securityContext.?capabilities.?add.orValue([])
                  .filter(capability, capability != '' && !(capability in variables.allowedCapabilities))
                  .join(", ")
              + "]"
            )
            .join("; ")
        + "]. Allowed capabilities: AUDIT_WRITE, CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, FOWNER, "
        + "FSETID, KILL, MKNOD, NET_BIND_SERVICE, SETFCAP, SETGID, SETPCAP, SETUID, SYS_CHROOT."
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Capabilities Beyond PSS Baseline
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.30+"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod,Deployment,CronJob
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Adding capabilities beyond the Pod Security Standards baseline allow-list
      (AUDIT_WRITE, CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, FOWNER, FSETID, KILL, MKNOD,
      NET_BIND_SERVICE, SETFCAP, SETGID, SETPCAP, SETUID, SYS_CHROOT) is
      disallowed. This policy checks containers, initContainers, and
      ephemeralContainers in Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  # autogen generates matching rules for Deployments and CronJobs automatically.
  autogen:
    podControllers:
      controllers:
        - deployments
        - cronjobs
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  variables:
    - name: allowedCapabilities
      expression: >-
        [
          'AUDIT_WRITE', 'CHOWN', 'DAC_OVERRIDE', 'FOWNER', 'FSETID',
          'KILL', 'MKNOD', 'NET_BIND_SERVICE', 'SETFCAP', 'SETGID',
          'SETPCAP', 'SETUID', 'SYS_CHROOT'
        ]
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          container.?securityContext.?capabilities.?add.orValue([]).all(capability,
            capability == '' || capability in variables.allowedCapabilities
          )
        )
      messageExpression: >-
        "One or more containers add Linux capabilities beyond the PSS baseline "
        + "allow-list. Disallowed capabilities found: ["
        + variables.allContainers
            .filter(container,
              container.?securityContext.?capabilities.?add.orValue([]).exists(capability,
                capability != '' && !(capability in variables.allowedCapabilities)
              )
            )
            .map(container,
              container.name + ": ["
              + container.?securityContext.?capabilities.?add.orValue([])
                  .filter(capability, capability != '' && !(capability in variables.allowedCapabilities))
                  .join(", ")
              + "]"
            )
            .join("; ")
        + "]. Allowed capabilities: AUDIT_WRITE, CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, FOWNER, "
        + "FSETID, KILL, MKNOD, NET_BIND_SERVICE, SETFCAP, SETGID, SETPCAP, SETUID, SYS_CHROOT."
raw_log:
The policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what it does and why each part was chosen:

---

**Policy: `disallow-capabilities`**

**`apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`** — The stable API version introduced in Kyverno 1.17+, as required.

**`validationActions: [Deny]`** — Hard-deny at admission time rather than just audit.

**`spec.autogen.podControllers`** — Kyverno's auto-generation feature automatically synthesizes additional `matchConstraints` rules for `deployments` and `cronjobs`, avoiding the need to manually duplicate the Pod-level logic for `spec.template.spec.containers`. The Pod rule you write is the source of truth; Kyverno injects the controller wrappers.

**`evaluation.background.enabled: true`** — Enables periodic background scans so existing resources are also reported, not only resources submitted during admission.

**`variables.allowedCapabilities`** — A CEL list of the 13 PSS baseline-allowed capabilities, making the allowed set easy to audit and update in one place.

**`variables.allContainers`** — Concatenates `containers`, `initContainers`, and `ephemeralContainers` into one list using CEL's optional chaining (`?`) and `.orValue([])` so missing fields don't cause evaluation errors.

**`validations[0].expression`** — Asserts that every capability in every container's `securityContext.capabilities.add` list is either empty or in the allow-list. Optional chaining ensures absent `securityContext`/`capabilities`/`add` fields are treated as empty lists (pass).

**`messageExpression`** — Builds a dynamic denial message naming the offending container(s) and the specific disallowed capabilities, making remediation straightforward.

yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-host-namespaces
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Host Namespaces
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod, Deployment, CronJob
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Host namespaces (Process ID namespace, Inter-Process Communication
      namespace, and network namespace) allow access to shared information and
      can be used to elevate privileges. Pods should not be allowed access to
      host namespaces. This policy ensures fields which make use of these host
      namespaces are unset or set to `false` on Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
      - apiGroups:
          - apps
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - deployments
      - apiGroups:
          - batch
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - cronjobs
  variables:
    # For Pods: spec is at object.spec
    # For Deployments: pod spec is at object.spec.template.spec
    # For CronJobs: pod spec is at object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec
    - name: podSpec
      expression: >-
        has(object.spec) && has(object.spec.template)
          ? (has(object.spec.jobTemplate)
              ? object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec
              : object.spec.template.spec)
          : object.spec
  validations:
    - expression: "!variables.podSpec.?hostNetwork.orValue(false)"
      message: >-
        Sharing the host network namespace is disallowed.
        spec.hostNetwork must be unset or set to false.
    - expression: "!variables.podSpec.?hostPID.orValue(false)"
      message: >-
        Sharing the host PID namespace is disallowed.
        spec.hostPID must be unset or set to false.
    - expression: "!variables.podSpec.?hostIPC.orValue(false)"
      message: >-
        Sharing the host IPC namespace is disallowed.
        spec.hostIPC must be unset or set to false.

The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of key design decisions:

**Structure:** Uses `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` (Kyverno 1.17+) with `kind: ValidatingPolicy` and `validationActions: [Deny]` for hard enforcement.

**Resource matching:** Three explicit `resourceRules` entries cover:
- `""` / `v1` / `pods`
- `apps` / `v1` / `deployments`
- `batch` / `v1` / `cronjobs`

**Pod spec resolution via a `variables` entry:** Since the pod spec lives at different paths depending on the resource kind, a single `podSpec` variable uses a CEL conditional to resolve the correct location:
- Pod → `object.spec`
- Deployment → `object.spec.template.spec`
- CronJob → `object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec`

**Three separate validations** — one per host namespace field (`hostNetwork`, `hostPID`, `hostIPC`) — each using `.?field.orValue(false)` (the CEL optional-chaining idiom) so that an unset field is treated as `false` and passes, while an explicitly `true` value triggers a Deny with a targeted message.

The policy is failing to block CronJob resources that use host namespaces, even though it correctly blocks the same violations in Pods and Deployments. This indicates the policy's validation rules do not account for the CronJob resource type, which wraps pod specifications in a nested jobTemplate structure that the policy is not inspecting.

description: denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs that use any hostPath volume. The check must reject any volume in spec.volumes whose hostPath field is set, regardless of the hostPath value.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs that use any hostPath volume. The check must reject any volume in spec.volumes whose hostPath field is set, regardless of the hostPath value. Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/gen_disallow_host_path.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-host-path
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow hostPath Volumes
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod,Deployment,CronJob,Volume
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      HostPath volumes allow Pods to mount host directories directly into
      containers, which can be used to access sensitive host data or escalate
      privileges. This policy denies any Pod, Deployment, or CronJob that
      declares a volume with the hostPath field set, regardless of the path
      value.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
      - apiGroups:
          - apps
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - deployments
      - apiGroups:
          - batch
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - cronjobs
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.volumes) ||
        object.spec.volumes.all(volume, !has(volume.hostPath))
      message: >-
        HostPath volumes are forbidden. The field spec.volumes[*].hostPath must
        not be set.
    - expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.template) ||
        !has(object.spec.template.spec) ||
        !has(object.spec.template.spec.volumes) ||
        object.spec.template.spec.volumes.all(volume, !has(volume.hostPath))
      message: >-
        HostPath volumes are forbidden. The field
        spec.template.spec.volumes[*].hostPath must not be set.
    - expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.jobTemplate) ||
        !has(object.spec.jobTemplate.spec) ||
        !has(object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template) ||
        !has(object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec) ||
        !has(object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.volumes) ||
        object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.volumes.all(volume, !has(volume.hostPath))
      message: >-
        HostPath volumes are forbidden. The field
        spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.volumes[*].hostPath must not be set.
raw_log:
The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the design decisions:

**Structure:**
- `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` with `kind: ValidatingPolicy` as required
- `validationActions: [Deny]` to block (not just audit) non-compliant resources

**Coverage — three `resourceRules` entries:**
| Resource | API Group | Volume path in spec |
|---|---|---|
| `pods` | `""` (core) | `spec.volumes[*]` |
| `deployments` | `apps` | `spec.template.spec.volumes[*]` |
| `cronjobs` | `batch` | `spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.volumes[*]` |

**Three CEL validation expressions** — each checks the relevant volume path for its resource kind. All three apply to every matched resource; the `!has(...)` guards make each expression vacuously pass for resource kinds that don't have that path (e.g., the CronJob expression trivially passes for a Pod since it has no `spec.jobTemplate`).

**The core check** in each expression is `volumes.all(volume, !has(volume.hostPath))` — it iterates all volumes and rejects any that have the `hostPath` field set, regardless of the path value, matching the requirement exactly.

description: denies any Pod, Deployment, or CronJob whose containers, initContainers, or ephemeralContainers set securityContext.privileged to true.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that denies any Pod, Deployment, or CronJob whose containers, initContainers, or ephemeralContainers set securityContext.privileged to true. Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/gen_disallow_privileged.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-privileged-containers
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Privileged Containers
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: high
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Privileged containers have access to all Linux kernel capabilities and devices,
      effectively running as root on the host. This policy denies any Pod, Deployment,
      or CronJob whose containers, initContainers, or ephemeralContainers set
      securityContext.privileged to true.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
      - apiGroups: ["apps"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["deployments"]
      - apiGroups: ["batch"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["cronjobs"]
  variables:
    - name: podSpec
      expression: >-
        (object.kind == "Pod") ? object.spec :
        (object.kind == "Deployment") ? object.spec.template.spec :
        (object.kind == "CronJob") ? object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec :
        null
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        variables.podSpec.containers
        + variables.podSpec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + variables.podSpec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(c,
          !has(c.securityContext) ||
          !has(c.securityContext.privileged) ||
          c.securityContext.privileged == false
        )
      messageExpression: >-
        "Privileged containers are not allowed. The following containers have securityContext.privileged=true: " +
        variables.allContainers.filter(c,
          has(c.securityContext) &&
          has(c.securityContext.privileged) &&
          c.securityContext.privileged == true
        ).map(c, c.name).join(", ")
raw_log:
The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what it does:

**Policy: `disallow-privileged-containers`**

- **`apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`** — uses the Kyverno 1.17+ `ValidatingPolicy` type
- **`validationActions: [Deny]`** — blocks admission on violation
- **Matches** `CREATE`/`UPDATE` operations on `pods` (core/v1), `deployments` (apps/v1), and `cronjobs` (batch/v1)

**Key design decisions:**

1. **Single policy, three resource kinds** — A `variables.podSpec` variable normalizes the spec path across all three resource kinds (`object.spec` for Pods, `object.spec.template.spec` for Deployments, `object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec` for CronJobs) using CEL conditional expressions.

2. **All container types covered** — A `variables.allContainers` variable concatenates `containers`, `initContainers`, and `ephemeralContainers` (the latter two using `.orValue([])` since they are optional fields), following the same pattern shown in the official CEL libraries documentation.

3. **Safe field access** — The validation uses `has()` guards before accessing `securityContext` and `securityContext.privileged` to avoid errors on containers that omit these fields entirely.

4. **Dynamic error message** — `messageExpression` reports exactly which container names triggered the violation.

description: requires all Pods to have the label 'app.kubernetes.io/name' set to a non-empty value. Deny admission if the label is missing or empty.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that requires all Pods to have the label 'app.kubernetes.io/name' set to a non-empty value. Deny admission if the label is missing or empty. Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/gen_require_labels.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-app-name-label
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require app.kubernetes.io/name Label
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Requires all Pods to have the 'app.kubernetes.io/name' label set to a
      non-empty value. Admission is denied if the label is missing or empty.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        has(object.metadata.labels) &&
        'app.kubernetes.io/name' in object.metadata.labels &&
        size(object.metadata.labels['app.kubernetes.io/name']) > 0
      messageExpression: >-
        !has(object.metadata.labels) || !('app.kubernetes.io/name' in object.metadata.labels)
          ? "Pod '" + object.metadata.name + "' is missing required label 'app.kubernetes.io/name'"
          : "Pod '" + object.metadata.name + "' has an empty value for required label 'app.kubernetes.io/name'"
      message: "Pod must have the label 'app.kubernetes.io/name' set to a non-empty value"

description: requires every container in a Pod to set spec.containers[].resources.requests.memory, spec.containers[].resources.requests.cpu, and spec.containers[].resources.limits.memory. Deny admission if any container is missing any of these three fields. CPU limit is NOT required.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that requires every container in a Pod to set spec.containers[].resources.requests.memory, spec.containers[].resources.requests.cpu, and spec.containers[].resources.limits.memory. Deny admission if any container is missing any of these three fields. CPU limit is NOT required. Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/gen_require_resource_limits.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-container-resources
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Container Resource Requests and Memory Limit
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Every container must set resources.requests.cpu, resources.requests.memory,
      and resources.limits.memory. A missing CPU limit is permitted. Admission is
      denied if any container omits any of the three required fields.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['pods']
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(c,
          has(c.resources) &&
          has(c.resources.requests) &&
          has(c.resources.requests.cpu)
        )
      messageExpression: >-
        "Container '" +
        variables.allContainers.filter(c,
          !(has(c.resources) &&
            has(c.resources.requests) &&
            has(c.resources.requests.cpu))
        )[0].name +
        "' is missing resources.requests.cpu"

    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(c,
          has(c.resources) &&
          has(c.resources.requests) &&
          has(c.resources.requests.memory)
        )
      messageExpression: >-
        "Container '" +
        variables.allContainers.filter(c,
          !(has(c.resources) &&
            has(c.resources.requests) &&
            has(c.resources.requests.memory))
        )[0].name +
        "' is missing resources.requests.memory"

    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(c,
          has(c.resources) &&
          has(c.resources.limits) &&
          has(c.resources.limits.memory)
        )
      messageExpression: >-
        "Container '" +
        variables.allContainers.filter(c,
          !(has(c.resources) &&
            has(c.resources.limits) &&
            has(c.resources.limits.memory))
        )[0].name +
        "' is missing resources.limits.memory"

- **`apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`** — uses the Kyverno 1.17+ `ValidatingPolicy` type (not the older `kyverno.io/v1` ClusterPolicy).
- **`validationActions: [Deny]`** — blocks admission on violation.
- **Matches** all `CREATE` and `UPDATE` operations on `v1/pods`.
- **`variables.allContainers`** collects `spec.containers`, `spec.initContainers`, and `spec.ephemeralContainers` into a single list using the `?`-optional-field / `orValue([])` CEL pattern from the Kyverno CEL library, so init and ephemeral containers are also covered.

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-image-registries
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Image Registries
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images from unknown or public registries can be of dubious quality and
      may not be scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk.
      This policy denies any Pod whose containers (including init and ephemeral
      containers) use images that do not originate from 'eu.foo.io/' or 'bar.io/'.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers +
        object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([]) +
        object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          container.image.startsWith('eu.foo.io/') ||
          container.image.startsWith('bar.io/')
        )
      message: >-
        All container images must originate from an approved registry
        ('eu.foo.io/' or 'bar.io/'). Unknown registries are not permitted.
