The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what it does and why each field was chosen:

**API / Kind**: `policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `ValidatingPolicy` — the stable (GA) CEL-based policy type promoted in Kyverno 1.17.

**`validationActions: [Deny]`** — rejects the admission request outright for any non-conforming Pod (rather than just auditing).

**`matchConstraints`** — targets `pods` in the core API group on `CREATE` and `UPDATE` operations.

**`variables.allContainers`** — collects all three container types (regular, init, and ephemeral) in a single list using the `?field.orValue([])` optional-field CEL pattern, so no containers are missed.

**`validations[0].expression`** — uses CEL's `.all()` macro to assert that every container's image starts with either `eu.foo.io/` or `bar.io/`. If any container fails this check, the policy returns the descriptive `message` and the Pod is denied.

description: validates that the Principal in S3 bucket IAM policies is not set to '*', which would allow any principal access to the resource.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1) that evaluates Terraform plan JSON files (object.planned_values.root_module.resources contains the planned resources). The policy must set evaluation.mode: JSON, match all resource types (apiGroups: ["*"], resources: ["*"]), and use matchConditions to identify Terraform plans by checking has(object.planned_values) && has(object.terraform_version). The policy validates that the Principal in S3 bucket IAM policies is not set to '*', which would allow any principal access to the resource. Write the policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/tf_s3_no_wildcard_principal.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: s3-no-wildcard-principal
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: S3 Bucket Policy No Wildcard Principal
    policies.kyverno.io/category: AWS S3 Security Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: high
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      S3 bucket IAM policies must not grant access to any principal by setting
      Principal to '*'. A wildcard principal allows any entity, including
      anonymous users, to perform the allowed actions on the bucket, which
      creates a significant security risk. This policy validates that no
      aws_s3_bucket_policy resource in a Terraform plan contains an IAM policy
      statement whose Principal (or Principal.AWS) is set to '*'.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  evaluation:
    mode: JSON
    background:
      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ["*"]
        apiVersions: ["*"]
        resources: ["*"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
  matchConditions:
    - name: is-terraform-plan
      expression: "has(object.planned_values) && has(object.terraform_version)"
    - name: has-s3-bucket-policy
      expression: >-
        has(object.planned_values) &&
        has(object.planned_values.root_module) &&
        has(object.planned_values.root_module.resources) &&
        object.planned_values.root_module.resources.exists(r,
          r.type == 'aws_s3_bucket_policy'
        )
  variables:
    - name: bucketPolicies
      expression: >-
        object.planned_values.root_module.resources.filter(r,
          r.type == 'aws_s3_bucket_policy'
        )
  validations:
    - message: >-
        S3 bucket policies must not set Principal to '*'. A wildcard principal
        grants unrestricted access to any entity including anonymous users.
      expression: |
        variables.bucketPolicies.all(bucketPolicy,
          !has(bucketPolicy.values) ||
          !has(bucketPolicy.values.policy) ||
          (
            json.unmarshal(bucketPolicy.values.policy).Statement.all(stmt,
              (
                // Principal is a plain string wildcard
                !(type(stmt.Principal) == string && stmt.Principal == '*')
              ) && (
                // Principal is an object with AWS key set to '*' as a string
                !(type(stmt.Principal) == map &&
                  has(stmt.Principal.AWS) &&
                  type(stmt.Principal.AWS) == string &&
                  stmt.Principal.AWS == '*')
              ) && (
                // Principal is an object with AWS key as a list containing '*'
                !(type(stmt.Principal) == map &&
                  has(stmt.Principal.AWS) &&
                  type(stmt.Principal.AWS) == list &&
                  stmt.Principal.AWS.exists(p, p == '*'))
              )
            )
          )
        )

raw_log:
The policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what it does and the key design decisions:

---

**Policy: `s3-no-wildcard-principal`**

**Structure overview:**

- `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1` / `kind: ValidatingPolicy` — Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy type
- `evaluation.mode: JSON` — processes non-Kubernetes payloads (Terraform plan JSON)
- `matchConstraints` — `apiGroups: ["*"]`, `resources: ["*"]` to match all resource types as required
- `matchConditions` — two guards:
  1. `is-terraform-plan`: `has(object.planned_values) && has(object.terraform_version)` identifies Terraform plan files
  2. `has-s3-bucket-policy`: only activates when at least one `aws_s3_bucket_policy` resource is present in the plan
- `variables.bucketPolicies` — pre-filters `aws_s3_bucket_policy` resources for reuse in the validation expression
- `validations` — uses `json.unmarshal()` (from Kyverno's extended CEL library) to parse the IAM policy JSON string stored in `values.policy`, then walks every `Statement` and rejects any where `Principal` is `"*"` as a bare string, `Principal.AWS` is `"*"` as a string, or `Principal.AWS` is a list that contains `"*"`

The use of `json.unmarshal()` rather than string `.contains()` makes the check structurally sound and immune to JSON key-ordering or whitespace variations in the serialized policy document.
error_summary: The policy tried to use a JSON parsing function (`json.unmarshal`) that doesn't exist in Kyverno's CEL expression language. Kyverno doesn't provide built-in JSON unmarshaling capabilities within its policy rules, so the expression fails when it attempts to parse the S3 bucket policy string into an object structure.

description: generate one Chainsaw test set with exactly two scenarios for Kyverno Helm install; scenario A must pass with policy smoke test and scenario B must fail with invalid image/resource pressure as expected
prompt:
You are generating Kubernetes end-to-end tests using Kyverno Chainsaw.

Create a clean test setup with exactly two scenarios to verify Kyverno Helm deployment.

Source policy path: /Users/shutingzhao/go/src/github.com/nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_resource_limits.yaml
Output directory: /Users/shutingzhao/go/src/github.com/nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/ch_kyverno_helm_install
(The source policy is copied into the output directory as policy.yaml.)

Goals:
- Scenario A (pass): Helm install succeeds and Kyverno becomes healthy.
- Scenario A must also include a policy enforcement smoke test after install (one denied and one allowed resource) and it must pass.
- Scenario B (fail): Helm install is intentionally broken by invalid image override and must fail readiness.
- Scenario B must also include a resource pressure failure (unschedulable Kyverno pods via impossible CPU/memory requests/limits) and it must fail as expected.

Requirements:
- Generate all required Chainsaw assets for both scenarios.
- Use separate namespaces and release names:
  - pass namespace: kyverno-pass
  - fail namespace: kyverno-fail
  - pass release: kyverno-pass
  - fail release: kyverno-fail
- Use Helm chart kyverno/kyverno.
- For pass scenario, use valid values and wait for readiness.
- For pass scenario, apply a simple Kyverno validating policy and verify enforcement behavior (deny bad resource, allow good resource).
- For fail scenario, override image repository/tag to an invalid value so pods hit image pull errors.
- For fail scenario, include a resource-pressure variant with unrealistic resource settings that leads to Pending/Unschedulable pods.
- For EVERY fail-scenario Helm install/upgrade command, include `--skip-crds` so cluster-scoped Kyverno CRDs are never re-owned by the fail release.
What to generate:
- A Chainsaw test suite for pass scenario.
- A Chainsaw test suite for fail scenario.
- Any values files, manifests, and helper scripts needed to run both tests.
- A simple runner script that executes both Chainsaw tests and prints clear PASS/FAIL summary.
- Ensure /Users/shutingzhao/go/src/github.com/nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/ch_kyverno_helm_install/chainsaw-test.yaml exists as the primary test entry file.

File/path consistency rules (REQUIRED):
- In chainsaw-test.yaml scripts, reference generated assets using paths relative to the test dir (for example `./policy.yaml`, `./pass/values-pass.yaml`, `./pass/manifests/bad-pod.yaml`).
- Do NOT hardcode `/workspace/output` (or any absolute path) in Helm/Kubectl commands.
- Every referenced file path must exist in the generated output tree with the exact same filename.

Assertions required:
- Pass scenario assertions:
  - Helm release status is deployed.
  - Kyverno controller pod(s) are Ready.
  - No CrashLoopBackOff or ImagePullBackOff in kyverno-pass namespace.
  - Policy enforcement smoke test passes (bad resource denied, good resource allowed).
- Fail scenario assertions:
  - Helm install or rollout readiness fails as expected.
  - At least one Kyverno pod in kyverno-fail shows ErrImagePull or ImagePullBackOff.
  - At least one Kyverno pod in kyverno-fail is Pending with Unschedulable events caused by impossible resource settings.
  - Failure is marked as expected behavior, not an unexpected infra failure.

Output format:
- First, show the test matrix with the two scenarios and expected outcomes.
- Then provide generated file contents.
- Then provide exact commands to run:
  - helm repo add/update
  - chainsaw test commands
- Finally, provide expected output snippets for both PASS and expected FAIL.

Important:
- Keep everything reproducible on a local cluster.
- Do not add extra scenarios.
- Do not leave placeholders. Fill concrete names and values.
- Both Tests share cluster-scoped Kyverno CRDs and webhooks. They MUST run serially.
  Set `spec.concurrent: false` on every Test in chainsaw-test.yaml.
- The pass scenario installs Kyverno via Helm, which creates cluster-scoped
  resources (CRDs in `*.kyverno.io` and `*.wgpolicyk8s.io`, webhooks labeled
  `webhook.kyverno.io/managed-by=kyverno`) that `helm uninstall` does NOT
  remove. If they survive into the fail scenario, the second `helm install`
  will fail with `invalid ownership metadata: ... must equal "<fail-release>":
  current value is "<pass-release>"`.
  The pass scenario's final cleanup step MUST therefore (in this order):
    1. `helm uninstall <pass-release> -n <pass-ns>`
    2. `kubectl delete ns <pass-ns> kyverno-test --ignore-not-found`
    3. `kubectl delete validatingwebhookconfigurations,mutatingwebhookconfigurations \
         -l webhook.kyverno.io/managed-by=kyverno --ignore-not-found`
    4. `kubectl get crds -o name | grep -E '\.(kyverno\.io|wgpolicyk8s\.io)$' \
         | xargs -r kubectl delete --ignore-not-found --timeout=60s`
  Put this in the pass Test's last step (use `finally:` so it runs even on
  failure) with `timeouts: { exec: 4m, cleanup: 4m, delete: 2m }`.
- After pass cleanup, the fail scenario must be resilient to partial cleanup of
  cluster-scoped artifacts. Therefore fail-scenario Helm commands MUST include
  `--skip-crds` (required even if you already delete CRDs in pass cleanup).
- Use deployment label selectors, NOT guessed names, when waiting for
  Kyverno controllers. The Kyverno chart names deployments
  `kyverno-admission-controller` (not `<release>-admission-controller`).
  Prefer:
    kubectl wait --for=condition=Available deployment \
      -l app.kubernetes.io/component=admission-controller \
      -n <ns> --timeout=3m
- Chainsaw default timeouts are aggressive (ApplyTimeout 5s, AssertTimeout 30s,
  CleanupTimeout 30s, ExecTimeout 5s). Long-running scripts (helm install/uninstall,
  kubectl wait, polling loops) WILL be killed unless you override timeouts.
  Required overrides:
    - On any step containing a `helm install`/`helm upgrade`: set
        timeouts:
          exec: 6m
    - On any cleanup block (or step doing `helm uninstall` + ns deletes): set
        timeouts:
          cleanup: 4m
          exec: 4m
          delete: 2m
    - For polling loops or `kubectl wait` scripts longer than 5s, set
        timeouts:
          exec: 4m
  Per-step `timeouts:` is a sibling of `try:`, not nested under it.

Chainsaw schema rules (Chainsaw v0.2.x — STRICT, violating these causes parse errors):
- A Test manifest has only these top-level spec fields: `description`, `concurrent`, `skip`, `skipDelete`, `template`, `timeouts`, `cluster`, `clusters`, `namespace`, `bindings`, `catch`, `steps`. NOTHING ELSE.
- DO NOT put `cleanup:` at `spec` level. There is no `spec.cleanup`. Cleanup must be expressed as either:
    (a) `cleanup:` block INSIDE a `spec.steps[]` entry (runs after that step), or
    (b) a final dedicated step whose `try:` actions delete the resources, or
    (c) rely on Chainsaw's automatic per-test resource cleanup (default behavior).
- Inside a step, valid keys are: `name`, `description`, `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `cleanup`, `bindings`, `timeouts`, `skipDelete`, `cluster`, `clusters`, `template`, `use`. NOTHING ELSE. IMPORTANT: `continueOnError` is NOT valid at step level — it belongs on individual try/catch action items.
- Inside `try`/`catch`/`finally`/`cleanup` action items, valid operations/fields are: `apply`, `assert`, `error`, `command`, `script`, `delete`, `create`, `update`, `patch`, `get`, `describe`, `events`, `logs`, `podLogs`, `sleep`, `wait`, `proxy`, `description`, `continueOnError`. Use ONLY these names.
- `continueOnError: true` is a SIBLING of the operation (e.g. sibling of `script:`), NOT nested inside the operation. Example:
    try:
    - continueOnError: true
      script:
        content: |
          helm install ...
- For `delete:`, use `ref:` (with apiVersion/kind/name/namespace), NOT `resource:`.
- For `describe:`, the operation takes `apiVersion`, `kind`, `name`, `namespace`, `selector`, `showEvents` — these are direct fields on `describe:`, not nested under another key.
- Do not invent fields. When in doubt, omit.

Validation:
- Generate all files as fully specified above.
- Ensure /Users/shutingzhao/go/src/github.com/nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/ch_kyverno_helm_install/chainsaw-test.yaml is valid Chainsaw YAML (check syntax).
- The generated files will be validated separately via chainsaw test CLI.
- Focus on correctness of the manifest content, not on runtime execution.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ Chainsaw examples before writing the test:
- https://kyverno.github.io/chainsaw/latest/
- https://github.com/kyverno/chainsaw
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-requests-limits
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Limits and Requests
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      As application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources
      requested and consumed by each Pod. It is recommended to require resource requests and
      limits per Pod, especially for memory and CPU. If a Namespace level request or limit is specified,
      defaults will automatically be applied to each Pod based on the LimitRange configuration.
      This policy validates that all containers have something specified for memory and CPU
      requests and memory limits.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-resources
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "CPU and memory resource requests and memory limits are required for containers."
      pattern:
        spec:
          containers:
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
          =(initContainers):
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
          =(ephemeralContainers):
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
output_yaml:
# =============================================================================
# Kyverno Helm Deployment — Chainsaw Test Suite
# Scenarios:
#   A (pass) : Helm install succeeds → Kyverno healthy → policy smoke test
#   B (fail) : Helm install with bad image → ErrImagePull/ImagePullBackOff
#              + resource-pressure install → Pending/Unschedulable pods
#
# Both tests share cluster-scoped CRDs/webhooks and MUST run serially.
# Run from the directory that contains this file (i.e. ./).
# =============================================================================

---
# =============================================================================
# Scenario A — PASS
# =============================================================================
apiVersion: chainsaw.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: Test
metadata:
  name: kyverno-helm-pass
spec:
  description: "Scenario A: Helm install succeeds, Kyverno becomes healthy, policy smoke test passes."
  concurrent: false

  steps:

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # Step 1 — Create namespace and install Kyverno via Helm
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  - name: helm-install-pass
    description: "Create kyverno-pass namespace and install Kyverno with valid values."
    timeouts:
      exec: 6m
    try:
    - script:
        content: |
          set -e
          kubectl create namespace kyverno-pass --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
          helm install kyverno-pass kyverno/kyverno \
            --namespace kyverno-pass \
            --values ./pass/values-pass.yaml \
            --wait=false \
            --timeout 5m
    catch:
    - describe:
        apiVersion: v1
        kind: Pod
        namespace: kyverno-pass
        showEvents: true
    - events:
        namespace: kyverno-pass

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # Step 2 — Wait for Kyverno admission controller to be Available
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  - name: wait-kyverno-ready
    description: "Wait up to 5m for Kyverno admission-controller Deployment to be Available."
    timeouts:
      exec: 6m
    try:
    - script:
        content: |
          set -e
          kubectl wait --for=condition=Available deployment \
            -l app.kubernetes.io/component=admission-controller \
            -n kyverno-pass \
            --timeout=5m
          kubectl wait --for=condition=Available deployment \
            -l app.kubernetes.io/component=background-controller \
            -n kyverno-pass \
            --timeout=5m
          kubectl wait --for=condition=Available deployment \
            -l app.kubernetes.io/component=cleanup-controller \
            -n kyverno-pass \
            --timeout=5m
          kubectl wait --for=condition=Available deployment \
            -l app.kubernetes.io/component=reports-controller \
            -n kyverno-pass \
            --timeout=5m
    catch:
    - podLogs:
        namespace: kyverno-pass
        selector: "app.kubernetes.io/instance=kyverno-pass"
        tail: 50
    - describe:
        apiVersion: apps/v1
        kind: Deployment
        namespace: kyverno-pass
        showEvents: true

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # Step 3 — Assert Helm release is deployed and pods are healthy
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  - name: assert-helm-release-deployed
    description: "Assert Helm release status=deployed and no bad pod states in kyverno-pass."
    timeouts:
      exec: 4m
    try:
    - script:
        content: |
          set -e
          STATUS=$(helm status kyverno-pass -n kyverno-pass -o json | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['info']['status'])")
          if [ "$STATUS" != "deployed" ]; then
            echo "ERROR: Helm release status is '$STATUS', expected 'deployed'"
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "Helm release status: $STATUS — OK"

          # Check no pod has CrashLoopBackOff or ImagePullBackOff
          BAD=$(kubectl get pods -n kyverno-pass -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{" "}{range .status.containerStatuses[*]}{.state.waiting.reason}{" "}{end}{"\n"}{end}' \
                | grep -E 'CrashLoopBackOff|ImagePullBackOff' || true)
          if [ -n "$BAD" ]; then
            echo "ERROR: Found bad pod state(s): $BAD"
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "No CrashLoopBackOff or ImagePullBackOff found — OK"

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # Step 4 — Apply enforce-mode policy and create smoke-test namespace
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  - name: apply-policy-and-namespace
    description: "Apply enforce-mode ClusterPolicy and create kyverno-test namespace."
    timeouts:
      exec: 4m
    try:
    - script:
        content: |
          set -e
          kubectl create namespace kyverno-test --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
          kubectl apply -f ./pass/manifests/enforce-policy.yaml
          # Wait for policy to be ready (Kyverno marks it Ready)
          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
            READY=$(kubectl get clusterpolicy require-requests-limits \
              -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}' 2>/dev/null || echo "")
            if [ "$READY" = "True" ]; then
              echo "ClusterPolicy is Ready"
              break
            fi
            echo "Waiting for policy to be Ready ($i/30)..."
            sleep 5
          done
    catch:
    - describe:
        apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
        kind: ClusterPolicy
        name: require-requests-limits
        showEvents: true

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # Step 5 — Smoke test: bad pod must be denied
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  - name: smoke-test-deny-bad-pod
    description: "Verify that bad-pod (no resource requests/limits) is denied by Kyverno."
    timeouts:
      exec: 2m
    try:
    - script:
        content: |
          set -e
          # Attempt to create bad-pod; expect rejection (non-zero exit)
          if kubectl apply -f ./pass/manifests/bad-pod.yaml 2>&1; then
            echo "ERROR: bad-pod was NOT denied by Kyverno — policy enforcement is broken"
            exit 1
          else
            echo "PASS: bad-pod was correctly denied by Kyverno"
          fi
    catch:
    - events:
        namespace: kyverno-test

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # Step 6 — Smoke test: good pod must be allowed
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  - name: smoke-test-allow-good-pod
    description: "Verify that good-pod (with resource requests/limits) is allowed by Kyverno."
    timeouts:
      exec: 2m
    try:
    - script:
        content: |
          set -e
          kubectl apply -f ./pass/manifests/good-pod.yaml
          echo "PASS: good-pod was correctly allowed by Kyverno"
    catch:
    - events:
        namespace: kyverno-test
    - podLogs:
        namespace: kyverno-test
        name: good-pod
        tail: 20

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # Step 7 — Cleanup (finally: runs even on failure)
  # Removes pass release + all cluster-scoped Kyverno artifacts so Scenario B
  # can install its own Helm release without ownership conflicts.
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  - name: pass-cleanup
    description: "Uninstall Kyverno pass release and remove all cluster-scoped CRDs/webhooks."
    timeouts:
      exec: 4m
      cleanup: 4m
      delete: 2m
    try:
    - script:
        content: |
          echo "Cleanup step placeholder — real cleanup is in finally block"
    finally:
    - script:
        content: |
          # 1. Uninstall Helm release
          helm uninstall kyverno-pass -n kyverno-pass --ignore-not-found 2>/dev/null || \
            helm uninstall kyverno-pass -n kyverno-pass 2>/dev/null || true

          # 2. Delete namespaces
          kubectl delete ns kyverno-pass kyverno-test --ignore-not-found --timeout=90s || true

          # 3. Delete webhooks managed by Kyverno
          kubectl delete validatingwebhookconfigurations \
            -l webhook.kyverno.io/managed-by=kyverno \
            --ignore-not-found --timeout=60s || true
          kubectl delete mutatingwebhookconfigurations \
            -l webhook.kyverno.io/managed-by=kyverno \
            --ignore-not-found --timeout=60s || true

          # 4. Delete all Kyverno CRDs
          kubectl get crds -o name 2>/dev/null \
            | grep -E '\.(kyverno\.io|wgpolicyk8s\.io)$' \
            | xargs -r kubectl delete --ignore-not-found --timeout=60s || true

          # 5. Delete the ClusterPolicy created for smoke test
          kubectl delete clusterpolicy require-requests-limits --ignore-not-found || true

          echo "Pass cleanup complete"

---
# =============================================================================
# Scenario B — FAIL (expected)
# =============================================================================

kind: Test
metadata:
  name: kyverno-helm-fail
spec:
  description: "Scenario B: Helm install with bad image causes ErrImagePull; resource-pressure causes Unschedulable pods. Both failures are expected."
  concurrent: false

  steps:

  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # Step 1 — Create namespace and install Kyverno with bad image
  # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
  - name: helm-install-bad-image

raw_log:
---

## Test Matrix

| # | Test Name | Expected Outcome | Release | Namespace |
|---|-----------|-----------------|---------|-----------|
| A | `kyverno-helm-pass` | **PASS** — Helm deploys, Kyverno healthy, policy enforcement verified | `kyverno-pass` | `kyverno-pass` |
| B | `kyverno-helm-fail` | **FAIL** (expected) — ErrImagePull + Unschedulable pods confirmed | `kyverno-fail` | `kyverno-fail` |

---

## Generated Files

```
/workspace/output/
├── chainsaw-test.yaml            ← primary entry file (2 Test docs, serial)
├── policy.yaml                   ← source policy (copied from /workspace/policy.yaml)
├── run-tests.sh                  ← runner script with PASS/FAIL summary
├── pass/
│   ├── values-pass.yaml          ← valid Helm values for Scenario A
│   └── manifests/
│       ├── enforce-policy.yaml   ← Enforce-mode ClusterPolicy for smoke test
│       ├── bad-pod.yaml          ← Pod without resources → denied by Kyverno
│       └── good-pod.yaml         ← Pod with resources → allowed by Kyverno
└── fail/
    ├── values-fail.yaml          ← bad image registry → ErrImagePull
    └── values-pressure.yaml      ← 9999 CPU/9999Gi RAM → Unschedulable
```

---

## Commands to Run

**1. Add Helm repo (once per cluster):**
```bash
helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno/
helm repo update
```

**2. Run individual Chainsaw tests:**
```bash
cd /workspace/output

# Scenario A only
chainsaw test --test-file chainsaw-test.yaml --test-name-regex "^kyverno-helm-pass$"

# Scenario B only
chainsaw test --test-file chainsaw-test.yaml --test-name-regex "^kyverno-helm-fail$"

# Both (serially via concurrent: false in each Test)
chainsaw test --test-file chainsaw-test.yaml
```

**3. Run via the helper script (recommended — prints clear summary):**
```bash
cd /workspace/output
chmod +x run-tests.sh
./run-tests.sh
```

---

## Expected Output Snippets

**Scenario A (PASS):**
```
--- kyverno-helm-pass ---
STEP 1 | helm-install-pass          | PASS
STEP 2 | wait-kyverno-ready         | PASS
STEP 3 | assert-helm-release-deployed
  Helm release status: deployed — OK
  No CrashLoopBackOff or ImagePullBackOff found — OK | PASS
STEP 4 | apply-policy-and-namespace
  ClusterPolicy is Ready             | PASS
STEP 5 | smoke-test-deny-bad-pod
  PASS: bad-pod was correctly denied by Kyverno | PASS
STEP 6 | smoke-test-allow-good-pod
  PASS: good-pod was correctly allowed by Kyverno | PASS
STEP 7 | pass-cleanup               | PASS (finally block executed)

✔ Scenario A — Helm install pass + policy smoke test: PASS
```

**Scenario B (expected FAIL, confirmed):**
```
--- kyverno-helm-fail ---
STEP 1 | helm-install-bad-image     | continueOnError=true → continues
STEP 2 | assert-image-pull-failure
  EXPECTED FAILURE CONFIRMED: Found ErrImagePull/ImagePullBackOff in kyverno-fail | PASS
STEP 3 | uninstall-bad-image-release | PASS
STEP 4 | helm-install-resource-pressure | continueOnError=true → continues
STEP 5 | assert-resource-pressure-unschedulable
  EXPECTED FAILURE CONFIRMED: Pending pods found in kyverno-fail
  Unschedulable events confirmed: 0/N nodes can satisfy resource request ... | PASS
STEP 6 | fail-cleanup               | PASS (finally block executed)

✔ Scenario B — Bad image + resource pressure (expected fail): PASS
```

---

## Key Design Decisions

- **`concurrent: false`** on both `Test` specs guarantees serial execution and prevents CRD ownership races.
- **Pass cleanup `finally:` block** (Step 7) runs in this exact order: `helm uninstall` → delete namespaces → delete webhooks by label → delete Kyverno/wgpolicyk8s CRDs — ensuring a clean slate before Scenario B.
- **`--skip-crds`** on every fail-scenario `helm install` prevents the fail release from trying to take ownership of any lingering cluster-scoped CRDs.
- **Deployment label selectors** (`app.kubernetes.io/component=admission-controller`) are used instead of guessed pod names for `kubectl wait`.
- **`continueOnError: true`** on expected-fail Helm installs so Chainsaw doesn't abort the test; the subsequent assertion steps actively confirm the failure state and exit 0 only when the expected failure is observed.
- **All `timeouts:`** at step level (sibling of `try:`), with `exec: 6m` on Helm installs and `exec: 4m` on polling loops, well above Chainsaw's 5 s default.
- **Enforce-mode policy** (`enforce-policy.yaml`) scoped to `kyverno-test` namespace is applied only after Kyverno is ready, avoiding admission webhook races during install.

You MUST write exactly two files directly into /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/tg_cp_disallow_default_namespace — no subdirectories:
1. `/Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/tg_cp_disallow_default_namespace/kyverno-test.yaml` — apiVersion: cli.kyverno.io/v1alpha1, kind: Test, with `policies: [policy.yaml]` (the policy is already copied there as policy.yaml).

IMPORTANT: Both files must exist at exactly those paths when you are done. Do NOT create a kyverno-tests/ subdirectory or any other subdirectory.

CRITICAL: Write resources.yaml FIRST. Then in kyverno-test.yaml, use the EXACT same metadata.name values from resources.yaml in the results[].resources lists. Any name in results that does not match a resource name in resources.yaml will cause test failures.

Requirements:
- Cover both passing cases (resources the policy allows) and failing cases (resources the policy denies or flags).
- For new-style policy kinds (ValidatingPolicy, MutatingPolicy, GeneratingPolicy, DeletingPolicy, NamespacedDeletingPolicy, ImageValidatingPolicy): set `isValidatingPolicy: true` on each result entry and OMIT the `rule:` field.
- For ClusterPolicy: include the `rule:` field matching the exact rule name.
- Each result entry must include: `policy` (the policy metadata.name), `kind`, `resources` (list of resource names from resources.yaml), `result` (pass or fail).

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the test:

input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-default-namespace
  annotations:
    pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers: none
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Default Namespace
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Kubernetes Namespaces are an optional feature that provide a way to segment and
      isolate cluster resources across multiple applications and users. As a best
      practice, workloads should be isolated with Namespaces. Namespaces should be required
      and the default (empty) Namespace should not be used. This policy validates that Pods
      specify a Namespace name other than `default`. Rule auto-generation is disabled here
      due to Pod controllers need to specify the `namespace` field under the top-level `metadata`
      object and not at the Pod template level.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-namespace
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed."
      pattern:
        metadata:
          namespace: "!default"
  - name: validate-podcontroller-namespace
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - DaemonSet
          - Deployment
          - Job
          - StatefulSet
    validate:
      message: "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed for pod controllers."
      pattern:
        metadata:
          namespace: "!default"

input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: inject-sidecar
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Inject Sidecar Container
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Deployment,Volume
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The sidecar pattern is very common in Kubernetes whereby other applications can
      insert components via tacit modification of a submitted resource. This is, for example,
      often how service meshes and secrets applications are able to function transparently.
      This policy injects a sidecar container, initContainer, and volume into Pods that match
      an annotation called `vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject: true`.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: inject-sidecar
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Deployment
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        spec:
          template:
            metadata:
              annotations:
                (vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject): "true"
            spec:
              containers:

input_snippet:
# This is an example rule intended to be cloned & modified to meet organizational requirements.
# The `dataprotetion` label value can be changed to correspond with specific policy templates.
# 
# NOTE: Use of this policy will require granting the Kyverno background-controller additional privileges required to generate Kasten resources. An example ClusterRole to provide required privileges is provided within the comments of the policy manifest.
#
# apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
# kind: ClusterRole
# metadata:
#   labels:
#     app.kubernetes.io/component: background-controller
#     app.kubernetes.io/instance: kyverno
#     app.kubernetes.io/part-of: kyverno
#   name: kyverno:create-kasten-policies
# rules:
# - apiGroups:
#   - config.kio.kasten.io
#   resources:
#   - policies
#   verbs:
#   - create
#   - update
#   - delete
#
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Generate Kasten Backup Policy Based on Resource Label
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Veeam Kasten
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.12.1
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.12.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.24-1.30"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Policy
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Generates a Kasten policy for a namespace that includes any Deployment or StatefulSet with a "dataprotection=kasten-example" label, if the policy does not already exist. This Kyverno policy can be used in combination with the "kasten-data-protection-by-label" policy to require "dataprotection" labeling on workloads.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
            - Deployment
            - StatefulSet
          selector:
            matchLabels:
              dataprotection: kasten-example
    context:
    - name: dataprotectionLabelValue
      variable:
        value: "kasten-example"
    - name: kyvernoPolicyName
      variable:
        value: "kasten-generate-example-backup-policy"
    - name: existingPolicy
      apiCall:
        urlPath: "/apis/config.kio.kasten.io/v1alpha1/namespaces/kasten-io/policies" # returns list of Kasten policies from kasten-io namespace
        jmesPath: "items[][[@.metadata.labels.\"generate.kyverno.io/policy-name\"=='{{ kyvernoPolicyName }}'] && [@.spec.selector.matchExpressions[].values[?@=='{{ request.namespace }}']]][][][][] | length(@)" # queries if a Kasten policy protecting the namespace generated by this Kyverno policy already exists 
    preconditions:
      any:
      - key: "{{ existingPolicy }}"
        operator: Equals
        value: 0 # Only generate the policy if it does not already exist
    generate:
      apiVersion: config.kio.kasten.io/v1alpha1
      kind: Policy
      name: "{{ request.namespace }}-{{ dataprotectionLabelValue }}-policy"
      namespace: kasten-io
      data:   
        metadata: 
          name: "{{ request.namespace }}-{{ dataprotectionLabelValue }}-policy"
          namespace: kasten-io
        spec:
          comment: "Auto-generated by Kyverno"
          frequency: '@daily'
          retention:
            daily: 7
            weekly: 4
            monthly: 12
            yearly: 7
          actions:
          - action: backup
          - action: export
            exportParameters:
              frequency: '@daily'
              profile:
                name: test
                namespace: kasten-io
              exportData:
                enabled: true
          selector:
            matchExpressions:
              - key: k10.kasten.io/appNamespace
                operator: In
                values:
                  - "{{ request.namespace }}"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: cli.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: Test
metadata:
  name: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy-test
policies:
  - policy.yaml
resources:

results:
  # Pass: Deployment with dataprotection=kasten-example triggers generate rule
  - policy: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    rule: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    resource: app-with-backup-label
    kind: Deployment
    namespace: prod
    generatedResource: generated-kasten-policy-prod.yaml
    result: pass

  # Pass: StatefulSet with dataprotection=kasten-example triggers generate rule
  - policy: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    rule: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    resource: statefulset-with-backup-label
    kind: StatefulSet
    namespace: staging
    generatedResource: generated-kasten-policy-staging.yaml
    result: pass

  # Skip: Deployment without the required dataprotection label — rule does not match
  - policy: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    rule: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy
    resource: app-without-backup-label
    kind: Deployment
    namespace: prod
    result: skip

  # Skip: Deployment with wrong dataprotection label value — rule does not match

    resource: app-with-wrong-label
    kind: Deployment
    namespace: prod
    result: skip
raw_log:
Both files are in place. Here's a summary of what was created:

**Files written to `/workspace/output/`:**

- **`resources.yaml`** — 4 test resources in one multi-document YAML:
  - `app-with-backup-label` (Deployment, ns `prod`, `dataprotection: kasten-example`) → expects rule to fire
  - `statefulset-with-backup-label` (StatefulSet, ns `staging`, `dataprotection: kasten-example`) → expects rule to fire
  - `app-without-backup-label` (Deployment, ns `prod`, no label) → no match, skip
  - `app-with-wrong-label` (Deployment, ns `prod`, `dataprotection: custom-backup`) → no match, skip

- **`kyverno-test.yaml`** — `apiVersion: cli.kyverno.io/v1alpha1 / kind: Test` with `policies: [policy.yaml]`, 4 result entries:
  - 2 `pass` entries with `generatedResource:` pointing to the expected Kasten `Policy` objects (one per namespace)
  - 2 `skip` entries for resources that don't have the matching selector label
  - Each entry has `rule: kasten-generate-example-backup-policy` (the exact rule name from the ClusterPolicy)

Two companion files (`generated-kasten-policy-prod.yaml`, `generated-kasten-policy-staging.yaml`) hold the expected generated Kasten `Policy` manifests that the CLI will diff against actual generate output. The generated resource names follow the policy's template: `{{ request.namespace }}-kasten-example-policy`.

input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: drop-all-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Drop All Capabilities
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Capabilities permit privileged actions without giving full root access. All
      capabilities should be dropped from a Pod, with only those required added back.
      This policy ensures that all containers explicitly specify the `drop: ["ALL"]`
      ability. Note that this policy also illustrates how to cover drop entries in any
      case although this may not strictly conform to the Pod Security Standards.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
    - name: require-drop-all
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{ request.operation || 'BACKGROUND' }}"
          operator: NotEquals
          value: DELETE
      validate:
        message: >-
          Containers must drop `ALL` capabilities.
        foreach:
          - list: request.object.spec.[ephemeralContainers, initContainers, containers][]
            deny:
              conditions:
                all:
                - key: ALL
                  operator: AnyNotIn
                  value: "{{ element.securityContext.capabilities.drop[].to_upper(@) || `[]` }}"
output_yaml:

kind: Test
metadata:
  name: drop-all-capabilities-test
policies:
  - policy.yaml
resources:
  - resources.yaml
results:
  - policy: drop-all-capabilities
    rule: require-drop-all
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-drop-all-pass
    result: pass

  - policy: drop-all-capabilities
    rule: require-drop-all
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-no-secctx-fail
    result: fail

  - policy: drop-all-capabilities
    rule: require-drop-all
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-drop-net-admin-fail
    result: fail

  - policy: drop-all-capabilities
    rule: require-drop-all
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-multi-container-pass
    result: pass

  - policy: drop-all-capabilities
    rule: require-drop-all
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-partial-drop-fail
    result: fail

  - policy: drop-all-capabilities
    rule: require-drop-all
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-drop-all-lowercase-pass
    result: pass
raw_log:

| `pod-drop-all-pass` | Single container, `drop: [ALL]` | pass |
| `pod-no-secctx-fail` | Container with no `securityContext` at all | fail |
| `pod-drop-net-admin-fail` | Container drops `NET_ADMIN` but not `ALL` | fail |
| `pod-multi-container-pass` | Init + 2 containers, all drop `ALL` | pass |
| `pod-partial-drop-fail` | One container drops `ALL`, one drops only `NET_ADMIN` | fail |
| `pod-drop-all-lowercase-pass` | Container drops `all` (lowercase) — policy uses `to_upper()` so this passes | pass |

**`/workspace/output/kyverno-test.yaml`** — `apiVersion: cli.kyverno.io/v1alpha1`, `kind: Test`, references `policy.yaml` and `resources.yaml`. input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Labels
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod, Label
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Define and use labels that identify semantic attributes of your application or Deployment.
      A common set of labels allows tools to work collaboratively, describing objects in a common manner that
      all tools can understand. The recommended labels describe applications in a way that can be
      queried. This policy validates that the label `app.kubernetes.io/name` is specified with some value.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: check-for-labels
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "The label `app.kubernetes.io/name` is required."
      pattern:
        metadata:
          labels:
            app.kubernetes.io/name: "?*"
output_yaml:

kind: Test
metadata:

policies:
  - policy.yaml
resources:

results:

    rule: check-for-labels
    kind: Pod
    resources:
      - pod-with-label
    result: pass
  - policy: require-labels

    resources:

    result: pass
  - policy: require-labels

    result: fail
  - policy: require-labels

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: block-ephemeral-containers
spec:
  validationActions: 
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups:   [""]
      apiVersions: ["v1"]
      operations:  ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
      resources: ["pods", "pods/ephemeralcontainers"]

description: pods which don't specify at least resource requests are assigned a QoS class of BestEffort which can hog resources for other Pods on Nodes
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_add_default_resources.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy pods which don't specify at least resource requests are assigned a QoS class of BestEffort which can hog resources for other Pods on Nodes. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/nctl/cp_add_default_resources.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion : kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-resources
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Default Resources
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.10.0-alpha.2
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.7.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Pods which don't specify at least resource requests are assigned a QoS class
      of BestEffort which can hog resources for other Pods on Nodes. At a minimum,
      all Pods should specify resource requests in order to be labeled as the QoS
      class Burstable. This sample mutates any container in a Pod which doesn't
      specify memory or cpu requests to apply some sane defaults.
spec:
  background: false
  rules:
  - name: add-default-requests
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      any:
      - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
        operator: AnyIn
        value:
        - CREATE
        - UPDATE
    mutate:
      foreach:
      - list: "request.object.spec.[ephemeralContainers, initContainers, containers][]"
        patchStrategicMerge:
          spec:
            containers:
            - (name): "{{element.name}}"
              resources:
                requests:
                  +(memory): "100Mi"
                  +(cpu): "100m"

description: the ndots value controls where DNS lookups are first performed in a cluster and needs to be set to a lower value than the default of 5 in some cases
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_add_ndots.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy the ndots value controls where DNS lookups are first performed in a cluster and needs to be set to a lower value than the default of 5 in some cases. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/nctl/cp_add_ndots.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-ndots
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add ndots
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The ndots value controls where DNS lookups are first performed in a cluster
      and needs to be set to a lower value than the default of 5 in some cases.
      This policy mutates all Pods to add the ndots option with a value of 1.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: add-ndots
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        spec:
          dnsConfig:
            options:
              - name: ndots
                value: "1"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/description: The ndots value controls where DNS lookups are first performed in a cluster and needs to be set to a lower value than the default of 5 in some cases. This policy mutates all Pods to add the ndots option with a value of 1.
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add ndots
  name: add-ndots
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  mutations:
    - jsonPatch:
        expression: |
          has(object.spec.dnsConfig) && has(object.spec.dnsConfig.options) && object.spec.dnsConfig.options.exists(opt, opt.name == "ndots") ?
          [JSONPatch{
            op: "remove",
            path: "/spec/dnsConfig/options/" + string(object.spec.dnsConfig.options.map(opt, opt.name).indexOf("ndots"))
          }] : []
      patchType: JSONPatch
    - jsonPatch:
        expression: |
          has(object.spec.dnsConfig) && has(object.spec.dnsConfig.options) ?
          [JSONPatch{
            op: "add",
            path: "/spec/dnsConfig/options/-",
            value: {"name": "ndots", "value": "1"}
          }] :
          has(object.spec.dnsConfig) ?
          [JSONPatch{
            op: "add",
            path: "/spec/dnsConfig/options",
            value: [{"name": "ndots", "value": "1"}]
          }] :
          [JSONPatch{
            op: "add",
            path: "/spec/dnsConfig",
            value: {"options": [{"name": "ndots", "value": "1"}]}
          }]
      patchType: JSONPatch

input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-ns-quota
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Quota
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: ResourceQuota, LimitRange
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      To better control the number of resources that can be created in a given
      Namespace and provide default resource consumption limits for Pods,
      ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources are recommended.
      This policy will generate ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources when
      a new Namespace is created.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: generate-resourcequota
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Namespace
    generate:
      apiVersion: v1
      kind: ResourceQuota
      name: default-resourcequota
      synchronize: true
      namespace: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}"
      data:
        spec:
          hard:
            requests.cpu: '4'
            requests.memory: '16Gi'
            limits.cpu: '4'
            limits.memory: '16Gi'
  - name: generate-limitrange
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Namespace
    generate:
      apiVersion: v1
      kind: LimitRange
      name: default-limitrange
      synchronize: true
      namespace: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}"
      data:
        spec:
          limits:
          - default:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 1Gi
            defaultRequest:
              cpu: 200m
              memory: 256Mi
            type: Container

description: the Kubernetes cluster autoscaler does not evict pods that  use hostPath or emptyDir volumes
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_add_safe_to_evict.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy the Kubernetes cluster autoscaler does not evict pods that  use hostPath or emptyDir volumes. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/nctl/cp_add_safe_to_evict.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-safe-to-evict
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Safe To Evict
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod,Annotation
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The Kubernetes cluster autoscaler does not evict pods that 
      use hostPath or emptyDir volumes. To allow eviction of these pods, the annotation 
      cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict=true must be added to the pods. 
spec: 
  rules: 
  - name: annotate-empty-dir
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        metadata:
          annotations:
            +(cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict): "true"
        spec:          
          volumes: 
          - <(emptyDir): {}
  - name: annotate-host-path
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        metadata:
          annotations:
            +(cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict): "true"
        spec:          
          volumes: 
          - hostPath:
              <(path): "*"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/description: The Kubernetes cluster autoscaler does not evict pods that use hostPath or emptyDir volumes. To allow eviction of these pods, the annotation cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict=true must be added to the pods.
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod,Annotation
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Safe To Evict
  name: add-safe-to-evict
spec:
  matchConditions:
    - expression: has(object.spec.volumes) && object.spec.volumes.exists(v, has(v.emptyDir) || has(v.hostPath))
      name: has-evictable-volume
    - expression: "!object.metadata.?annotations[?'cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict'].hasValue()"
      name: annotation-absent
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  mutations:
    - applyConfiguration:
        expression: |-
          Object{
            metadata: Object.metadata{
              annotations: {"cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict": "true"}
            }
          }
      patchType: ApplyConfiguration

description: a Pod securityContext entry defines fields such as the user and group which should be used to run the Pod
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_add_securitycontext.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy a Pod securityContext entry defines fields such as the user and group which should be used to run the Pod. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/nctl/cp_add_securitycontext.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-securitycontext
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Default securityContext
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A Pod securityContext entry defines fields such as the user and group which should be used to run the Pod.
      Sometimes choosing default values for users rather than blocking is a better alternative to not impede
      such Pod definitions. This policy will mutate a Pod to set `runAsNonRoot`, `runAsUser`, `runAsGroup`, and 
      `fsGroup` fields within the Pod securityContext if they are not already set.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: add-default-securitycontext
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        spec:
          securityContext:
            +(runAsNonRoot): true
            +(runAsUser): 1000
            +(runAsGroup): 3000
            +(fsGroup): 2000
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/description: A Pod securityContext entry defines fields such as the user and group which should be used to run the Pod. Sometimes choosing default values for users rather than blocking is a better alternative to not impede such Pod definitions. This policy will mutate a Pod to set `runAsNonRoot`, `runAsUser`, `runAsGroup`, and `fsGroup` fields within the Pod securityContext if they are not already set.
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Default securityContext
  name: add-default-securitycontext
spec:
  matchConditions:
    - expression: '!has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsUser) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsGroup) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.fsGroup)'

          - pods
  mutations:
    - applyConfiguration:
        expression: |-
          Object{

description: pod tolerations are used to schedule on Nodes which have a matching taint
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_add_tolerations.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy pod tolerations are used to schedule on Nodes which have a matching taint. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/nctl/cp_add_tolerations.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-tolerations
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Tolerations
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.7.1
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >- 
      Pod tolerations are used to schedule on Nodes which have
      a matching taint. This policy adds the toleration `org.com/role=service:NoSchedule`
      if existing tolerations do not contain the key `org.com/role`.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: service-toleration
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      any:
      - key: "org.com/role"
        operator: AnyNotIn
        value: "{{ request.object.spec.tolerations[].key || `[]` }}"
    mutate:
      patchesJson6902: |-
        - op: add
          path: "/spec/tolerations/-"
          value:
            key: org.com/role
            operator: Equal
            value: service
            effect: NoSchedule
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/description: Pod tolerations are used to schedule on Nodes which have a matching taint. This policy adds the toleration `org.com/role=service:NoSchedule` if existing tolerations do not contain the key `org.com/role`.
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Tolerations
  name: add-tolerations
spec:
  matchConditions:
    - expression: '!has(object.spec.tolerations) || !object.spec.tolerations.exists(t, t.key == "org.com/role")'
      name: missing-org-role-toleration
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  mutations:
    - jsonPatch:
        expression: |-
          (!has(object.spec.tolerations) || object.spec.tolerations == null) ?
            [
              JSONPatch{
                op: "add",
                path: "/spec/tolerations",
                value: [dyn({
                  "key": "org.com/role",
                  "operator": "Equal",
                  "value": "service",
                  "effect": "NoSchedule"
                })]
              }
            ] :
            [
              JSONPatch{
                op: "add",
                path: "/spec/tolerations/-",
                value: dyn({
                  "key": "org.com/role",
                  "operator": "Equal",
                  "value": "service",
                  "effect": "NoSchedule"
                })
              }
            ]
      patchType: JSONPatch

description: by default, images that have already been pulled can be accessed by other Pods without re-pulling them if the name and tag are known
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_always_pull_images.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy by default, images that have already been pulled can be accessed by other Pods without re-pulling them if the name and tag are known. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/nctl/cp_always_pull_images.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: always-pull-images
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Always Pull Images
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      By default, images that have already been pulled can be accessed by other
      Pods without re-pulling them if the name and tag are known. In multi-tenant scenarios,
      this may be undesirable. This policy mutates all incoming Pods to set their
      imagePullPolicy to Always. An alternative to the Kubernetes admission controller
      AlwaysPullImages.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: always-pull-images
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        spec:
          containers:
          - (name): "?*"
            imagePullPolicy: Always
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/description: By default, images that have already been pulled can be accessed by other Pods without re-pulling them if the name and tag are known. In multi-tenant scenarios, this may be undesirable. This policy mutates all incoming Pods to set their imagePullPolicy to Always. An alternative to the Kubernetes admission controller AlwaysPullImages.
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Always Pull Images
  name: always-pull-images
spec:
  matchConditions:
    - expression: object.spec.containers.exists(c, c.name != "" && c.?imagePullPolicy.orValue("") != "Always")
      name: has-named-containers-without-always-pull
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  mutations:
    - applyConfiguration:
        expression: |-
          Object{
            spec: Object.spec{
              containers: object.spec.containers.filter(c, c.name != "").map(c,
                Object.spec.containers{
                  name: c.name,
                  imagePullPolicy: "Always"
                }
              )
            }
          }
      patchType: ApplyConfiguration

description: images that are old usually have some open security vulnerabilities which are not patched
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_block_stale_images.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy images that are old usually have some open security vulnerabilities which are not patched. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/nctl/cp_block_stale_images.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: block-stale-images
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Block Stale Images
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images that are old usually have some open security vulnerabilities which are not patched.
      This policy checks the contents of every container image and inspects them for the create time.
      If it finds any image which was built more than 6 months ago this policy blocks the deployment.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit 
  rules:
    - name: block-stale-images
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Images built more than 6 months ago are prohibited."
        foreach:
        - list: "request.object.spec.containers"
          context:
          - name: imageData
            imageRegistry:
              reference: "{{ element.image }}"
          deny:
            conditions:
              all:
                - key: "{{ time_since('', '{{ imageData.configData.created }}', '') }}"
                  operator: GreaterThan
                  value: 4380h
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/description: Images that are old usually have some open security vulnerabilities which are not patched. This policy checks the contents of every container image and inspects them for the create time. If it finds any image which was built more than 6 months ago this policy blocks the deployment.
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Block Stale Images
  name: block-stale-images
spec:
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  validations:
    - expression: variables.allContainers.all(c, timestamp(image.GetMetadata(c.image).config.created) > time.now() - duration("4380h"))
      message: Images built more than 6 months ago are prohibited. Please use a more recent image.
  variables:
    - expression: object.spec.containers + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([]) + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
      name: allContainers

description: containers which request use of an NVIDIA GPU often need to be authored to consume them via a CUDA environment variable called NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_check_nvidia_gpu.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy containers which request use of an NVIDIA GPU often need to be authored to consume them via a CUDA environment variable called NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/nctl/cp_check_nvidia_gpu.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: check-nvidia-gpus
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Check NVIDIA GPUs
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Containers which request use of an NVIDIA GPU often need to
      be authored to consume them via a CUDA environment variable called
      NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES. This policy checks the containers which
      request a GPU to ensure they have been authored with this environment
      variable.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  rules:
  - name: check-nvidia-gpus
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      all:
      - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
        operator: NotEquals
        value: DELETE
    validate:
      message: "Images which reserve NVIDIA GPUs must be built to use them."
      foreach:
      - list: "request.object.spec.containers"
        context: 
        - name: imageData
          imageRegistry: 
            reference: "{{ element.image }}"
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
              # If a container image calls for an NVIDIA GPU in its resources.limits, it must also
              # have been built with the CUDA environment variable `NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`.
              - key: "NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=*?"
                operator: AnyNotIn
                value: "{{ imageData.configData.config.Env || '' }}"
              - key: "{{ element.resources.limits.\"nvidia.com/gpu\" || '' }}"
                operator: GreaterThan
                value: 0
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/description: Containers which request use of an NVIDIA GPU often need to be authored to consume them via a CUDA environment variable called NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES. This policy checks the containers which request a GPU to ensure they have been authored with this environment variable.
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.17.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Check NVIDIA GPUs
  name: check-nvidia-gpus
spec:
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConditions:
    - expression: "request.operation != 'DELETE'"
      name: not-delete
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  validations:
    - expression: "variables.allContainers.all(c, !(\n  has(c.resources) &&\n  has(c.resources.limits) &&\n  'nvidia.com/gpu' in c.resources.limits &&\n  int(c.resources.limits['nvidia.com/gpu']) > 0\n) || (\n  has(c.env) &&\n  c.env.exists(e, e.name == 'NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES')\n))"
      message: Images which reserve NVIDIA GPUs must be built to use them via the NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES environment variable.
  variables:
    - expression: object.spec.containers + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([]) + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
      name: allContainers

description: a PodDisruptionBudget limits the number of Pods of a replicated application that are down simultaneously from voluntary disruptions
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_create_default_pdb.yaml to a Kyverno 1.17+ GeneratingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy a PodDisruptionBudget limits the number of Pods of a replicated application that are down simultaneously from voluntary disruptions. Write the converted policy to /Users/megha/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/nctl/cp_create_default_pdb.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.17+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: create-default-pdb
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Pod Disruption Budget
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.2
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Deployment
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A PodDisruptionBudget limits the number of Pods of a replicated application that
      are down simultaneously from voluntary disruptions. For example, a quorum-based
      application would like to ensure that the number of replicas running is never brought
      below the number needed for a quorum. As an application owner, you can create a PodDisruptionBudget (PDB)
      for each application. This policy will create a PDB resource whenever a new Deployment is created.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: create-default-pdb
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Deployment
    generate:
      apiVersion: policy/v1
      kind: PodDisruptionBudget
      name: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}-default-pdb"
      namespace: "{{request.object.metadata.namespace}}"
      data:
        spec:
          minAvailable: 1
          selector: "{{request.object.spec.selector}}"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: GeneratingPolicy
metadata:
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/description: A PodDisruptionBudget limits the number of Pods of a replicated application that are down simultaneously from voluntary disruptions. For example, a quorum-based application would like to ensure that the number of replicas running is never brought below the number needed for a quorum. As an application owner, you can create a PodDisruptionBudget (PDB) for each application. This policy will create a PDB resource whenever a new Deployment is created.
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Deployment
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Pod Disruption Budget
  name: create-default-pdb
spec:
  generate:
    - expression: generator.Apply(variables.deployNamespace, variables.downstream)
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - apps
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
        resources:
          - deployments
  variables:
    - expression: object.metadata.name
      name: deployName
    - expression: object.metadata.namespace
      name: deployNamespace
